A.definition: A method of comparing two versions of a webpage, app, or feature to determine which performs better. Users are randomly split between variants, and statistical analysis determines the winner based on predefined metrics.

A numeronym abbreviation for accessibility, where 11 represents the number of letters between 'a' and 'y'. It refers to the practice of making websites and applications usable by people with disabilities.

Attribute-Based Access Control — an authorization model that evaluates attributes of users, resources, actions, and the environment to determine access permissions. It provides finer-grained control than role-based approaches.

A creational design pattern that provides an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes. It encapsulates a group of individual factories with a common theme.

A tree representation of the syntactic structure of source code, where each node denotes a construct in the code. ASTs are used by compilers and interpreters to analyze and transform code during compilation.

The process of hiding complex implementation details and exposing only essential features. Abstraction reduces complexity, improves maintainability, and allows developers to work at higher conceptual levels.
A formal testing phase conducted to determine whether a system satisfies its acceptance criteria and to enable the customer to decide whether to accept the system. It validates business requirements.

The selective restriction of access to resources, data, or places. Access control mechanisms determine who can view or use resources in a computing environment through authentication and authorization.

A list of permissions attached to an object that specifies which users or system processes can access it and what operations they can perform. ACLs are fundamental to network security and file system permissions.

The practice of designing products, devices, and environments that can be used by people with disabilities. In web development, this means following WCAG guidelines to ensure content is perceivable, operable, and understandable by everyone.
A register or variable that stores intermediate results of arithmetic and logic operations. In functional programming, accumulators are used in tail-recursive functions to build up results.
A set of properties — Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — that guarantee database transactions are processed reliably even in the event of errors, power failures, or crashes.
Access Control List — a table that tells an operating system or network device which access rights each user or system process has to a particular object such as a file or network resource.

The process of applying an activation function to the output of a neuron in a neural network. Activation introduces non-linearity, enabling the network to learn complex patterns beyond simple linear relationships.
A mathematical function applied to the output of each neuron in a neural network that determines whether the neuron should be activated. Common functions include ReLU, sigmoid, and tanh.

Microsoft's directory service for Windows domain networks that provides authentication and authorization services. It stores information about network resources and allows administrators to manage permissions and access.

A machine learning approach where the algorithm can interactively query a user or information source to label new data points. This is particularly useful when labeled data is scarce or expensive to obtain.

An open, decentralized social networking protocol that enables different platforms to communicate with each other. It powers the fediverse, including Mastodon, allowing users on different servers to interact.

A mathematical model of concurrent computation where actors are the universal primitives. Each actor can make local decisions, create more actors, send messages, and determine how to respond to received messages.
Technology used for managing and delivering digital advertising across channels. It encompasses demand-side platforms, supply-side platforms, ad exchanges, and data management platforms.
A structural design pattern that allows objects with incompatible interfaces to collaborate. The adapter wraps one of the objects to hide the complexity of conversion happening behind the scenes.
A technique used in streaming multimedia over HTTP that adjusts the quality of a video or audio stream in real time according to the viewer's network bandwidth and device capabilities.

A computer security technique that randomly arranges the address space positions of key data areas of a process. ASLR makes it more difficult for attackers to predict target addresses for exploitation.
A square matrix used to represent a finite graph where elements indicate whether pairs of vertices are adjacent. It is widely used in graph algorithms and network analysis.
The study of attacks on machine learning models and defenses against them. Adversarial examples are inputs deliberately designed to cause a model to make mistakes.
Software that automatically displays or downloads advertising material when a user is online, often bundled with free software. Adware can track browsing habits and compromise user privacy.

Advanced Encryption Standard — a symmetric block cipher algorithm adopted by the U.S. government for encrypting classified information. AES supports key sizes of 128, 192, and 256 bits and is widely used worldwide.
A mathematical mapping that preserves points, straight lines, and planes. In computer graphics and machine learning, affine transformations include translation, scaling, rotation, and shearing.
A clustering algorithm that identifies exemplars among data points and forms clusters around them. Unlike k-means, it does not require specifying the number of clusters in advance.
An autonomous AI system that can perceive its environment, make decisions, and take actions to achieve goals. AI agents can use tools, plan multi-step tasks, and interact with external systems.
A computational modeling approach that simulates the actions and interactions of autonomous agents to assess their effects on a system. It is used in economics, biology, and social sciences.
A function that performs a calculation on a set of values and returns a single value, such as SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, or MAX. Aggregate functions are essential in SQL queries and data analysis.
The field of research focused on ensuring that artificial general intelligence systems behave safely and align with human values when they reach or exceed human-level capabilities.

A project management methodology that emphasizes iterative development, collaboration, and flexibility. Agile teams work in short cycles called sprints, continuously delivering working software and adapting to changing requirements.

A declaration of four core values and twelve principles for agile software development published in 2001. It emphasizes individuals and interactions, working software, customer collaboration, and responding to change.

The research field focused on ensuring that artificial intelligence systems pursue goals that are beneficial to humans. It addresses the challenge of specifying human values in a way machines can understand.

The use of artificial intelligence tools to assist in writing, reviewing, and debugging code. AI coding assistants leverage large language models to generate code suggestions, complete functions, and explain code.

The branch of ethics that examines the moral implications of artificial intelligence development and deployment. It covers issues like bias, privacy, accountability, transparency, and the impact of AI on society.

Specialized computing hardware designed to accelerate artificial intelligence workloads, including GPUs, TPUs, and custom AI accelerator chips. These processors are optimized for the matrix operations common in neural networks.

The research field focused on ensuring that AI systems operate safely and do not cause unintended harm. It encompasses alignment, robustness, interpretability, and the prevention of catastrophic risks from advanced AI.

Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning — a combined term referring to the broad field of building systems that can learn from data, recognize patterns, make decisions, and perform tasks that typically require human intelligence.

A security measure where a computer or network is physically isolated from unsecured networks including the public internet. Air-gapped systems are used for highly sensitive data and critical infrastructure.

Asynchronous JavaScript and XML — a set of web development techniques that allow web applications to send and receive data asynchronously without interfering with the display of the existing page. Ajax enables dynamic, responsive user interfaces.

A technique where the client repeatedly sends HTTP requests to the server at regular intervals to check for new data. It is a simple but less efficient alternative to WebSockets for real-time updates.
A condition where an operator becomes desensitized to alerts due to receiving too many notifications. This can lead to missed critical alerts and slower incident response times.

The process of sending notifications when monitoring systems detect anomalies, threshold breaches, or incidents. Effective alerting ensures teams are informed of issues requiring attention while avoiding alert fatigue.

A step-by-step procedure or formula for solving a problem or accomplishing a task. Algorithms form the foundation of all computer programs and range from simple sorting routines to complex machine learning models.
A measure of the amount of time and space resources required by an algorithm as a function of input size. It is typically expressed using Big O notation and guides optimization decisions.
Systematic and repeatable errors in a computer system that create unfair outcomes such as privileging one group over another. It can arise from biased training data or flawed algorithm design.

The use of computer algorithms to automatically execute trading orders based on predetermined criteria such as timing, price, and volume. It is widely used in financial markets for high-frequency trading.
A situation where the same memory location can be accessed using different names or references. Aliasing can cause unexpected behavior in programs and is a key consideration in compiler optimization.
The process of reserving memory or resources for use by a program. Memory allocation can be static, done at compile time, or dynamic, done at runtime using the heap.
An early phase of software testing performed internally by the development team before releasing to external testers. It helps identify bugs and usability issues before beta testing begins.
A monitoring and observability service from AWS that collects and tracks metrics, logs, and events from AWS resources and applications. It provides dashboards and alarms for operational health.
A fully managed NoSQL key-value and document database service by AWS that delivers single-digit millisecond performance at any scale. It supports both document and key-value data models.
Amazon Elastic Container Service — a fully managed container orchestration service that makes it easy to deploy, manage, and scale containerized applications on AWS.
A fully managed cloud data warehouse service by AWS that allows running complex analytic queries against petabytes of structured data using SQL and existing business intelligence tools.

Amazon Simple Storage Service — an object storage service offering industry-leading scalability, data availability, security, and performance. It is one of the most widely used cloud storage services.
Amazon Simple Queue Service — a fully managed message queuing service that enables decoupling and scaling of microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications.
A computing paradigm where technology is embedded in the environment and responds to human presence and needs without explicit interaction. Smart homes and IoT devices exemplify this concept.
An electronic environment that is sensitive and responsive to the presence of people, using sensors and AI to adapt spaces to individuals' needs without requiring explicit commands.
Advanced Micro Devices — a semiconductor company that designs and manufactures processors, graphics cards, and related technologies. AMD competes with Intel in CPUs and Nvidia in GPUs.

A method for analyzing algorithm complexity that averages the time required to perform a sequence of operations over all operations performed. It provides a more accurate picture of average-case performance.
Accelerated Mobile Pages — a web component framework and website publishing technology developed by Google that optimizes web pages for fast loading on mobile devices.
SQL window functions that compute aggregate values based on a group of rows while retaining individual row details. They include ranking, offset, and distribution functions like ROW_NUMBER and NTILE.
The systematic computational analysis of data to discover patterns, trends, and insights. Analytics encompasses descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analysis techniques.
A mobile operating system developed by Google based on the Linux kernel. Android is the most widely used mobile OS globally and powers smartphones, tablets, wearables, and other devices.

A TypeScript-based open-source web application framework led by Google for building single-page applications. It provides a comprehensive set of tools for routing, forms, HTTP communication, and testing.

The technique of creating the illusion of movement by displaying a sequence of images or frames in rapid succession. In web and UI development, animation enhances user experience through transitions and visual feedback.

The identification of rare items, events, or observations that raise suspicions by differing significantly from the majority of data. It is used in fraud detection, network security, and quality control.
The process of removing or altering personally identifiable information from data so that individuals cannot be identified. It is essential for compliance with privacy regulations like GDPR.
The standardized version of SQL maintained by the American National Standards Institute. It defines the core syntax and functionality that all relational database management systems should support.
An open-source automation tool for configuration management, application deployment, and task automation. Ansible uses human-readable YAML files called playbooks and operates agentlessly over SSH.
A YAML file that defines a set of tasks to be executed on remote hosts by the Ansible automation tool. Playbooks describe desired system states and support variables, loops, and conditionals.
A common response to a recurring problem that is usually ineffective and risks being counterproductive. Recognizing anti-patterns helps developers avoid poor design choices and maintain code quality.
Software designed to detect, prevent, and remove malicious software from computers and networks. Modern antivirus solutions use signature-based detection, behavioral analysis, and machine learning.
Aspect-Oriented Programming — a programming paradigm that aims to increase modularity by allowing the separation of cross-cutting concerns such as logging, security, and transaction management.
An open-source platform to programmatically author, schedule, and monitor workflows. It uses directed acyclic graphs to manage task dependencies and is widely used for ETL pipelines.

A cross-language development platform for in-memory analytics that provides a standardized columnar memory format for flat and hierarchical data. It enables zero-copy reads for lightning-fast data access.
A unified programming model for defining both batch and streaming data-parallel processing pipelines. It provides SDKs for building pipelines and runners for executing them on various engines.

A free and open-source distributed NoSQL database management system designed to handle large amounts of data across many commodity servers. It provides high availability with no single point of failure.
A framework and distributed processing engine for stateful computations over unbounded and bounded data streams. It provides high throughput, low latency, and exactly-once semantics.
An open-source software framework for distributed storage and processing of big data sets using the MapReduce programming model. It enables processing of large data sets across clusters of computers.
A data warehouse software built on top of Apache Hadoop for querying and managing large datasets in distributed storage. It provides an SQL-like interface called HiveQL for data summarization.
An open table format for huge analytic datasets that provides reliable, performant tables on top of data lakes. Iceberg supports schema evolution, partition evolution, and time travel queries.

A distributed event streaming platform used for building real-time data pipelines and streaming applications. Kafka handles trillions of events per day and is widely used for log aggregation, metrics collection, and event sourcing.

A build automation and project management tool primarily for Java projects that uses XML-based configuration files. Maven manages project builds, reporting, and documentation from a central piece of information.

A data integration tool that provides a web-based interface for designing, controlling, and monitoring data flows between systems. It supports data routing, transformation, and system mediation logic.

A cloud-native distributed messaging and streaming platform that provides multi-tenancy, geo-replication, and strong durability guarantees. It separates serving and storage layers for independent scaling.
A unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing that provides high-level APIs in Java, Scala, Python, and R. Spark is known for its speed, ease of use, and support for complex analytics.
An open-source implementation of the Java Servlet, JavaServer Pages, and WebSocket technologies. It provides a pure Java HTTP web server environment for running Java web applications.
A centralized service for maintaining configuration information, naming, providing distributed synchronization, and providing group services for distributed applications.

Application Programming Interface — a set of rules and protocols that allows different software applications to communicate with each other. APIs enable developers to access functionality or data from external services without knowing their internal implementation.

A server that acts as an API front-end, receiving API requests, enforcing throttling and security policies, passing requests to the back-end service, and then passing the response back. It centralizes API management.
A unique identifier used to authenticate a user, developer, or calling program to an API. API keys help track and control how the API is being used and prevent unauthorized access.
The practice of controlling the number of requests a user can make to an API within a specified time period. It protects services from abuse and ensures fair usage among all consumers.
The practice of managing changes to an API by assigning version identifiers. Versioning strategies include URI path versioning, query parameter versioning, and header-based versioning.

Application Performance Management — a set of tools and practices for monitoring and managing the performance and availability of software applications. APM solutions track response times, error rates, and throughput.
The process of improving the visibility of a mobile application in an app store's search results. ASO involves optimizing titles, keywords, descriptions, and visual assets to increase downloads.
A data structure where new records are only added to the end and existing records are never modified or deleted. It is the foundation of event sourcing, write-ahead logs, and blockchain systems.
A specification that defines how different components of binary code interface with each other at the machine code level, including calling conventions, data type sizes, and system call numbers.

A firewall that monitors, filters, and blocks HTTP traffic to and from a web application. Web application firewalls protect against attacks like SQL injection, cross-site scripting, and session hijacking.
The topmost layer of the OSI model that directly interacts with end-user applications. It provides protocols for email, file transfer, web browsing, and other network services.
A server that hosts applications and provides business logic, transaction management, and security for enterprise applications. It sits between the web server and the database.
An algorithm that finds near-optimal solutions to optimization problems within a guaranteed factor of the optimal solution. These are used when exact solutions are computationally infeasible.

A native multi-model database system that supports key/value, document, and graph data models with one database core and a unified query language. It combines the strengths of different NoSQL approaches.

The high-level structure of a software system including its components, their relationships, and the principles governing its design and evolution. Good architecture enables maintainability, scalability, and reliability.
A document that captures an important architectural decision made along with its context and consequences. ADRs help teams track the reasoning behind technical choices over time.
A reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem in software architecture. Examples include microservices, event-driven architecture, layered architecture, and hexagonal architecture.
A collection of data or files stored for long-term preservation and retrieval. In computing, archives compress multiple files into a single file for efficient storage and transfer.

An open-source electronics platform based on simple hardware and software for building interactive projects. Arduino boards read inputs from sensors and turn them into outputs, making it popular for prototyping and education.
A declarative GitOps continuous delivery tool for Kubernetes that automates the deployment of applications by syncing cluster state with Git repository definitions.

A value passed to a function or method when it is called. Arguments provide input data that the function uses during execution, as opposed to parameters which are the variable names defined in the function signature.
A fundamental building block of a computer's central processing unit that performs arithmetic and bitwise operations on integer binary numbers. The ALU is essential for all computational operations.

Advanced RISC Machines — a family of reduced instruction set computing architectures for processors that are widely used in smartphones, tablets, and increasingly in laptops and servers due to their energy efficiency.

A family of reduced instruction set computing architectures for computer processors widely used in smartphones, tablets, and increasingly in laptops and servers. ARM chips are known for energy efficiency.
A data structure consisting of a collection of elements, each identified by an index or key. Arrays are one of the most fundamental data structures and provide constant-time access to elements.
A fixed-length raw binary data buffer in JavaScript that represents a generic block of memory. It is used with typed arrays to manipulate binary data for tasks like file I/O and network protocols.
A hypothetical type of AI that would have the ability to understand, learn, and apply intelligence across any intellectual task that a human can perform. AGI remains a long-term research goal.

The simulation of human intelligence by computer systems, including learning, reasoning, and self-correction. AI encompasses everything from simple rule-based systems to advanced neural networks capable of generating text, images, and code.

A computing system inspired by biological neural networks that consists of interconnected nodes organized in layers. ANNs learn to perform tasks by considering examples without being explicitly programmed.

American Standard Code for Information Interchange — a character encoding standard that assigns numerical values to 128 characters including letters, digits, and symbols. ASCII forms the basis for most modern character encodings.

Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface — a specification for Python web servers and applications that supports asynchronous request handling. ASGI extends WSGI to handle WebSockets, HTTP/2, and other async protocols.

Abstract Syntax Notation One — a standard interface description language for defining data structures that can be serialized and deserialized in a cross-platform way across different programming languages.
A web application framework developed by Microsoft for building dynamic websites, web services, and web applications. ASP.NET runs on the .NET platform and supports multiple programming languages.
The proportional relationship between the width and height of an image or display screen. Common aspect ratios include 16:9 for widescreen displays and 4:3 for traditional screens.
A low-level programming language that uses mnemonic codes and labels to represent machine-level instructions. Each assembly language is specific to a particular computer architecture.
A statement in code that checks whether a condition is true at a specific point during execution. Assertions are used for debugging and testing to catch logical errors early.
A framework for concatenating, minifying, and compressing JavaScript and CSS assets in web applications. It improves page load times by reducing the number and size of HTTP requests.

A modern web framework for building fast, content-focused websites. Astro renders pages to static HTML by default and only hydrates interactive components when needed, resulting in excellent performance.
A cryptographic system that uses a pair of keys — a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption. It enables secure communication without sharing secret keys.

Short for asynchronous — a programming pattern where operations are initiated and then execution continues without waiting for the result. Async programming prevents blocking and improves responsiveness in I/O-heavy applications.

A syntactic feature in many programming languages that simplifies asynchronous programming by allowing developers to write asynchronous code in a synchronous-looking style using async and await keywords.

A programming pattern where operations can execute independently without blocking the main execution thread. Async programming is essential for handling I/O operations, network requests, and user interactions efficiently.
A programming paradigm that allows operations to execute independently of the main program flow. It enables non-blocking I/O operations, improving application responsiveness and throughput.
A methodology for creating design systems composed of five distinct levels: atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, and pages. It provides a clear hierarchy for building user interface components.
An operation that is completed in a single step relative to other operations, without the possibility of being interrupted. Atomicity is crucial in concurrent programming to prevent race conditions.
The total number of points where an unauthorized user can try to enter or extract data from an environment. Reducing the attack surface is a fundamental security principle.
A component of neural networks that allows the model to focus on specific parts of the input when producing output. It is the foundational concept behind transformer architectures.
An access control paradigm that grants or denies access based on attributes of the user, the resource, and the environment. ABAC provides more granular control than role-based approaches.
Software or hardware that compresses and decompresses digital audio data according to a specific format such as MP3, AAC, or FLAC. Codecs balance audio quality with file size.

The manipulation of audio signals using algorithms for tasks like noise reduction, equalization, compression, and effects. Digital audio processing is fundamental to music production and speech recognition.

A chronological record of system activities that provides documentary evidence of the sequence of activities affecting an operation, procedure, or event. Audit trails are essential for security, compliance, and forensics.
A technology that overlays digital information such as images, text, or sounds onto the real-world environment. AR is used in gaming, education, retail, and industrial applications.

The process of verifying the identity of a user, device, or system before granting access to resources. Common methods include passwords, biometrics, multi-factor authentication, and token-based systems.

The process of determining whether an authenticated user has permission to access specific resources or perform certain actions. Authorization is distinct from authentication, which verifies identity.

A cloud computing feature that automatically adjusts the number of compute resources based on current demand. It helps maintain performance during traffic spikes while minimizing costs during low usage.

A cloud computing feature that automatically adjusts the number of compute resources based on current demand. It maintains performance during traffic spikes while minimizing costs during low-usage periods.

A type of neural network that learns to compress data into a lower-dimensional representation and then reconstruct it. Autoencoders are used for dimensionality reduction, denoising, and generative modeling.

Abstract machines used in theoretical computer science to study computation and formal languages. Types include finite automata, pushdown automata, and Turing machines, each recognizing different language classes.
The use of software tools to run tests on code automatically without manual intervention. It includes unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests that verify software functionality.

The use of technology to perform tasks with reduced human intervention. In software development, automation encompasses build processes, testing, deployment, infrastructure provisioning, and monitoring.
A library for building collaborative applications that implements conflict-free replicated data types. It allows multiple users to edit shared data concurrently without coordination.
Automated Machine Learning — tools and techniques that automate the process of applying machine learning, including feature engineering, model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and deployment.
Systems capable of performing tasks in complex environments without continuous human guidance. Examples include self-driving cars, drones, and robotic systems that make decisions independently.

An isolated location within a cloud data center region that has independent power, cooling, and networking. Distributing resources across availability zones provides fault tolerance and high availability.

A desirable property of cryptographic algorithms where a small change in input produces a significant and unpredictable change in output. Good hash functions and ciphers exhibit strong avalanche effects.
A self-balancing binary search tree where the heights of the two child subtrees of any node differ by at most one. AVL trees guarantee O(log n) search, insertion, and deletion operations.

A row-oriented remote procedure call and data serialization framework developed within Apache's Hadoop project. It uses JSON for defining data types and protocols, and serializes data in a compact binary format.

Amazon Web Services — the world's largest cloud computing platform, offering over 200 services including compute, storage, databases, and machine learning. AWS pioneered the Infrastructure-as-a-Service model.

An Amazon Web Services service that gives developers and businesses an easy way to create a collection of related AWS resources and provision them in an orderly and predictable fashion using infrastructure as code.

An Amazon Web Services service that provides user authentication, authorization, and user management for web and mobile apps. It supports sign-up, sign-in, and access control with social and enterprise identity providers.

A serverless compute service from Amazon that runs code in response to events and automatically manages the underlying compute resources. It supports multiple programming languages and scales automatically.

Microsoft's cloud computing platform that provides a wide range of cloud services including compute, analytics, storage, and networking. Azure supports multiple programming languages, tools, and frameworks.
A Microsoft product providing version control, reporting, requirements management, project management, automated builds, testing, and release management capabilities.

B.definition: A self-balancing tree data structure that maintains sorted data and allows searches, insertions, and deletions in logarithmic time. B-trees are widely used in databases and file systems for indexing.
Business-to-Business — commerce transactions between businesses rather than between a business and individual consumers. B2B companies provide products and services to other businesses.
Business-to-Consumer — commerce transactions where businesses sell products or services directly to individual consumers. E-commerce platforms and retail apps are common B2C channels.

A JavaScript compiler that converts modern JavaScript code into backwards-compatible versions for older browsers. Babel enables developers to use the latest language features without worrying about browser support.
The primary algorithm for training neural networks that calculates the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight by propagating errors backwards through the network.

The server-side portion of a web application that handles business logic, database interactions, and API responses. Backend systems process requests from the frontend and return data, typically built with languages like Python, Node.js, or Go.

A task that runs asynchronously outside the main request-response cycle of a web application. Background jobs handle time-consuming operations like sending emails, processing images, and generating reports.
An ordered list of work items that need to be completed, typically maintained in agile project management. The product backlog represents everything that might be needed in the product.

A mechanism for handling situations where a data producer generates data faster than the consumer can process it. Backpressure strategies include buffering, dropping, and signaling the producer to slow down.
The primary algorithm for training neural networks that calculates the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight by propagating errors backwards through the network layers.
The process of creating copies of data so it can be restored in the event of data loss, corruption, or disaster. Backup strategies include full, incremental, and differential approaches.
The process of creating copies of data and systems to protect against data loss and enabling restoration of those copies in the event of a disaster, hardware failure, or accidental deletion.
The ability of a system or component to work with older versions of itself or with older data formats. Maintaining backward compatibility prevents breaking existing users when releasing updates.
A person or entity responsible for actions that are harmful or malicious in cyberspace. Bad actors include hackers, cybercriminals, nation-state attackers, and insider threats.

Bootstrap Aggregating — an ensemble machine learning method that combines predictions from multiple models trained on random subsets of the training data. It reduces variance and helps prevent overfitting.

The maximum rate of data transfer across a network connection, typically measured in bits per second. Higher bandwidth allows more data to flow simultaneously, affecting download speeds, streaming quality, and application performance.
A machine-readable representation of data using varying widths and spacings of parallel lines or geometric patterns. Barcodes are used for product identification, inventory management, and tracking.
A physical server dedicated to a single tenant without any virtualization layer. Bare metal servers provide maximum performance and control for workloads that require direct hardware access.

A coordination primitive in parallel computing where threads or processes must all reach a designated point before any can proceed past it, ensuring that a phase of computation completes globally before the next begins.
A class from which other classes inherit methods and properties. Also known as a parent class or superclass, it defines common behavior shared by its derived classes.

A binary-to-text encoding scheme that represents binary data in an ASCII string format. It is commonly used for encoding email attachments, embedding images in HTML, and transmitting binary data over text-based protocols.
The Bourne Again Shell — a Unix shell and command language that serves as the default shell on most Linux distributions and macOS. It provides scripting capabilities for automating system tasks.
A special-purpose computer on a network specifically designed and configured to withstand attacks. It serves as a gateway between trusted and untrusted networks, providing controlled access.

A technique used in neural networks that normalizes the inputs of each layer to have zero mean and unit variance. It speeds up training, reduces sensitivity to initialization, and acts as a regularizer.
The execution of a series of programs or jobs on a computer without manual intervention. Batch processing is used for large-scale data transformations, ETL jobs, and report generation.
The number of training examples used in one iteration of model training. Batch size affects training speed, memory usage, and model convergence, with common choices being 32, 64, or 128 samples.
A statistical method that uses Bayes' theorem to update the probability of a hypothesis as more evidence becomes available. It provides a principled framework for reasoning under uncertainty.
A probabilistic graphical model that represents a set of variables and their conditional dependencies via a directed acyclic graph. It is used for reasoning under uncertainty and causal inference.
A sequential design strategy for global optimization of black-box functions that uses a probabilistic surrogate model to guide the search for optimal hyperparameters or configurations.
A password-hashing function based on the Blowfish cipher that incorporates a salt and an adaptive cost factor to resist brute-force attacks. It is widely recommended for securely storing passwords.

Behavior-Driven Development — a software development methodology that extends TDD by writing tests in natural language that describe the expected behavior of the system. It bridges the gap between technical and non-technical stakeholders.
A small Bluetooth Low Energy device that broadcasts signals to nearby smartphones and tablets. Beacons are used for indoor navigation, proximity marketing, and asset tracking.
A type of access token that grants the bearer access to a protected resource. Anyone holding the token can access the resource, making secure token storage and transmission critical.
A software development process that encourages collaboration between developers, QA, and non-technical stakeholders. BDD uses natural language specifications to describe software behavior.
A category of design patterns that deal with communication and interaction between objects. Examples include Observer, Strategy, Command, and Iterator patterns.
A graph algorithm that computes shortest paths from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted directed graph. Unlike Dijkstra's algorithm, it can handle negative edge weights.
A standardized test or set of tests used to measure the performance of hardware or software components. Benchmarking helps compare different systems and identify performance bottlenecks.

Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers — a pre-trained natural language processing model developed by Google. BERT understands context by reading text bidirectionally and excels at tasks like question answering and sentiment analysis.
A method or technique that has been generally accepted as superior to alternatives because it produces better results. In technology, best practices evolve with industry standards and experience.
A phase of software testing where a near-complete version is released to a limited audience outside the development team. Beta testers provide feedback on bugs, usability, and features.

A parametric curve defined by control points that determine its shape, used extensively in vector graphics, font design, CSS animations, and CAD. Cubic Bezier curves with four control points are the most common variant.

Border Gateway Protocol — the routing protocol used to exchange routing information between autonomous systems on the internet. BGP is fundamental to how data finds its path across the global internet.
Systematic error in AI systems that produces unfair outcomes, often reflecting prejudices in training data or algorithm design. Addressing bias is essential for building fair and ethical AI systems.
A fundamental concept in machine learning that describes the tradeoff between a model's ability to fit training data and its ability to generalize to new data. Lower bias often means higher variance.

A neural architecture that processes input sequences in both forward and backward directions simultaneously, capturing context from both sides of each token. BERT's masked language model is the most influential example.

Extremely large datasets that cannot be processed by traditional methods, characterized by volume, velocity, variety, and veracity. Big data technologies enable organizations to extract insights from massive amounts of structured and unstructured information.

A mathematical notation that describes the limiting behavior of a function when the argument tends towards infinity. In computer science, it is used to classify algorithms by their time and space complexity.

A base-2 number system using only 0 and 1, which forms the foundation of all digital computing. Every piece of data in a computer — text, images, video — is ultimately represented as sequences of binary digits (bits).
The examination of compiled binary executables to understand their behavior without access to source code. It is used in malware analysis, vulnerability research, and reverse engineering.
A machine learning task where the model predicts one of two possible classes for each input. Common examples include spam detection, fraud detection, and medical diagnosis.
A complete binary tree data structure where each node satisfies the heap property — the parent node is either greater than or equal to (max-heap) or less than or equal to (min-heap) its children.

An operator that takes two operands and performs an operation on them. Common binary operators include arithmetic operators like + and -, comparison operators like == and <, and logical operators like && and ||.
An efficient search algorithm that finds the position of a target value within a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. It has O(log n) time complexity.

A binary tree data structure where each node has at most two children, with left children containing smaller values and right children containing larger values. BSTs enable efficient searching, insertion, and deletion.
A tree data structure in which each node has at most two children, referred to as the left child and right child. Binary trees form the basis for many advanced data structures and algorithms.
The association of a name with an entity such as a variable, function, or type in a programming language. Early binding occurs at compile time while late binding occurs at runtime.

An interdisciplinary field that develops methods and software tools for understanding biological data. It combines biology, computer science, mathematics, and statistics to analyze genomic and proteomic data.

Authentication methods that use unique physical or behavioral characteristics to verify identity, such as fingerprints, facial recognition, iris scans, or voice patterns. Biometrics offer convenience but raise privacy and security considerations.
Basic Input/Output System — firmware that initializes hardware components during the boot process and provides runtime services for the operating system. BIOS has largely been replaced by UEFI.
The most basic unit of information in computing, representing a single binary value of either 0 or 1. All digital data is ultimately composed of bits, with 8 bits forming a byte.

A compact integer representation where individual bits serve as boolean flags, manipulated via bitwise operations to test, set, or clear specific flags. Widely used for file permissions, feature flags, and capability sets.
A peer-to-peer file sharing protocol that distributes data across multiple nodes rather than relying on a central server. It reduces bandwidth costs and increases download speeds for popular files.
An operation that directly manipulates bits of a binary number. Bitwise operations include AND, OR, XOR, NOT, and shift operations and are used for low-level programming and optimization.
A system or component whose internal workings are hidden from the user, who interacts with it solely through its inputs and outputs. Black box design promotes encapsulation and modular architecture.
A software testing method that examines functionality without knowledge of internal code structure. Testers verify that inputs produce expected outputs without understanding implementation details.
A list of entities such as IP addresses, email addresses, or domains that are denied access or blocked. Blacklisting is used in spam filtering, network security, and content moderation.
A type of cloud storage optimized for storing massive amounts of unstructured data such as text, binary data, images, and documents. Azure Blob Storage and Amazon S3 are popular implementations.
A symmetric encryption algorithm that encrypts data in fixed-size blocks rather than one bit at a time. AES and DES are common block ciphers, with AES using 128-bit blocks.

A decentralized, distributed ledger technology that records transactions across multiple computers. Each block contains a cryptographic hash of the previous block, creating an immutable chain that ensures data integrity without a central authority.
The mechanism by which nodes in a blockchain network agree on the current state of the distributed ledger. Common consensus algorithms include Proof of Work, Proof of Stake, and BFT.

A space-efficient probabilistic data structure used to test whether an element is a member of a set. It may return false positives but never false negatives, making it useful for caching and filtering.

The defensive security team responsible for maintaining an organization's security posture, detecting threats, and responding to incidents. Blue teams work to defend against attacks simulated by red teams.

A release strategy that maintains two identical production environments (blue and green), where one serves live traffic while the other is updated. Traffic is switched instantly to the new version, enabling zero-downtime deployments and easy rollbacks.
A wireless technology standard for exchanging data over short distances using UHF radio waves. Bluetooth connects devices like headphones, keyboards, and IoT sensors without cables.
A wireless personal area network technology designed for low power consumption while maintaining a similar communication range to classic Bluetooth. BLE is widely used in IoT devices and wearables.

A visual collaboration surface used in design tools like FigJam and Miro for brainstorming, wireframing, and planning. Boards support real-time collaboration with sticky notes, shapes, and connectors.

Sections of code that have to be included in many places with little or no alteration. Reducing boilerplate through abstractions, code generation, or language features improves developer productivity.
A data type with only two possible values: true or false. Booleans are fundamental to programming logic, used in conditional statements, loops, and logical operations to control program flow.
A branch of algebra dealing with variables that have two possible values: true and false. Boolean algebra forms the foundation of digital logic circuits and conditional expressions in programming.
A form of algebra in which all values are reduced to either true or false. Boolean logic forms the basis of digital circuit design and conditional expressions in programming.
An ensemble machine learning technique that builds models sequentially, with each new model correcting errors made by the previous ones. Popular boosting algorithms include AdaBoost and XGBoost.

The sequence of operations a computer performs when powered on, from initial hardware checks through loading the operating system. The boot process includes POST, bootloader execution, and kernel initialization.

A popular open-source CSS framework for building responsive, mobile-first websites. Bootstrap provides pre-built components, a flexible grid system, and JavaScript plugins that accelerate web development.

Google's internal cluster management system that predates and inspired Kubernetes, scheduling hundreds of thousands of jobs across massive fleets by bin-packing workloads, enforcing priorities, and providing declarative job specifications.

A software application that runs automated tasks over the internet, typically performing simple and repetitive tasks much faster than a human could. Bots are used for web scraping, chatbots, and automation.

A network of compromised computers controlled remotely by an attacker, typically used to perform coordinated tasks like distributed denial-of-service attacks, spam distribution, and credential stuffing.
A point in a system where performance is limited, causing the overall throughput to be constrained. Identifying and resolving bottlenecks is key to optimizing system performance.

The process of identifying the slowest component or constraint in a system that limits overall throughput. Bottleneck analysis in software examines CPU, memory, I/O, and network to optimize performance.
A software testing technique that focuses on values at the boundaries of input ranges rather than the center. Boundary tests catch off-by-one errors and edge cases that are common sources of bugs.
A central pattern in Domain-Driven Design that defines the boundary within which a particular domain model is defined and applicable. It helps manage complexity in large software systems.
A rectangular border around an object in an image used in computer vision to define the location and extent of the object. Bounding boxes are fundamental to object detection tasks.
The CSS box model describes the rectangular boxes generated for elements in the document tree. It consists of content, padding, border, and margin areas that determine element size and spacing.

The process of converting a value type to a reference type by wrapping it in an object. In languages like C# and Java, boxing allows primitive types to be treated as objects, though it incurs a performance overhead.
Business Process as a Service — a cloud computing model that delivers business process outsourcing through cloud services. It combines cloud infrastructure with business process management.

Berkeley Packet Filter — a virtual machine in the Linux kernel originally designed for packet filtering, now extended as eBPF to safely run sandboxed programs for observability, networking, and security without kernel module changes.
An independent line of development in a version control system. Branches allow developers to work on features or fixes in isolation before merging changes back to the main codebase.

A CPU pipeline optimization where the processor speculatively guesses the outcome of conditional branches and begins executing instructions along the predicted path, achieving near-zero branch penalty when predictions are correct.

A version control practice where developers create independent copies of code to work on features or fixes without affecting the main codebase. Git branching enables parallel development and is central to workflows like Git Flow and trunk-based development.
A convention for creating, using, and merging branches in version control systems. Strategies like Git Flow, GitHub Flow, and trunk-based development define how teams collaborate on code changes.
A graph traversal algorithm that explores all neighbor nodes at the present depth before moving to nodes at the next depth level. BFS is used for finding shortest paths and level-order traversal.

A virtual sub-meeting space within a video conference that allows participants to split into smaller groups for discussions. Breakout rooms are used for workshops, brainstorming, and parallel discussions.
A debugging tool that pauses program execution at a specified line of code. Breakpoints allow developers to inspect variables, evaluate expressions, and step through code line by line.
A structural design pattern that separates an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently. It is useful when both the class and what it does need to vary.
The transmission of data to all devices on a network simultaneously. Broadcasting is used for service discovery, network announcements, and distributing information to multiple recipients.
A data compression algorithm developed by Google that offers better compression ratios than gzip for web content. It is widely supported by modern browsers and reduces page load times.

A software development approach that involves working with existing systems, codebases, or infrastructure. Brownfield projects require dealing with legacy code, technical debt, and existing constraints.

Software development that involves working with existing legacy systems and infrastructure. Brownfield projects must account for existing code, data, and constraints unlike greenfield projects built from scratch.
A software application used to access and display web pages from the internet. Modern browsers like Chrome, Firefox, and Safari support HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and various web APIs.

Application programming interfaces provided by web browsers that allow JavaScript code to interact with browser features and the web platform. Examples include the DOM API, Fetch API, and Web Storage API.

The temporary storage of web resources like HTML pages, images, and scripts on a user's local device by the web browser. Caching reduces server load and improves page load times for returning visitors.

The ability of a website or web application to function correctly across different web browsers and versions. Ensuring compatibility requires testing and sometimes using polyfills or progressive enhancement.
The core component of a web browser that interprets HTML, CSS, and JavaScript to render web pages. Major browser engines include Blink (Chrome), Gecko (Firefox), and WebKit (Safari).
A technique that identifies and tracks users based on the unique configuration of their web browser and device properties such as screen resolution, installed fonts, and plugin versions.

An attack method that tries every possible combination of passwords or encryption keys until the correct one is found. Brute force attacks are computationally expensive but guaranteed to succeed given enough time.
Binary JSON — a binary-encoded serialization format used to store documents and make remote procedure calls in MongoDB. BSON extends JSON with additional data types like Date and BinData.

Binary Space Partitioning tree — a data structure that recursively subdivides space into convex sets using hyperplanes, used in 3D rendering for visibility determination and in computational geometry for point location.

A simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through a list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. It has O(n squared) time complexity and is mainly used for education.

A distribution-based sorting algorithm that distributes elements into a number of buckets, sorts each bucket individually, and then concatenates the results. It achieves O(n) time for uniformly distributed data.
A financial plan that estimates income and expenses over a specified period. In technology, budgets cover infrastructure costs, development resources, licensing, and operational expenses.
An automated notification triggered when cloud spending approaches or exceeds a predefined threshold. Budget alerts help organizations monitor and control their cloud infrastructure costs.
A region of memory used to temporarily store data while it is being moved from one place to another. Buffers help smooth out differences in processing speed between components.
A software vulnerability that occurs when a program writes data beyond the boundaries of a pre-allocated buffer in memory. Buffer overflows can be exploited to execute arbitrary code.

An error, flaw, or unintended behavior in a software program that causes it to produce incorrect results or crash. The term originated from an actual moth found in a Harvard computer in 1947. Debugging is the process of finding and fixing bugs.

A program offered by organizations that rewards individuals for discovering and reporting software vulnerabilities. Bug bounty programs leverage crowdsourced security testing to find bugs before attackers do.
A software application that tracks reported software bugs and their resolution status. Bug trackers help teams prioritize fixes, assign work, and maintain a history of issues and solutions.
The process of logging, tracking, and managing software defects throughout their lifecycle from discovery to resolution. Bug tracking systems help teams prioritize and organize fix efforts.

The process of automating the creation of a software build and associated processes including compiling, packaging, and testing. Build tools like Make, Gradle, and Maven streamline development workflows.
A mechanism that stores the results of build steps so they can be reused in subsequent builds when inputs have not changed. Build caching significantly reduces build times in CI/CD pipelines.
A collection of tools and processes that automate the compilation, linking, and packaging of source code into executable programs or deployable artifacts. Examples include Make, Gradle, and Webpack.
Software that automates the process of compiling source code into binary code, packaging binary code, and running automated tests. Examples include Maven, Gradle, Make, and npm scripts.
A creational design pattern that separates the construction of a complex object from its representation, allowing the same construction process to create different representations.
An architecture pattern that isolates elements of an application into pools so that if one fails, the others continue to function. Named after ship compartments that prevent flooding from spreading.
A design pattern that isolates elements of an application into pools so that if one fails, the others will continue to function. It is named after the watertight compartments in a ship's hull.
A tool that combines multiple JavaScript, CSS, and asset files into optimized bundles for efficient delivery to web browsers. Popular bundlers include Webpack, Rollup, Vite, and esbuild.
A graphical representation of work remaining versus time in an agile sprint. It shows the amount of work left to complete and helps teams track progress toward sprint goals.
The rate at which a company spends its cash reserves or venture capital before generating positive cash flow. In cloud computing, it can refer to the rate of resource consumption.
A computer architecture where all components are connected through a shared communication pathway called a bus. It allows data transfer between the CPU, memory, and peripheral devices.
The minimum number of team members whose sudden loss would cripple a project due to lack of knowledge or capability. A low bus factor indicates high risk from key person dependency.

The organized effort of individuals to produce and sell goods or services for profit. In technology, business encompasses strategy, operations, marketing, finance, and the commercial aspects of software products.

The planning and preparation to ensure that an organization can continue to operate during and after a disaster. It encompasses disaster recovery, crisis management, and business resumption strategies.

Technologies, practices, and strategies for collecting, integrating, and analyzing business data to support better decision-making. BI tools create dashboards, reports, and visualizations that transform raw data into actionable insights.
The part of a software application that encodes the real-world business rules that determine how data can be created, stored, and changed. It represents the core functionality of the application.

A collection of related, structured activities that produce a specific outcome for a particular customer or market. Business process management involves analyzing, modeling, and optimizing these workflows.
The use of technology to execute recurring tasks or processes where manual effort can be replaced. BPA improves efficiency, reduces errors, and frees employees for higher-value work.
A synchronization technique where a process repeatedly checks a condition in a tight loop instead of sleeping or yielding. While simple, busy waiting wastes CPU cycles and is generally avoided.
A unit of digital information consisting of eight bits that can represent 256 different values. Bytes are the fundamental unit of data storage and processing in computing.

A form of instruction set designed for efficient execution by a software interpreter or virtual machine. Java bytecode and Python bytecode are compiled from source code and executed by their respective VMs.
A program that executes bytecode instructions one at a time, translating each bytecode operation into native machine operations. Interpreters trade execution speed for portability and simplicity.
A sequence of bytes transmitted between processes or over a network. TCP provides a reliable bytestream abstraction, while UDP provides datagram-based communication.
The ability of a distributed system to continue operating correctly even when some components fail or act maliciously. BFT consensus algorithms ensure agreement despite the presence of faulty nodes.

C.definition: A general-purpose, procedural programming language developed at Bell Labs in the 1970s. C provides low-level memory access and maps efficiently to machine instructions, making it foundational for operating systems and embedded systems.

A general-purpose procedural programming language developed at Bell Labs that has greatly influenced many other modern languages. C provides low-level access to memory and is widely used for system programming.

A general-purpose programming language that extends C with object-oriented features, templates, and standard libraries. It is used for system software, game engines, embedded systems, and high-performance applications.
Customer Acquisition Cost — the total cost of acquiring a new customer, including marketing, sales, and related expenses. CAC is a key metric for evaluating the efficiency of growth strategies.

A high-speed data storage layer that stores frequently accessed data for rapid retrieval. Caching reduces latency and server load by keeping copies of data closer to where it's needed, used in browsers, CDNs, databases, and CPUs.
The process of removing or updating stale data from a cache when the underlying data changes. It is famously one of the hardest problems in computer science, requiring careful consistency management.
The practice of storing frequently accessed data in a fast-access storage layer to reduce latency and load on backend systems. Caching strategies include write-through, write-back, and write-around.
A stack data structure that stores information about the active subroutines of a program. Each function call pushes a new frame onto the stack, and returning from a function pops the frame.

A function passed as an argument to another function, to be executed after an operation completes. Callbacks are a fundamental pattern in asynchronous programming, though modern code often uses Promises or async/await instead.
A deployment strategy where a new version of software is gradually rolled out to a small subset of users before being deployed to the entire infrastructure. It reduces risk by limiting exposure.
A deployment strategy where a new version is gradually rolled out to a small subset of users before being deployed to the entire infrastructure. It reduces risk by detecting issues early.
An HTML element used to draw graphics on a web page via JavaScript. The Canvas API provides means for drawing shapes, text, images, and animations in both 2D and 3D contexts.
A theoretical framework stating that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide only two of three guarantees: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.
The process of determining the production capacity needed by an organization to meet changing demands for its products or services. In IT, it ensures systems can handle expected workloads.
Capital Expenditure — funds used by an organization to acquire, upgrade, or maintain physical assets such as servers, buildings, or equipment. Cloud computing shifts many CapEx costs to OpEx.

A type of challenge-response test used to determine whether the user is human. CAPTCHAs prevent bots from accessing websites and services by requiring tasks that are easy for humans but difficult for machines.
The practice of shifting computing workloads to times and locations where electricity generation produces the lowest carbon emissions. It is part of sustainable and green computing initiatives.
The total amount of greenhouse gases produced by computing activities, including data centers, networks, and end-user devices. Green computing initiatives aim to reduce technology's carbon footprint.
The number of distinct values in a data set or database column. Understanding cardinality is important for query optimization, index selection, and data modeling.
The practice of copying code or patterns from elsewhere without understanding how they work, hoping they will produce the desired result. It leads to fragile and poorly understood codebases.
The process by which CSS rules are applied to HTML elements based on specificity, source order, and importance. Understanding cascading is essential for managing style conflicts in web development.
A phenomenon in neural networks where learning new information causes the network to forget previously learned information. This is a major challenge in continual learning and transfer learning.
Data that represents categories or groups rather than numerical quantities. Categorical variables include nominal data like colors and ordinal data like satisfaction ratings.

Content Delivery Network — a geographically distributed network of proxy servers that cache and deliver content to users from the nearest location. CDNs reduce latency, improve load times, and handle traffic spikes.
The process of removing or updating cached content from a content delivery network before its natural expiration. Invalidation ensures users receive the latest version of web resources.
A structure that houses antennas and electronic communications equipment for cellular networks. Cell towers enable mobile phone connectivity by transmitting and receiving radio frequency signals.
A cloud architecture pattern that divides infrastructure into isolated cells, each serving a subset of users. Cell failures are contained and do not propagate across the entire system.
The primary component of a computer that performs most of the processing inside a computer. The CPU executes instructions from programs by performing basic arithmetic, logic, and I/O operations.
A digital document that uses a digital signature to bind a public key with an identity. SSL/TLS certificates authenticate websites and enable encrypted HTTPS connections.
An entity that issues digital certificates to verify the ownership of a public key. CAs are a critical component of public key infrastructure and enable secure HTTPS connections.
A behavioral design pattern that passes a request along a chain of handlers until one of them handles it. It decouples senders and receivers and allows multiple handlers to process a request.
A hierarchical security model where each component verifies the integrity of the next component in the chain. It is used in certificate authorities, secure boot processes, and code signing.

A technique for improving AI model reasoning by including step-by-step reasoning examples in the prompt. It helps large language models solve complex problems by breaking them into intermediate steps.
A design pattern that identifies and captures changes made to data in a database so that action can be taken using the changed data. CDC is essential for real-time data integration.

The structured approach to transitioning individuals, teams, and organizations from a current state to a desired future state. In IT, it includes controlling changes to infrastructure and applications.
A concurrency primitive used for communication between goroutines in Go or between concurrent processes. Channels provide a typed conduit through which values can be sent and received.
The discipline of experimenting on a system to build confidence in its capability to withstand turbulent conditions in production. Netflix's Chaos Monkey is a famous chaos engineering tool.
A system that pairs each character in a set with a numeric code to enable digital representation and processing of text. UTF-8, ASCII, and Unicode are common character encoding standards.
A graphical representation of data using visual elements like bars, lines, pies, or scatter plots. Charts make complex data more understandable and are essential for data visualization and reporting.

A software application that simulates human conversation through text or voice interactions. Modern chatbots use natural language processing and machine learning to understand and respond to user queries.

A collaboration model that connects people, tools, processes, and automation into a transparent workflow using chat platforms. Teams manage infrastructure, deploy code, and resolve incidents from a chat interface.
A value calculated from a data set that is used to verify data integrity. If the calculated checksum differs from the expected value, the data may have been corrupted or tampered with.
A statistical test used to determine whether there is a significant association between categorical variables. It compares observed frequencies with expected frequencies under the null hypothesis.

A set of web developer tools built directly into the Google Chrome browser for debugging, profiling, and optimizing web applications. DevTools include panels for elements, console, network, and performance.
The rate at which customers stop using a product or service over a given period. Reducing churn is critical for subscription-based businesses and SaaS companies.
The percentage of customers who stop subscribing to a service within a given time period. Churn rate is a critical metric for SaaS and subscription businesses, directly impacting revenue.

Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment — practices that automate the building, testing, and deployment of code changes. CI ensures code is regularly merged and tested, while CD automatically releases validated changes to production.

The three core principles of information security: Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability. The CIA triad guides security policies and is the foundation for evaluating and implementing security measures.
Classless Inter-Domain Routing — a method for allocating IP addresses and IP routing that replaces the older classful addressing system. CIDR notation like 192.168.1.0/24 specifies IP ranges.
A design pattern that prevents a system from repeatedly trying to execute an operation that is likely to fail. It detects failures and prevents the application from trying to perform the action.
A communication method that establishes a dedicated communication channel between two nodes for the duration of a conversation. It was used in traditional telephone networks.

A non-professional developer who creates business applications using low-code or no-code platforms sanctioned by IT. Citizen developers extend an organization's development capacity beyond the IT department.
A blueprint for creating objects in object-oriented programming that defines attributes and methods. Classes encapsulate data and behavior, enabling code reuse through inheritance and polymorphism.
A UML diagram that shows the structure of a system by displaying its classes, attributes, operations, and relationships. Class diagrams are used in object-oriented analysis and design.

A situation in classification tasks where the number of samples in different classes is significantly unequal. Class imbalance can bias models toward the majority class and requires special handling techniques.
A supervised learning task that assigns input data to predefined categories or classes. Common algorithms include logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, and neural networks.
A software architecture philosophy that separates concerns into layers with dependencies pointing inward. It makes systems testable, independent of frameworks, and maintainable over time.
Code that is easy to understand, easy to modify, and easy to maintain. Clean code follows principles like meaningful naming, small functions, and clear abstractions.

A controlled environment for software development where engineers implement functionality based only on specifications, without access to existing proprietary code. It ensures intellectual property protection.
Command-Line Interface — a text-based interface for interacting with software by typing commands. CLIs are widely used by developers and system administrators for their efficiency and scriptability.
A digital certificate used to authenticate a client's identity to a server during a TLS handshake. Mutual TLS authentication uses both client and server certificates for enhanced security.
A distributed application structure that partitions tasks between providers of a resource or service (servers) and service requesters (clients). It is the fundamental architecture of the internet.
A rendering approach where the browser downloads a minimal HTML page and JavaScript bundle, then renders the content on the client using JavaScript. Frameworks like React and Vue commonly use CSR.
The difference in time readings between clocks on different nodes in a distributed system. Clock skew can cause ordering issues and is managed through protocols like NTP and logical clocks.
The process of ensuring that multiple computer clocks maintain the same time. NTP and PTP protocols are used to synchronize clocks across distributed systems.
A modern, dynamic, functional programming language that runs on the Java Virtual Machine. Clojure emphasizes immutability, persistent data structures, and a robust concurrency model.

A function that captures and retains access to variables from its enclosing lexical scope even after the outer function has returned. Closures are fundamental to functional programming and event handling.
The delivery of computing services including servers, storage, databases, networking, and software over the internet. Cloud computing offers on-demand scalability and pay-as-you-go pricing.
A configuration where an application runs in a private cloud or data center and bursts into a public cloud when demand for computing capacity spikes, providing elastic scalability.

The delivery of computing services — servers, storage, databases, networking, and software — over the internet on a pay-as-you-go basis. Cloud computing eliminates the need for organizations to own and maintain physical infrastructure.

The process of reducing overall cloud spending by identifying mismanaged resources, eliminating waste, and right-sizing capacity. It involves monitoring usage patterns and leveraging reserved instances.
An open-source platform as a service that provides a choice of clouds, developer frameworks, and application services. It simplifies the deployment and management of cloud applications.

Event-driven serverless compute services offered by cloud providers that execute code in response to triggers without managing servers. Google Cloud Functions and AWS Lambda are popular implementations.
A cloud-based hardware security module that provides secure cryptographic key storage and operations. Cloud HSMs protect sensitive keys while offering the scalability of cloud services.

The process of moving data, applications, or other business elements from an on-premises data center to a cloud computing environment. Migration strategies include lift-and-shift, re-platforming, and re-architecting.

An approach to building and running applications that exploits the advantages of cloud computing delivery models. Cloud-native applications are designed using microservices, containers, and dynamic orchestration.

The networking infrastructure and services that connect cloud resources, including virtual networks, load balancers, DNS, and VPN gateways. Cloud networking enables secure and efficient communication between cloud components.

The broad set of policies, technologies, and controls deployed to protect data, applications, and infrastructure associated with cloud computing. It addresses threats unique to shared, multi-tenant environments.
A model of data storage where digital data is stored on remote servers accessed via the internet. Cloud storage providers manage capacity, security, and durability while offering scalable access.
An approach to building and running applications that fully exploits the advantages of cloud computing, including microservices, containers, orchestration, and continuous delivery.

A serverless execution environment that allows developers to run JavaScript code at the edge of Cloudflare's global network. Workers enable low-latency responses by processing requests close to users.

Common Language Runtime — the virtual machine component of Microsoft's .NET framework that manages the execution of .NET programs. It provides services like memory management, type safety, and exception handling.
A group of interconnected computers that work together as a single system to provide high availability, load balancing, or parallel processing. Clusters are fundamental to distributed computing.

An unsupervised machine learning technique that groups similar data points together based on shared characteristics. Common algorithms include K-means, DBSCAN, and hierarchical clustering, used in customer segmentation and pattern discovery.

An open-source, cross-platform build system generator that uses platform-independent configuration files to generate native build files for compilers. CMake supports complex build processes for C and C++ projects.
