Content Management System — software that enables users to create, manage, and modify digital content without specialized technical knowledge. Popular CMS platforms include WordPress, Drupal, and headless CMS solutions like Strapi and Contentful.
A Canonical Name record in DNS that maps an alias name to a true canonical domain name. CNAME records are commonly used to associate subdomains with the domain hosting their content.

Convolutional Neural Network — a deep learning architecture primarily used for processing structured grid data like images. CNNs use convolutional layers to automatically learn spatial hierarchies of features.
An IDE feature that suggests code completions as the developer types, based on context, language syntax, and available APIs. Modern AI-powered completions can suggest entire functions.
A metric that measures the percentage of source code executed during automated testing. Higher code coverage generally indicates more thorough testing but does not guarantee absence of bugs.

A software application designed for writing and editing source code, offering features like syntax highlighting, auto-completion, and debugging tools. Popular editors include VS Code, Vim, and Sublime Text.
A period during which no new code changes are allowed in a codebase, typically before a major release. Code freezes ensure stability by allowing only critical bug fixes during the freeze window.
The process of automatically producing source code from higher-level specifications, models, or AI prompts. Code generation reduces boilerplate and accelerates development through automation.

The practice of deliberately making source or machine code difficult to understand to prevent reverse engineering or tampering. Obfuscation techniques include renaming, control flow alteration, and encryption.
A measure of how well source code meets standards for readability, maintainability, reliability, and performance. Code quality practices include code reviews, linting, static analysis, and testing.
The systematic examination of source code by peers to find bugs, improve code quality, and share knowledge. Code reviews are a standard practice in professional software development.
The process of digitally signing executables and scripts to confirm the software author and guarantee that the code has not been altered or corrupted since it was signed.
Any characteristic in source code that possibly indicates a deeper problem, such as duplicated code, long methods, or excessive coupling. Code smells suggest the need for refactoring.
A technique that breaks a JavaScript bundle into smaller chunks that are loaded on demand. Code splitting reduces initial page load time by deferring the loading of non-critical code.
A set of conventions and guidelines governing the formatting and organization of source code. Consistent code style improves readability and reduces cognitive load during code reviews.
A program or device that encodes or decodes a data stream or signal. Codecs are used for compressing audio, video, and image files to reduce storage requirements and bandwidth usage.
A problem or exercise designed to test programming skills, often used in technical interviews and competitive programming. Platforms like LeetCode and HackerRank host coding challenges.
The automatic or implicit conversion of values from one data type to another in a programming language. Type coercion can lead to unexpected behavior and subtle bugs if not well understood.
Technology platforms that simulate human thought processes using self-learning algorithms, data mining, pattern recognition, and natural language processing. IBM Watson is a notable example.
The total amount of mental effort being used in working memory when interacting with a system. Reducing cognitive load in interface design improves usability and user satisfaction.
The degree to which the elements inside a module belong together. High cohesion means a module's responsibilities are closely related and focused, which improves maintainability and reusability.
An analytical technique that groups users who share a common characteristic during a defined time period and tracks their behavior over time. It reveals trends in user retention and engagement.

The latency experienced when a serverless function is invoked for the first time or after being idle. Cold starts occur because the cloud provider must allocate resources and initialize the runtime environment.

The process of two or more people or teams working together to achieve shared goals. In software development, collaboration tools and practices like pair programming and code reviews improve outcomes.
A technique used by recommendation systems that filters information by collecting preferences from many users. It predicts a user's interests by finding patterns among similar users or items.
A mathematical model that describes the range of colors that can be represented. Common color spaces include RGB for screens, CMYK for print, and HSL for intuitive color selection.
A database management system that stores data by columns rather than rows. Column stores excel at analytical queries over large datasets because they can read only the columns needed.
A database management system that stores data by columns rather than by rows. Column stores are optimized for read-heavy analytical queries and are widely used in data warehousing.
A database that stores data in columns instead of rows, optimizing read performance for analytical queries. Examples include Apache Cassandra, ClickHouse, and Amazon Redshift.
A data storage format that organizes data by columns rather than rows, enabling efficient compression and fast analytical queries. Parquet and ORC are popular columnar storage formats.
A text-based interface for interacting with a computer's operating system or applications by typing commands. The command line provides powerful automation and system management capabilities.
A text-based interface used to interact with software and operating systems by typing commands. CLIs offer powerful automation capabilities and are preferred by many developers for their efficiency.
A behavioral design pattern that encapsulates a request as an object, thereby allowing parameterization of clients with different requests and supporting undoable operations.
A principle that states every method should either be a command that performs an action or a query that returns data, but not both. It simplifies reasoning about code and improves testability.
An operation in version control that saves changes to a repository. Each commit creates a snapshot of the project at a specific point in time with an associated message describing the changes.
A temporary named result set in SQL that exists only within the execution scope of a single statement. CTEs improve readability and enable recursive queries.
The exchange of information between people, teams, or systems. In technology, communication encompasses protocols, messaging systems, and collaboration tools that enable teamwork.
A group of people who share common interests, goals, or values, often centered around a technology, framework, or programming language. Open-source communities drive innovation and provide support.
A programming language whose source code is translated into machine code by a compiler before execution. Compiled languages like C, C++, Go, and Rust typically offer better runtime performance.

A program that translates source code written in a high-level programming language into machine code or an intermediate form that a computer can execute. Unlike interpreters, compilers process the entire program before execution.
Techniques used by compilers to improve the performance or reduce the size of generated code. Optimizations include dead code elimination, loop unrolling, and constant folding.
A method of tracking and analyzing streams of information about events and deriving conclusions from them in real time. CEP is used in financial trading, fraud detection, and IoT.

Systems composed of many interconnected components whose collective behavior cannot be simply inferred from the behavior of individual parts. Examples include the internet, ecosystems, and financial markets.
The act of conforming to established guidelines, specifications, regulations, or legislation. In technology, compliance covers data protection laws, industry standards, and security certifications.
A reusable, self-contained piece of UI that encapsulates its own structure, style, and behavior. Components are the building blocks of modern frontend frameworks like React, Vue, and Angular.
A collection of reusable UI components with consistent styling and behavior that developers can use to build applications. Component libraries accelerate development and ensure design consistency.
A system design principle that allows components to be combined in various ways to satisfy specific user requirements. Highly composable systems enable flexible and reusable software architecture.

A design principle where complex objects are built by combining simpler ones rather than through inheritance. Composition promotes flexibility and is favored over inheritance in modern software design.
The process of reducing the size of data files for storage or transmission. Compression algorithms can be lossless (preserving all data) or lossy (sacrificing some data for smaller size).
The process of performing mathematical calculations or data processing using algorithms. Computation is the fundamental activity of computers and forms the basis of all software operations.
The branch of computer science and mathematics that studies what problems can be solved computationally and how efficiently. It encompasses automata theory, computability, and complexity theory.
The study of the resources required to solve computational problems, primarily time and space. It classifies problems into complexity classes like P, NP, and NP-complete.

The application of computational strategies to the design process, using algorithms, simulations, and data to generate and evaluate design solutions. It is used in architecture, product design, and graphics.
The design of computer systems including the instruction set architecture, microarchitecture, and system design. It determines how hardware components work together to execute software.
A field of artificial intelligence that trains computers to interpret and understand the visual world. Using digital images and deep learning models, machines can identify and classify objects.
The ability of a system to handle multiple tasks at the same time by interleaving their execution. Concurrency differs from parallelism in that concurrent tasks may not execute simultaneously.
A synchronization primitive used with a mutex to allow threads to wait for specific conditions to become true. Condition variables are essential for implementing producer-consumer patterns.
A programming construct that executes different code blocks based on whether a specified condition evaluates to true or false. If-else statements and switch statements are common conditionals.
A cloud computing technology that isolates sensitive data in a protected CPU enclave during processing. It ensures data remains encrypted and inaccessible even to the cloud provider.
The arrangement of settings and options that define how software or systems operate. Configuration management ensures systems are set up consistently and changes are tracked and controlled.
The process of systematically handling changes to a system to maintain integrity over time. Tools like Ansible, Chef, and Puppet automate configuration management across servers.
The process of resolving incompatible changes when merging code branches or synchronizing distributed data. In version control, conflict resolution requires manual review of conflicting changes.

A data structure designed for distributed systems that can be updated independently on different replicas and always converges to a consistent state without coordination. Used in collaborative editing and distributed databases.

A table used to evaluate the performance of a classification model showing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives. It enables calculation of accuracy, precision, and recall.

A cache of database connections maintained so they can be reused rather than created and destroyed for each request. Connection pooling significantly improves application performance and resource utilization.

An agreement protocol in distributed systems where multiple nodes agree on a single data value or state. Consensus algorithms like Raft and Paxos are fundamental to distributed databases and blockchains.
An algorithm used in distributed systems to achieve agreement on a single data value among distributed processes. Raft, Paxos, and PBFT are well-known consensus algorithms.

A property of distributed systems ensuring that all nodes see the same data at the same time. Consistency is one of the three guarantees in the CAP theorem, along with availability and partition tolerance.
A hashing technique that minimizes the number of keys that need to be remapped when the number of nodes changes. It is widely used in distributed caches and load balancers.
A special method in object-oriented programming that is automatically called when an object is created. Constructors initialize the object's properties and set up its initial state.
A component that receives and processes messages or data from a queue, stream, or producer. In event-driven architectures, consumers subscribe to topics and handle incoming events.

A lightweight, standalone package that includes everything needed to run a piece of software, including the code, runtime, libraries, and system tools. Containers provide consistent environments across development and production.
A repository for storing and distributing container images. Docker Hub, Amazon ECR, and Google Container Registry are popular registries for managing container artifacts.

A lightweight virtualization method that packages applications with their dependencies into isolated containers. Containers share the host OS kernel, making them faster and more efficient than traditional virtual machines.
A geographically distributed group of servers that work together to provide fast delivery of internet content. CDNs cache content at edge locations close to end users to reduce latency.
The process of creating, managing, and modifying digital content. Content management systems like WordPress and Drupal provide tools for non-technical users to manage website content.
A strategic marketing approach focused on creating and distributing valuable, relevant content to attract and retain a target audience. It builds brand awareness and drives customer engagement.
The practice of monitoring and filtering user-generated content to ensure it complies with community guidelines and legal requirements. It uses a combination of AI and human review.

An HTTP response header that allows website administrators to control resources the user agent is allowed to load for a given page. CSP helps prevent cross-site scripting and other code injection attacks.
The planning, creation, delivery, and governance of content across all channels. A content strategy ensures that content supports business goals, meets user needs, and remains consistent.

A software development practice where code changes are automatically built, tested, and prepared for a release to production. It extends continuous integration by deploying all code changes after the build stage.
An ongoing effort to improve products, services, or processes through incremental and breakthrough improvements. In software, it involves iterative refinement based on feedback and metrics.
The ongoing process of observing system health, security, and compliance in real time. It enables rapid detection and response to performance issues, security threats, and policy violations.
The order in which individual statements, instructions, or function calls are executed in a program. Control flow is regulated by conditional statements, loops, and function calls.
The part of a network that carries signaling traffic and is responsible for routing. In Kubernetes, the control plane manages the overall state of the cluster and makes scheduling decisions.
A system that manages, commands, directs, or regulates the behavior of other systems or processes. Control systems are used in manufacturing, robotics, HVAC, and industrial automation.
A software design paradigm that decreases the number of decisions developers need to make by providing sensible defaults. Frameworks like Ruby on Rails popularized this principle.

AI systems designed to engage in human-like dialogue, understanding natural language inputs and generating contextual responses. Conversational AI powers chatbots, virtual assistants, and customer service tools.

The percentage of users who complete a desired action, such as making a purchase, signing up, or clicking a button. Optimizing conversion rates (CRO) is central to digital marketing and product design.

A mathematical operation used in neural networks, particularly CNNs, where a filter slides over input data to produce feature maps. Convolution is fundamental to image recognition and signal processing.
A class of deep neural networks most commonly applied to analyzing visual imagery. CNNs use convolution operations to automatically learn spatial hierarchies of features from input images.
A small piece of data stored on the user's computer by a web browser that remembers information between sessions. Cookies are used for session management, personalization, and tracking.
Legal protection granted to the creator of original works including software, documentation, and digital content. Copyright governs reproduction, distribution, and adaptation of creative works.

Common Object Request Broker Architecture — a standard defined by the Object Management Group for enabling communication between software components regardless of programming language or operating system.

A set of specific metrics defined by Google that measure user experience on web pages, including loading performance, interactivity, and visual stability. They influence search rankings and guide web optimization.
A component that generalizes subroutines for cooperative multitasking by allowing execution to be suspended and resumed. Coroutines are used in async/await patterns in modern programming languages.
A statistical measure that describes the extent to which two variables change together. Correlation does not imply causation but helps identify relationships between variables in data analysis.

Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — a security mechanism that allows web pages to request resources from a different domain than the one serving the page. CORS headers control which origins, methods, and headers are permitted in cross-origin requests.
The process of reducing cloud spending while maintaining performance and functionality. Cost optimization strategies include right-sizing resources, using reserved instances, and eliminating waste.
The degree of interdependence between software modules. Low coupling is desirable as it makes systems more modular, maintainable, and testable by reducing dependencies between components.

Central Processing Unit — the primary component of a computer that performs most of the data processing. The CPU executes instructions from programs by performing arithmetic, logic, control, and input/output operations.
The process of determining which process or thread gets to use the CPU at any given time. Scheduling algorithms like Round Robin, FIFO, and Priority Scheduling optimize system performance.

Command Query Responsibility Segregation — a pattern that separates read and write operations into different models. CQRS can improve performance, scalability, and security in complex domain-driven applications.

Conflict-Free Replicated Data Type — a data structure that can be replicated across multiple computers in a network, where replicas can be updated independently and always converge to a consistent state.
A type of cyber attack where stolen username-password combinations from one breach are used to gain unauthorized access to other accounts. It exploits the common practice of password reuse.

The longest sequence of dependent tasks in a project that determines the minimum project duration. Identifying the critical path helps project managers focus resources on tasks that affect the timeline.
Customer Relationship Management — a technology and strategy for managing all interactions with current and potential customers. CRM systems track sales, customer service, and marketing activities.

A time-based job scheduler in Unix-like operating systems that executes commands or scripts at specified intervals. Cron jobs are widely used for automated maintenance tasks, backups, and data processing.
A time-based job scheduler in Unix-like operating systems that allows users to schedule scripts or commands to run automatically at specified intervals. Cron is fundamental to system administration.
A statistical method for evaluating machine learning models by partitioning data into subsets, training on some and testing on others. K-fold cross validation is the most common technique.
The ability of software to run on multiple operating systems or hardware platforms without modification. Cross-platform frameworks like Flutter, React Native, and Electron enable this capability.
The practice of selling additional products or services to existing customers. In technology, cross-selling leverages data analytics and recommendation systems to suggest complementary offerings.
An attack that forces authenticated users to submit unwanted requests to a web application. CSRF exploits the trust that a site has in the user's browser.

A resampling technique used to evaluate machine learning models on limited data by splitting the dataset into multiple folds for iterative training and testing. It provides a more robust performance estimate.
Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four basic operations of persistent storage. CRUD operations form the foundation of most database-driven applications and RESTful API designs.
A digital or virtual currency that uses cryptography for security and operates on decentralized networks based on blockchain technology. Bitcoin and Ethereum are the most well-known cryptocurrencies.
A mathematical algorithm that maps data of arbitrary size to a fixed-size output. Cryptographic hashes are one-way functions used for data integrity verification and password storage.
The practice and study of techniques for secure communication in the presence of adversaries. Modern cryptography encompasses symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, and digital signatures.

Cross-Site Request Forgery — a web security vulnerability that tricks a user's browser into executing unwanted actions on a trusted site where they are authenticated. CSRF tokens are the primary defense.

Cascading Style Sheets — a stylesheet language used to describe the visual presentation of HTML documents. CSS controls layout, colors, fonts, animations, and responsive behavior, separating content structure from visual design.

The technique of animating HTML elements using CSS properties like transition, transform, and keyframes. CSS animations provide smooth visual effects without JavaScript and are hardware-accelerated by browsers.
A pre-built collection of CSS files providing consistent styling, layout systems, and UI components. Popular frameworks like Bootstrap, Tailwind CSS, and Bulma accelerate web development.
Custom properties defined in CSS using the -- prefix that store reusable values like colors, fonts, and spacing. CSS variables enable theming and reduce repetition in stylesheets.

Comma-Separated Values — a plain-text file format that stores tabular data with each line representing a row and commas separating column values. CSV is widely used for data exchange between applications.
A parallel computing platform and API model created by NVIDIA that allows developers to use GPUs for general-purpose processing. CUDA is extensively used in deep learning and scientific computing.

A functional programming technique that transforms a function with multiple arguments into a sequence of functions, each taking a single argument. Currying enables partial application and function composition.

A database object used to retrieve and manipulate data one row at a time from a result set. Cursors are useful when row-by-row processing is needed but can be less efficient than set-based operations.
A Web Components specification that allows developers to define new HTML elements with custom behavior and styling. Custom elements extend the browser's built-in element set with reusable components.

An extension of the Kubernetes API that allows users to define their own resource types. Custom resources, combined with custom controllers, enable operators to manage complex applications declaratively.
The overall perception a customer has of a brand based on all interactions throughout the customer journey. CX encompasses every touchpoint from awareness to purchase to support.

The complete path a customer takes when interacting with a product or service, from initial awareness through purchase and beyond. Mapping customer journeys helps identify opportunities for improvement.
A visual representation of every experience a customer has with a brand across all touchpoints. It helps organizations understand customer needs and identify opportunities for improvement.
The business methodology of ensuring customers achieve their desired outcomes while using a product or service. Customer success teams proactively guide customers to maximize value and reduce churn.
The range of services provided to help customers use a product or service effectively. Modern customer support combines human agents, self-service portals, chatbots, and knowledge bases.

Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures — a standardized system for identifying and cataloging publicly known cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Each CVE entry has a unique ID used for tracking and referencing.
Evidence-based knowledge about existing or emerging threats to an organization's assets. CTI helps security teams anticipate and prepare for potential cyber attacks.

The practice of protecting systems, networks, and data from digital attacks, unauthorized access, and damage. Cybersecurity encompasses everything from encryption and firewalls to security policies and incident response.
A structured set of guidelines and best practices for managing cybersecurity risk. Notable frameworks include NIST CSF, ISO 27001, and CIS Controls, which provide systematic approaches to security.

D.definition: A JavaScript library for producing dynamic, interactive data visualizations in web browsers using SVG, HTML, and CSS. D3 binds data to the DOM and applies data-driven transformations.
A background process that runs continuously on a system without direct user interaction. Daemons handle tasks like scheduling, logging, network requests, and hardware device monitoring.
Directed Acyclic Graph — a finite directed graph with no directed cycles. DAGs are used in version control systems, task scheduling, data processing pipelines, and blockchain technology.
A pointer that references a memory location that has been freed or deallocated. Accessing a dangling pointer leads to undefined behavior, crashes, or security vulnerabilities.

Information assets that organizations collect, process, and store during regular business operations but generally fail to use for any other purpose. Dark data represents untapped analytical potential.
A user interface design intentionally crafted to trick users into taking actions they did not intend, such as subscribing to services or sharing personal data. Dark patterns are considered unethical.
A client-optimized programming language developed by Google for building mobile, desktop, server, and web applications. Dart is the primary language for the Flutter framework.
An independent worker in the Dart programming language that has its own memory heap and runs concurrently with other isolates. Isolates communicate through message passing without shared state.
A visual display of the most important information needed to achieve one or more objectives, consolidated on a single screen. Dashboards provide at-a-glance views of key performance indicators.

The process of inspecting, cleansing, transforming, and modeling data to discover useful information, draw conclusions, and support decision-making. It combines statistical methods with domain expertise.

The design of data systems including models, policies, rules, and standards that govern how data is collected, stored, managed, and used. Good data architecture ensures data quality and accessibility.
Data that is stored on a device or medium and is not actively being transferred or processed. Encrypting data at rest protects it from unauthorized access if the storage medium is compromised.
A technique used to increase the diversity of training data without collecting new data by applying transformations like rotation, flipping, and scaling to existing samples.
A technique that connects data sources to UI elements so that changes in the data are automatically reflected in the UI and vice versa. Data binding simplifies frontend development.
An incident where sensitive, protected, or confidential data is accessed, disclosed, or stolen by an unauthorized party. Data breaches can result in financial losses and reputational damage.
A centralized repository of metadata that helps organizations find, understand, and manage their data assets. Data catalogs improve data governance and enable self-service analytics.
A facility that houses computer systems and associated components such as telecommunications and storage systems. Modern data centers feature redundant power, cooling, and network connectivity.

A class primarily used to store data with minimal behavior, often with automatically generated methods like equality comparison, hashing, and string representation. Kotlin, Python, and Java support data classes.
The process of detecting and correcting corrupt, inaccurate, or irrelevant records from a dataset. Data cleaning is a critical step in data preparation that significantly impacts analysis quality.
The systematic process of gathering information from various sources for analysis. Data collection methods include surveys, web scraping, APIs, sensors, and database queries.
A formal agreement between data producers and consumers that defines the structure, format, semantics, and quality guarantees of shared data. Data contracts improve reliability in data pipelines.
A change in the statistical properties of production data compared to the training data over time. Data drift can degrade model performance and requires monitoring and retraining strategies.
A symmetric-key algorithm for the encryption of digital data that was widely used from the 1970s until being superseded by AES. DES uses a 56-bit key and processes 64-bit blocks.
The practice of designing, building, and maintaining systems that collect, store, and analyze data at scale. Data engineers create the infrastructure that enables data scientists to do their work.
An architecture approach that provides a unified data management framework across distributed environments. It simplifies data access and sharing while ensuring consistent governance and security.
A standardized structure for organizing and encoding data for storage or transmission. Common data formats include JSON, XML, CSV, Parquet, and Protocol Buffers.
The overall management of the availability, usability, integrity, and security of data used in an enterprise. Data governance includes policies, standards, and processes for managing data assets.
The process of combining data from different sources into a unified view. Data integration involves ETL pipelines, data mapping, and ensuring consistency across diverse data sources.
The maintenance of, and assurance of, data accuracy and consistency over its entire lifecycle. Data integrity ensures that information remains unaltered during storage, transfer, and retrieval.

A centralized repository that stores vast amounts of raw data in its native format until needed for analysis. Unlike data warehouses, data lakes accept structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data.
A data management architecture that combines the key benefits of data lakes and data warehouses. It provides the low-cost storage of a data lake with the management features of a data warehouse.

The stages data goes through from creation to archival or deletion, including collection, storage, processing, analysis, sharing, and disposal. Managing the data lifecycle ensures compliance and efficiency.
The process of tracking data from its origin through all transformations to its final destination. Data lineage helps organizations understand data flow, ensure compliance, and debug data issues.
The concept of moving computation close to where data resides rather than moving large amounts of data to where computation happens. Data locality is a key optimization in distributed computing.
A subset of a data warehouse focused on a specific business line, department, or subject area. Data marts provide faster query performance for targeted analytical needs.
The process of hiding original data with modified content while maintaining its format and usability. Data masking protects sensitive information in non-production environments.
A decentralized data architecture that organizes data by business domain with domain-oriented ownership. It treats data as a product and applies platform thinking to data infrastructure.
The process of discovering patterns, correlations, and anomalies in large datasets using statistical and computational techniques. Data mining extracts actionable knowledge from raw data.
An abstract representation of how data is organized, stored, and accessed within a system. Data models define entities, attributes, relationships, and constraints that structure information.
The process of organizing data in a database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity. Normalization involves dividing large tables into smaller tables and defining relationships between them.
A set of data processing elements connected in series where the output of one element is the input of the next. Data pipelines automate the flow of data from source systems to target destinations.
The part of a network that carries user traffic and forwards packets based on routing decisions made by the control plane. The data plane handles the actual movement of data.
The aspect of information technology that deals with the proper handling of personal data including consent, notice, and regulatory obligations. GDPR and CCPA are major data privacy regulations.
The collection, transformation, and organization of raw data into meaningful information. Data processing includes cleaning, filtering, aggregating, and enriching data for analysis or storage.
The process of examining data available in a source and collecting statistics and information about that data. Profiling helps assess data quality and understand data structure before analysis.

Practices and technologies for safeguarding data from unauthorized access, corruption, or loss throughout its lifecycle. Data protection encompasses encryption, access controls, backups, and compliance with privacy regulations.

The measure of data's fitness for its intended use, assessed across dimensions like accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, and validity. High data quality is essential for reliable analysis and decision-making.
A condition where two or more threads access shared data simultaneously and at least one modifies it, leading to unpredictable results. Data races are a common source of concurrency bugs.

The methods and formats used to encode information in computing systems, including binary, hexadecimal, floating-point, and character encodings. Data representation determines how computers store and process information.
Legal or regulatory requirements that data about a country's citizens or residents must be collected, processed, and stored within that country's borders.

An interdisciplinary field that uses scientific methods, algorithms, and systems to extract knowledge and insights from structured and unstructured data. It combines statistics, machine learning, and domain expertise.

The practice of protecting digital data from unauthorized access, corruption, or theft throughout its lifecycle. Data security encompasses encryption, access controls, monitoring, and secure disposal.
The process of converting data structures or object states into a format that can be stored or transmitted. Common serialization formats include JSON, XML, Protocol Buffers, and Avro.
A horizontal scaling strategy that distributes data across multiple database instances based on a shard key. Sharding improves performance and scalability for large-scale databases.
The concept that data is subject to the laws and governance structures of the country in which it is collected or stored. It affects cloud architecture decisions for multinational organizations.
A role responsible for managing and overseeing an organization's data assets to ensure data quality, integrity, and compliance. Data stewards bridge the gap between technical and business teams.
The recording of information in a storage medium, including hard drives, SSDs, cloud storage, and distributed file systems. Data storage technologies vary in capacity, speed, cost, and durability.

A specialized format for organizing, processing, and storing data in a computer. Common data structures include arrays, linked lists, trees, graphs, hash tables, and stacks — each optimized for different operations and use cases.
A data lake that has become unmanageable due to lack of data quality, governance, or metadata. Data swamps make it difficult to find and trust data for analysis.
A classification that specifies which type of value a variable can hold, such as integer, string, boolean, or float. Data types determine the operations that can be performed on data.

The graphical representation of data and information using charts, graphs, maps, and dashboards. Effective visualization makes complex data patterns, trends, and outliers easily understandable for both technical and non-technical audiences.
A centralized repository that stores structured data from multiple sources for reporting and analysis. Data warehouses use ETL processes to transform raw data into a format optimized for querying.
The process of transforming and mapping raw data from one format into another to make it more appropriate for downstream analysis. Also known as data munging.

An organized collection of structured data stored electronically, designed for efficient retrieval, insertion, and management. Databases range from simple relational systems (SQL) to distributed NoSQL solutions handling millions of operations per second.
A rule enforced on data columns in a database table to ensure data integrity. Constraints include primary keys, foreign keys, unique constraints, not-null constraints, and check constraints.
The process of defining the structure, relationships, and constraints of a database to efficiently store and retrieve data. It involves normalization, indexing strategies, and schema design.
The process of moving data and schema changes between database versions or systems. Migration tools track and apply incremental changes to database schemas in a controlled, repeatable manner.

The measures and technologies used to protect databases from unauthorized access, data breaches, and malicious attacks. It includes access controls, encryption, auditing, and vulnerability management.
A Python decorator that automatically generates special methods such as __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ for classes that primarily store data. Dataclasses reduce boilerplate in Python code.

A programming paradigm where the execution of operations is determined by data availability rather than explicit control flow. Dataflow architectures are used in parallel processing and stream computing.
A programming paradigm where the program is modeled as a directed graph of data flowing between operations. Execution is driven by data availability rather than explicit control flow.
A two-dimensional, tabular data structure with labeled rows and columns, commonly used in data analysis. DataFrames in libraries like pandas and Spark provide powerful data manipulation capabilities.

A structured collection of data organized for analysis or machine learning. Datasets can be tabular, image-based, text-based, or multimodal, and serve as the foundation for training and evaluating models.
A command-line tool that enables data analysts and engineers to transform data in their warehouse using SQL. dbt handles dependency management, testing, and documentation of data transformations.

Distributed Denial of Service — a cyberattack that overwhelms a target server or network with a flood of traffic from multiple sources. DDoS attacks aim to make services unavailable to legitimate users and can be mitigated with CDNs and traffic filtering.
Source code that is executed but whose result is never used, or code that can never be reached during execution. Removing dead code improves maintainability, readability, and sometimes performance.
A queue that stores messages that cannot be processed successfully after a specified number of attempts. DLQs help debug message processing failures and prevent data loss.
A situation in concurrent programming where two or more processes are unable to proceed because each is waiting for the other to release a resource. Deadlock prevention is a critical concern.

The process of identifying circular wait conditions in concurrent systems where two or more processes are each waiting for resources held by the others. Detection algorithms use resource allocation graphs.
An open-source distributed platform for change data capture that streams changes from databases into Apache Kafka. It enables real-time data pipelines from traditional databases.

A free and open-source Linux distribution known for its stability, extensive package repository, and strong commitment to free software principles. Debian serves as the foundation for Ubuntu and many other distributions.

A programming technique that limits how often a function is called by delaying its execution until a specified time has elapsed since the last invocation. Debouncing is commonly used for search inputs and resize events.
A programming technique that ensures a function is only executed after a specified time period has elapsed since its last invocation. It is commonly used for handling rapid user input events.

A software tool that helps developers find and fix bugs by allowing them to pause execution, inspect variables, step through code line by line, and set breakpoints. Common debuggers include GDB, Chrome DevTools, and IDE-integrated tools.

The process of identifying, analyzing, and removing errors (bugs) from software. Debugging tools include breakpoints, stack traces, logging, and specialized debuggers that let developers step through code execution line by line.

The distribution of authority, control, and data away from a single central entity. Decentralized systems like blockchain networks improve resilience, censorship resistance, and reduce single points of failure.
An application that runs on a decentralized network rather than a single computer, typically using blockchain or peer-to-peer technology. DApps operate without a central point of control.

A tree-like model of decisions and their possible consequences used for classification and regression tasks. Decision trees are interpretable and form the basis for ensemble methods like Random Forest.
A programming paradigm that expresses the logic of a computation without describing its control flow. SQL, HTML, and CSS are examples of declarative languages.

A tool that translates compiled machine code or bytecode back into a higher-level programming language. Decompilers are used in reverse engineering, security analysis, and understanding legacy software.
A structural design pattern that allows adding new behaviors to objects dynamically by placing them inside special wrapper objects. Decorators provide a flexible alternative to subclassing.
A copy of an object that creates a new instance with copies of all nested objects, rather than references to the same nested objects. Deep copies are independent of the original.

A subset of machine learning that uses artificial neural networks with multiple layers to learn complex patterns from large amounts of data. Deep learning powers breakthroughs in image recognition, natural language processing, and generative AI.
A subfield of machine learning that combines deep neural networks with reinforcement learning. It has achieved superhuman performance in games and is applied to robotics and autonomous systems.
AI-generated synthetic media where a person's likeness is convincingly replaced with someone else's using deep learning techniques. Deepfakes raise concerns about misinformation, fraud, and privacy.

Decentralized Finance — financial services built on blockchain technology that operate without traditional intermediaries like banks. DeFi applications include lending, borrowing, trading, and insurance.
An object-oriented design pattern where an object handles a request by delegating to a helper object rather than performing the task itself. Delegation promotes composition over inheritance.
An open-source storage layer that brings ACID transactions to Apache Spark and big data workloads. It provides reliability, performance, and data quality guarantees on top of data lakes.
A physical or logical subnetwork that separates an internal network from an untrusted external network like the internet. DMZs host public-facing services while protecting internal resources.

A tree-like diagram used to visualize the results of hierarchical clustering. Dendrograms show the arrangement of clusters and the distances at which clusters merge, helping determine optimal cluster counts.

An attack that aims to make a computer resource unavailable to its intended users by overwhelming it with traffic or exploiting vulnerabilities. DoS attacks disrupt services and can cause significant business impact.

A runtime for JavaScript and TypeScript built on the V8 engine that aims to be a more secure and modern alternative to Node.js. Deno supports TypeScript natively and has a permissions-based security model.
The process of adding redundant data to a normalized database to improve read performance. Denormalization trades storage space and write complexity for faster queries.
A software component, library, or module that another piece of software requires to function. Managing dependencies involves tracking versions, resolving conflicts, and ensuring compatibility.
A directed graph representing dependencies between modules, packages, or tasks. Dependency graphs are used for build ordering, package management, and understanding system architecture.

A design pattern where an object receives its dependencies from external sources rather than creating them internally. DI promotes loose coupling, testability, and modularity by separating object creation from business logic.
The process of tracking, resolving, and maintaining the external libraries and packages that a software project relies on. Tools like npm, pip, and Maven automate dependency management.
The process of releasing software to a production environment where it becomes available to users. Deployment strategies include rolling updates, blue-green deployments, and canary releases.
An automated sequence of stages that code changes pass through from development to production. Pipelines typically include building, testing, staging, and deploying phases.
The process of marking a feature, API, or component as outdated and scheduled for removal in future versions. Deprecation notices give users time to migrate to recommended alternatives.
A graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. DFS is used for topological sorting, cycle detection, and pathfinding.
The process of converting data from a serialized format like JSON, XML, or binary back into an in-memory object or data structure. Insecure deserialization is a common security vulnerability.
The process of planning and creating the visual appearance, user interface, and user experience of a product. Design encompasses research, wireframing, prototyping, and visual design.
A reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem in software design. The Gang of Four book documented 23 classic design patterns categorized as creational, structural, and behavioral.

A comprehensive collection of reusable components, guidelines, and standards that ensure consistency across a product or brand. Design systems include UI components, typography scales, color palettes, spacing rules, and interaction patterns.
A human-centered approach to innovation that draws from the designer's toolkit to integrate the needs of people, technology possibilities, and business requirements.
Named entities that store visual design attributes such as colors, typography, and spacing as platform-agnostic values. Design tokens bridge the gap between design tools and code implementations.

A personal computer designed for regular use at a single location, or the primary screen of a graphical user interface operating system. Desktop computing remains essential for professional and creative work.
A software application that runs locally on a computer's operating system rather than in a web browser. Desktop apps can offer better performance and deeper OS integration than web apps.

A graphical user interface implementation for operating systems that provides a complete workspace including a window manager, file manager, and system utilities. GNOME, KDE, and macOS are popular desktop environments.

A syntax feature in languages like JavaScript and Python that allows extracting values from arrays or objects into distinct variables. Destructuring simplifies code by reducing verbose property access.
An algorithm that, given a particular input, will always produce the same output and follow the same sequence of steps. Determinism is important for reproducibility and debugging.

The overall experience and satisfaction developers have when using tools, APIs, frameworks, and platforms. Good developer experience includes clear documentation, intuitive design, and helpful error messages.

A local server environment used during software development that provides features like hot reloading, debugging, and mock services. Development servers simulate production environments without affecting live systems.

A set of practices that combines software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops) to shorten the development lifecycle while delivering high-quality software. DevOps emphasizes automation, collaboration, and continuous improvement.
An approach that integrates security practices within the DevOps process, making security a shared responsibility throughout the software development lifecycle.

Browser Developer Tools — built-in debugging and profiling tools in web browsers that allow developers to inspect HTML, CSS, JavaScript, network requests, and performance. Chrome DevTools and Firefox DevTools are widely used.
Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol — a network management protocol that automatically assigns IP addresses and other network configuration parameters to devices on a network.
A comparison showing the differences between two files or versions of a file, typically displayed as added, removed, and modified lines. Diff tools are essential for code review and version control.
A method of securely exchanging cryptographic keys over a public channel, named after Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman. It is one of the earliest practical examples of public key exchange.
An electronic document that uses a digital signature to bind a public key with an identity. Digital certificates are used to verify the ownership of public keys in SSL/TLS connections.
An electronic circuit that operates on digital signals using discrete voltage levels representing binary 0 and 1. Digital circuits form the basis of all modern computers and digital devices.

The promotion of products or services through digital channels including search engines, social media, email, and websites. Digital marketing uses data analytics to target and measure campaign effectiveness.
A mathematical scheme for verifying the authenticity and integrity of digital messages or documents. Digital signatures provide authentication, non-repudiation, and integrity guarantees.
The process of using digital technologies to fundamentally change how an organization operates and delivers value to customers. It involves cultural change as well as technology adoption.
A virtual representation of a physical object, process, or system that serves as its real-time digital counterpart. Digital twins are used in manufacturing, urban planning, and healthcare.
A graph algorithm that finds the shortest path between nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It is fundamental to routing, navigation systems, and network optimization.
Techniques for reducing the number of input variables in a dataset while preserving important information. PCA, t-SNE, and autoencoders are common dimensionality reduction methods.
A graph in which edges have a direction, going from one vertex to another. Directed graphs model relationships like dependencies, workflows, and web page links.

A hierarchical database that stores and provides access to information about network resources including users, computers, and services. LDAP and Active Directory are common directory service implementations.
A set of policies, tools, and procedures to enable the recovery of vital technology infrastructure and systems following a natural or human-induced disaster.
A type of machine learning model that learns the boundary between classes directly from the data. Examples include logistic regression, SVMs, and neural networks used for classification.
An output device that visually presents information to users. Display technologies include LCD, OLED, LED, and e-ink, varying in resolution, refresh rate, color accuracy, and power consumption.

A process by which a smaller company with fewer resources successfully challenges established businesses by addressing overlooked segments. Disruptive innovation transforms markets and creates new value networks.

A model where components located on networked computers communicate and coordinate their actions by passing messages. Distributed computing enables processing large workloads across multiple machines.
A consensus of replicated, shared, and synchronized digital data geographically spread across multiple sites, countries, or institutions. Blockchain is the most well-known type of distributed ledger.

A system whose components are located on different networked computers that communicate and coordinate their actions by passing messages. Distributed systems face challenges like network partitions and consistency.

Systems whose components are located on different networked computers that communicate and coordinate to achieve a common goal. Distributed systems face challenges like consistency, partition tolerance, and latency.
A method of tracking application requests as they flow through distributed systems. Tools like Jaeger, Zipkin, and AWS X-Ray help developers identify performance bottlenecks.
A high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development and clean, pragmatic design. Django includes an ORM, authentication, admin interface, and many other features out of the box.
Dynamic Link Library — a file containing code and data that can be used by more than one program at the same time. DLLs promote modularization and reduce memory usage.

Domain Name System — the internet's phone book that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses. When you type a URL, DNS servers resolve it to the correct server address, enabling web navigation.

A platform for building, running, and managing containerized applications. Docker packages software into standardized units called containers that include everything needed to run — code, runtime, libraries, and system tools.
A type of NoSQL database that stores data as semi-structured documents, typically in JSON or BSON format. MongoDB and CouchDB are popular document stores that offer flexible schemas.
Written text that explains how software works, how to use it, and how to develop with it. Good documentation includes API references, tutorials, guides, and architecture overviews.
Document Object Model — a programming interface for web documents that represents the page as a tree of nodes. JavaScript interacts with the DOM to dynamically update content, structure, and styling.
A specific area of knowledge or activity that a software system is designed to address. In Domain-Driven Design, the domain model represents business concepts and rules.
A human-readable address used to identify a location on the internet, such as example.com. Domain names are translated to IP addresses by the Domain Name System.

A software development approach that focuses on modeling software to match the business domain it serves. DDD introduces concepts like bounded contexts, aggregates, and ubiquitous language to bridge the gap between technical and business teams.
Denial of Service — an attack that disrupts normal functioning of a targeted server, service, or network by overwhelming it with a flood of traffic or exploiting vulnerabilities to crash the system.
A technique that allows a function to be dynamically dispatched based on the runtime types of two objects rather than one. The visitor pattern is the most common implementation of double dispatch.
A potential flaw in digital cash schemes where the same single digital token can be spent more than once. Blockchain technology was designed specifically to solve the double-spending problem.
The transfer of data from a remote server to a local device over a network. Download speeds are measured in bits per second and depend on network bandwidth and server capacity.

The process of reducing the number of samples in a dataset, either by reducing the resolution of signals or by selecting a subset of data points. Used to handle class imbalance and reduce dataset size.
A period when a system, server, or service is unavailable or not operational. Minimizing downtime is a primary goal of high availability architectures and disaster recovery planning.

Data Plane Development Kit — a set of libraries and drivers for fast packet processing that bypasses the kernel network stack. DPDK enables high-performance networking applications running in user space.

Software that allows an operating system to communicate with hardware devices. Device drivers translate generic OS commands into device-specific instructions, enabling hardware like printers, GPUs, and network cards to function.
Digital Rights Management — technologies that control access to copyrighted digital content. DRM systems restrict copying, sharing, and modification of digital media like music, video, and software.
Don't Repeat Yourself — a software development principle aimed at reducing repetition of information. DRY promotes the creation of abstractions and the elimination of redundant code.

Digital Signal Processing — the manipulation of signals such as audio, video, and sensor data using mathematical algorithms. DSP is used in telecommunications, audio engineering, and image processing.
A style of dynamic typing where an object's suitability is determined by the presence of certain methods and properties rather than its actual type. The name comes from the duck test.

An in-process SQL OLAP database management system designed for analytical query workloads. DuckDB runs embedded within applications like SQLite but is optimized for columnar storage and vectorized execution.
In database systems, the guarantee that once a transaction has been committed, it will remain so even in the event of power loss, crashes, or errors. Durability is the D in ACID properties.

Developer Experience — the overall experience developers have when using tools, APIs, SDKs, and platforms. Good DX includes clear documentation, intuitive APIs, helpful error messages, and efficient workflows.
A pricing strategy where prices are adjusted in real time based on supply, demand, competition, and other market factors. Used in e-commerce, ride-sharing, airlines, and hospitality industries.

An algorithmic technique that solves complex problems by breaking them down into simpler overlapping subproblems and storing their solutions. It optimizes recursive algorithms by eliminating redundant calculations.
A type system where type checking occurs at runtime rather than at compile time. Languages like Python, JavaScript, and Ruby use dynamic typing, offering flexibility at the cost of some type safety.

E.definition: The buying and selling of goods and services over the internet, including online retail, electronic payments, and digital marketplaces. E-commerce platforms handle product catalogs, shopping carts, and payment processing.
An evaluation strategy where expressions are evaluated as soon as they are bound to variables, as opposed to lazy evaluation where evaluation is deferred until the value is needed.
A data loading strategy where related data is fetched at the same time as the primary data, typically through JOIN queries. It avoids the N+1 query problem but may retrieve unnecessary data.
Enterprise Application Integration — the use of middleware and software technologies to integrate a set of enterprise computer applications, enabling them to share data and business processes.
The gathering of information about Earth's physical, chemical, and biological systems using remote sensing technologies including satellites, aircraft, and ground-based sensors.

Extended Berkeley Packet Filter — a technology that allows running sandboxed programs in the Linux kernel without changing kernel source code. eBPF is used for networking, security, and observability.
Elastic Block Store — a block-level storage service provided by AWS designed for use with EC2 instances. EBS volumes persist independently from the running life of an EC2 instance.
Elastic Compute Cloud — a web service from AWS that provides resizable compute capacity in the cloud. EC2 allows users to launch virtual servers with various configurations on demand.

Error-Correcting Code memory — a type of computer memory that can detect and correct common types of internal data corruption. ECC memory is used in servers and workstations where data integrity is critical.
A situation where users primarily encounter information and opinions that reinforce their existing beliefs, often amplified by algorithmic content recommendation systems in social media.

An open-source integrated development environment primarily used for Java development but extensible to other languages through plugins. Eclipse provides code editing, debugging, and project management.
The standardized specification for the scripting language that forms the basis of JavaScript. ECMAScript defines the core language features, with new editions adding capabilities annually.
The network of organizations, technologies, and tools that interact to create and deliver products or services. Technology ecosystems include developers, platforms, APIs, and user communities.
The deployment of artificial intelligence algorithms directly on edge devices such as smartphones, IoT sensors, and embedded systems rather than relying on cloud-based processing.
A problem or situation that occurs at the extreme ends of operating parameters or under unusual conditions. Handling edge cases properly is essential for robust software.

A distributed computing paradigm that processes data closer to where it's generated rather than in a centralized data center. Edge computing reduces latency, saves bandwidth, and enables real-time processing for IoT devices and applications.
A serverless function that runs at the edge of a content delivery network, close to the end user. Edge functions reduce latency by processing requests near the user rather than at a central server.
Outbound data transfer from a cloud service or network to an external destination. Egress fees are a significant cost factor in cloud computing and can influence architecture decisions.

A network security practice of monitoring and restricting outbound traffic from an internal network, preventing data exfiltration, blocking command-and-control communications, and stopping IP spoofing of external addresses.
A scalar associated with a linear transformation that indicates the factor by which a corresponding eigenvector is scaled. Eigenvalues are central to PCA, spectral clustering, and quantum computing.
Elastic Kubernetes Service — a managed Kubernetes service by AWS that simplifies running Kubernetes clusters without needing to install and operate the control plane.
A static IPv4 address designed for dynamic cloud computing in AWS. Elastic IPs can be rapidly remapped to different instances, enabling quick failover and flexible architecture.
An AWS service that automatically distributes incoming application traffic across multiple targets such as EC2 instances, containers, and IP addresses across availability zones.
The ability of a cloud system to automatically scale resources up or down based on demand. Elasticity differs from scalability in that it implies automatic and dynamic resource adjustment.

A distributed, open-source search and analytics engine built on Apache Lucene. Elasticsearch enables fast full-text search, structured search, and analytics across large volumes of data, commonly used in logging, monitoring, and site search.
A JSON-based query language for Elasticsearch that provides a full range of search capabilities including full-text search, structured search, geo queries, and aggregations.
Disturbance generated by an external source that affects an electrical circuit through electromagnetic induction, electrostatic coupling, or conduction. EMI can degrade electronic device performance.
A framework for building cross-platform desktop applications using web technologies like JavaScript, HTML, and CSS. Apps like VS Code, Slack, and Discord are built with Electron.

Executable and Linkable Format — the standard binary file format for executables, object code, shared libraries, and core dumps on Linux and Unix systems, defining sections for code, data, symbols, and relocation entries.
A functional, concurrent programming language built on the Erlang VM designed for building scalable and maintainable applications. Elixir combines Erlang's reliability with modern language features.

A collection of three open-source tools — Elasticsearch, Logstash, and Kibana — used together for searching, analyzing, and visualizing log data in real time. It is widely used for centralized logging and observability.
A mathematical curve defined by an equation of the form y squared equals x cubed plus ax plus b, used in cryptography to create smaller, faster, and more efficient cryptographic keys.
A public-key cryptography approach based on the algebraic structure of elliptic curves over finite fields. ECC offers the same security as RSA with smaller key sizes, making it more efficient.
A functional programming language that compiles to JavaScript, designed for building reliable web applications. Elm is known for having no runtime exceptions and a friendly compiler.

Electronic mail — a method of exchanging digital messages between people using email clients and servers via protocols such as SMTP, IMAP, and POP3. Email remains a fundamental communication tool in business and personal use.
A set of techniques including SPF, DKIM, and DMARC that verify the identity of email senders and prevent email spoofing, phishing, and spam by authenticating the origin of messages.
A server or service that filters inbound and outbound email traffic for spam, malware, phishing attempts, and data loss. Email gateways are a critical layer of organizational cybersecurity.
The integration of analytical capabilities directly into business applications, dashboards, and workflows rather than requiring users to switch to separate analytics tools.
A computer system designed to perform a dedicated function within a larger mechanical or electrical system. Embedded systems are found in everything from washing machines to aircraft controls.

Computer systems with dedicated functions within larger mechanical or electrical systems. They combine hardware and software designed for specific tasks, found in devices from microwaves to automotive control systems.
A learned representation that maps discrete objects like words, sentences, or images to continuous vectors in a lower-dimensional space. Embeddings capture semantic relationships and similarities.

A neural network layer that maps discrete categorical tokens — such as words, user IDs, or product codes — into dense continuous vector representations in a lower-dimensional space, capturing semantic relationships.

Dense vector representations of data such as words, sentences, or images in a continuous vector space. Embeddings capture semantic relationships and are foundational to modern NLP and recommendation systems.
Complex system behavior that arises from the interaction of simpler components, not explicitly programmed. In large language models, emergent abilities appear at certain scale thresholds.
Small digital images or icons used to express ideas, emotions, or concepts in electronic communication. Emoji are encoded in Unicode and supported across all major platforms and operating systems.
A branch of artificial intelligence that enables machines to detect, interpret, and simulate human emotions through analysis of facial expressions, voice tone, text, and physiological signals.
A design approach that considers the emotional responses of users and crafts experiences that evoke specific feelings such as delight, trust, or engagement through aesthetics and interaction.
A compiler toolchain that compiles C and C++ code into WebAssembly, enabling high-performance applications to run in web browsers without native plugins.

Software or hardware that enables one system to behave like another, allowing programs designed for one platform to run on a different one. Emulators are essential for mobile app development, retro gaming, and cross-platform testing.

An object-oriented programming principle that bundles data and the methods that operate on it within a single unit (class), restricting direct access to internal state. Encapsulation protects data integrity and reduces coupling between components.
The process of converting data from one format to another for standardized communication or storage. Common encodings include UTF-8 for text, Base64 for binary, and URL encoding for web.

The process of converting readable data (plaintext) into an unreadable format (ciphertext) using mathematical algorithms and keys. Encryption protects data in transit and at rest, with symmetric and asymmetric methods serving different security needs.
A communication system where only the communicating users can read the messages. No eavesdropper, including the service provider, can access the cryptographic keys needed to decrypt the conversation.
A testing methodology that verifies the complete flow of an application from start to finish. E2E tests simulate real user scenarios to ensure all integrated components work together correctly.

A specific URL or URI where an API can be accessed to perform operations on resources. Each endpoint represents a distinct function, such as retrieving user data or submitting a form, and responds to specific HTTP methods.
A cybersecurity technology that continuously monitors endpoint devices for suspicious activity and provides tools for investigation, containment, and remediation of threats.

A measure of how actively users interact with a product, service, or content. Engagement metrics include time spent, clicks, shares, comments, and return visits, and are key indicators of product health.
A machine learning technique that combines multiple models to produce a single optimal predictive model. Ensemble methods like bagging, boosting, and stacking often outperform individual models.

Large-scale business software systems designed to support complex organizational operations. Enterprise software typically handles processes like resource planning, customer management, and supply chain coordination.
A discipline for proactively and holistically leading enterprise responses to disruptive forces by identifying and analyzing the execution of change toward desired business vision and outcomes.
A distinct object or concept in a domain that has a unique identity and lifecycle. In domain-driven design, entities are distinguished from value objects by their identity.
A visual representation of the relationships between entities in a database. ER diagrams are used in database design to model the logical structure of data.
The process of determining whether two or more records in a dataset refer to the same real-world entity. It is essential for data integration, deduplication, and master data management.
A measure of randomness or unpredictability in data, especially important in cryptography for generating secure keys and random numbers. Higher entropy means greater security.

A reservoir of environmental randomness collected by the operating system from hardware events like disk timings, mouse movements, and interrupt jitter, used to seed cryptographically secure random number generators.
An enumeration type that defines a set of named constants representing a fixed group of possible values. Enums improve code readability and type safety by replacing magic numbers.
A configured system where software is deployed and executed, such as development, staging, and production environments. Each environment typically has different configurations and access controls.

A dynamic value stored outside of application code that configures behavior across different environments. Environment variables typically hold sensitive data like API keys, database URLs, and feature flags, keeping secrets out of source code.

A temporary container injected into a running Kubernetes Pod for interactive debugging, enabling developers to inspect processes, filesystems, and network state without modifying the Pod's original spec or restarting it.
One complete pass through the entire training dataset during the training of a machine learning model. Multiple epochs are typically needed for the model to learn patterns in the data.
The number of seconds that have elapsed since January 1, 1970 (Unix epoch), used as a standard way to represent timestamps in computing. Also known as Unix time or POSIX time.

An exploration-exploitation balancing method in reinforcement learning that selects the action with the highest estimated reward with probability 1-epsilon and a random action with probability epsilon.

A data protection method that breaks data into fragments, expands and encodes them with redundant pieces, and distributes them across storage nodes. Unlike replication, it achieves fault tolerance with significantly less storage overhead.
A functional programming language designed for building massively scalable, fault-tolerant, distributed systems. Erlang is widely used in telecommunications, messaging systems, and databases.

Enterprise Resource Planning — integrated software systems that manage and automate core business processes including finance, HR, manufacturing, supply chain, and procurement. Major ERP vendors include SAP, Oracle, and Microsoft Dynamics.
The maximum amount of time a service can be unavailable within a given period, derived from service level objectives. Error budgets balance reliability engineering with feature development velocity.

Techniques used to detect and correct errors in data transmission or storage. Error-correcting codes like Hamming codes and Reed-Solomon codes add redundancy to data to enable automatic recovery from bit errors.
A technique used in data storage and transmission that detects and corrects errors introduced during transfer or storage. ECC memory can detect and fix single-bit errors automatically.
The process of responding to and recovering from error conditions in a program. Proper error handling includes try-catch blocks, error codes, and graceful degradation strategies.
The official standard module system for JavaScript, using import and export statements to share code between files. ES Modules support static analysis, tree shaking, and asynchronous loading.

ECMAScript 2015 — a major update to the JavaScript language specification that introduced arrow functions, classes, template literals, destructuring, promises, and modules. It modernized JavaScript for large-scale development.
A compiler optimization technique that determines whether an object allocated inside a function can be accessed outside of it. Objects that do not escape can be allocated on the stack.
A static code analysis tool for identifying problematic patterns in JavaScript and TypeScript code. ESLint helps enforce coding conventions and catch potential bugs before runtime.
A programming language designed as a proof of concept, joke, or art form rather than for practical use. Examples include Brainfuck, Whitespace, and LOLCODE.
A family of low-cost microcontroller chips with integrated Wi-Fi and Bluetooth developed by Espressif Systems. ESP32 and ESP8266 are widely used in IoT projects and home automation.
A low-cost, low-power system-on-a-chip microcontroller with integrated Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, widely used in IoT projects. It supports multiple development frameworks including Arduino and MicroPython.

An HTTP response header containing a validator token that uniquely identifies a specific version of a resource, enabling conditional requests for cache validation and optimistic concurrency control in REST APIs.
A decentralized, open-source blockchain platform that enables smart contracts and decentralized applications. Ethereum's native cryptocurrency Ether is the second-largest by market capitalization.

A family of wired networking technologies commonly used in local area networks. Ethernet defines wiring and signaling standards for the physical layer and data link layer, supporting speeds from 10 Mbps to 400 Gbps.

The practice of designing and deploying artificial intelligence systems that are fair, transparent, accountable, and aligned with human values. Ethical AI addresses bias, privacy, safety, and societal impact of AI systems.

Authorized security testing where professionals attempt to find vulnerabilities in systems using the same techniques as malicious hackers. Also called penetration testing, it helps organizations improve their security posture.

Extract, Transform, Load — a data integration process that extracts data from source systems, transforms it into a suitable format, and loads it into a destination database or data warehouse. ETL pipelines are foundational to business intelligence.
