A React feature introduced in version 16.8 that lets developers use state and other React features in functional components. Common hooks include useState, useEffect, useContext, and custom hooks.

A Kubernetes resource that automatically scales the number of pods in a deployment based on observed CPU utilization or other custom metrics. HPA ensures applications handle varying workloads efficiently.

The process of adding more machines or nodes to a system to handle increased load, as opposed to vertical scaling which adds resources to existing machines. Horizontal scaling provides better fault tolerance.
A database scaling technique that distributes rows of a table across multiple database instances based on a shard key. Horizontal sharding enables handling datasets too large for a single server.
A container for DNS records that defines how traffic is routed for a domain and its subdomains. In AWS Route 53, a hosted zone stores the routing information for a registered domain.

A development feature that updates modules in a running application without requiring a full page reload. HMR significantly speeds up the development workflow by preserving application state during code changes.

A development feature that applies code changes to a running application instantly without requiring a full restart. Hot reload preserves application state, dramatically speeding up the development feedback loop in frameworks like React, Flutter, and Vite.
A section of code or a node in a system that receives a disproportionately high amount of traffic or computation. Identifying hot spots is essential for performance optimization.
A redundancy approach where the backup system runs simultaneously with the primary system and takes over immediately upon failure. Hot standby minimizes downtime compared to cold or warm standby.

An urgent software update applied to fix a critical bug or security vulnerability in a production system. Hotfixes bypass the normal release cycle to address issues that cannot wait for the next scheduled release.

Hardware Security Module — a physical computing device that safeguards and manages digital keys, performs encryption and decryption, and provides strong authentication. HSMs are used to protect cryptographic keys in enterprise environments.

HTTP Strict Transport Security — a web security policy mechanism that forces browsers to interact with websites only over HTTPS connections. HSTS prevents protocol downgrade attacks and cookie hijacking.

HyperText Markup Language — the standard markup language for creating web pages and web applications. HTML provides the structural foundation of web content using elements like headings, paragraphs, links, images, and semantic tags.
The practice of using HTML elements according to their intended meaning rather than their visual appearance. Semantic HTML improves accessibility, SEO, and code maintainability.
The fifth major revision of HTML that introduces new elements, attributes, and APIs for building modern web applications. HTML5 includes native support for audio, video, canvas, and local storage.

A JavaScript library that allows access to AJAX, CSS transitions, WebSockets, and server-sent events directly in HTML using attributes. HTMX enables building modern user interfaces with minimal JavaScript.

HyperText Transfer Protocol — the foundation of data communication on the World Wide Web. HTTP defines how messages are formatted and transmitted, with methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE for interacting with web resources.
A mechanism for storing copies of web resources to reduce server load and improve response times. HTTP caching uses headers like Cache-Control, ETag, and Last-Modified to manage cached content.
A small piece of data stored on the user's computer by a web browser while browsing a website. Cookies are used for session management, personalization, and tracking user behavior across pages.
A component of an HTTP request or response that passes additional information between the client and server. Headers carry metadata about the request, response, or the data being transferred.

Metadata fields sent with HTTP requests and responses that provide information about the data being transferred. Headers control caching, authentication, content types, security policies, and connection management.
The second major version of HTTP that introduces multiplexing, header compression, and server push to improve web performance. HTTP/2 allows multiple requests and responses over a single connection.
The third major version of HTTP built on the QUIC transport protocol instead of TCP. HTTP/3 eliminates head-of-line blocking at the transport layer and provides faster connection establishment.
HTTP Secure — an extension of HTTP that uses TLS encryption to secure communication between a web browser and a server. HTTPS protects data integrity and confidentiality during transmission.
A network topology where all traffic passes through a central hub. This architecture simplifies management but creates a single point of failure and potential bottleneck at the hub.

A lossless data compression algorithm that assigns variable-length codes to characters based on their frequency of occurrence. More frequent characters receive shorter codes, reducing overall data size.

A company and open-source platform that provides tools, models, and datasets for natural language processing and machine learning. The Hugging Face Hub hosts thousands of pre-trained models and datasets.
A fast open-source static site generator written in Go that builds websites from content files and templates. Hugo is known for its build speed and is used for blogs, documentation, and portfolios.

The process of having humans assess the quality, relevance, or correctness of AI system outputs. Human evaluation remains the gold standard for judging natural language generation and creative AI tasks.
The study and design of the interaction between people and computers. HCI encompasses usability, user experience, accessibility, and the design of interfaces that are effective and satisfying to use.
A machine learning approach that incorporates human feedback into the training or decision-making process. Human-in-the-loop systems combine the strengths of human judgment with machine efficiency.
A mobile application built using web technologies like HTML, CSS, and JavaScript but wrapped in a native container. Hybrid apps can access device features and are distributed through app stores.

A computing environment that combines public cloud services with private cloud or on-premises infrastructure. Hybrid cloud allows organizations to place workloads in the optimal location based on requirements for performance, security, compliance, and cost.
An open-source network login cracker that supports numerous protocols for authorized penetration testing. Hydra can test password strength against services like SSH, FTP, HTTP, and databases.

The process of attaching event handlers and making a server-rendered HTML page interactive on the client side. Hydration bridges the gap between server-side rendering and client-side interactivity in frameworks like React and Next.js.

A software-defined IT infrastructure that virtualizes all elements of conventional hardware-defined systems including compute, storage, and networking. HCI combines these components into a single, easily managed platform.
An extension of hypertext that includes graphics, audio, video, and interactive elements linked together in a non-linear web of information. REST APIs that use hypermedia controls are called HATEOAS.

A parameter whose value is set before the machine learning training process begins, as opposed to model parameters that are learned during training. Examples include learning rate, batch size, and number of hidden layers.
The process of finding the optimal set of hyperparameters for a machine learning model. Techniques include grid search, random search, and Bayesian optimization.

A company that provides cloud computing infrastructure at massive scale, typically operating millions of servers across global data centers. AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud are the three major hyperscalers.
Text displayed on a computer that contains links to other text, documents, or resources that the reader can immediately access. Hypertext is the foundational concept of the World Wide Web.

Software that creates and manages virtual machines by abstracting physical hardware resources. Type 1 hypervisors run directly on hardware (VMware ESXi, KVM), while Type 2 run on a host operating system (VirtualBox, Parallels).
A statistical method for making decisions about population parameters based on sample data. Hypothesis testing uses null and alternative hypotheses, test statistics, and p-values to draw conclusions.

A Netflix library that implements the circuit breaker pattern for latency and fault tolerance in distributed systems. Hystrix isolates points of access to remote systems and prevents cascading failures.

I.definition: Input/Output — the communication between a computer system and the outside world, including reading from and writing to files, network sockets, and peripheral devices. I/O operations are often a performance bottleneck.

Infrastructure as a Service — a cloud computing model that provides virtualized computing resources over the internet. Users manage operating systems, storage, and applications while the provider manages the underlying infrastructure.

Identity and Access Management — a framework of policies and technologies for ensuring that the right people have appropriate access to technology resources. Cloud IAM services manage user identities, roles, and permissions.
A document in AWS Identity and Access Management that defines permissions for actions on resources. IAM policies are attached to users, groups, or roles to control access to cloud services.
An Interactive Connectivity Establishment candidate representing a potential network path for WebRTC peer-to-peer communication. ICE candidates include host, server-reflexive, and relay addresses.
A font that contains symbols and glyphs instead of letters and numbers, used to display scalable vector icons on web pages. Font Awesome and Material Icons are popular icon font libraries.

Integrated Development Environment — a software application that provides comprehensive tools for software development including a code editor, debugger, compiler, and build automation. Popular IDEs include VS Code, IntelliJ IDEA, and Xcode.

The creative process of generating, developing, and communicating new ideas for solving problems. In design thinking, ideation sessions use brainstorming, sketching, and prototyping to explore solution spaces.

A property of certain operations whereby they can be applied multiple times without changing the result beyond the initial application. Idempotent APIs ensure that duplicate requests do not cause unintended side effects.
A unique key sent with an API request to ensure that retrying the same operation produces the same result. Idempotent keys prevent duplicate actions when network issues cause request retries.
A name given to a program element such as a variable, function, class, or module. Identifiers follow naming rules specific to each programming language and should be descriptive and consistent.

A system of trust between multiple security domains that enables users to authenticate once and access resources across those domains. Federation eliminates the need for separate credentials per system.

The organizational process for managing digital identities throughout their lifecycle, including creation, maintenance, and deactivation. Identity management systems handle user provisioning, authentication, and access control.
A system that creates, maintains, and manages identity information and provides authentication services to relying applications. Examples include Okta, Auth0, and Azure Active Directory.
Intrusion Detection System — a device or software application that monitors a network or systems for malicious activity or policy violations. IDS types include network-based and host-based systems.

The IEEE standard for floating-point arithmetic that defines formats for representing real numbers in binary, including single and double precision. It specifies rounding rules, special values like NaN and infinity, and exception handling.

An inline frame HTML element that embeds another HTML page within the current page. Iframes are used for embedding third-party content, ads, and sandboxed widgets while maintaining isolation from the parent page.
Immediately Invoked Function Expression — a JavaScript design pattern that executes a function as soon as it is defined. IIFEs create a private scope and avoid polluting the global namespace.

A visual representation of data stored in digital format, using formats like JPEG, PNG, GIF, and WebP. Digital images consist of pixels and are used throughout computing for interfaces, media, and communication.

The task of automatically generating a textual description of an image using computer vision and natural language processing. Image captioning models combine CNNs for visual features with RNNs or transformers for text.

A computer vision task that involves categorizing images into predefined classes based on their visual content. Convolutional neural networks have achieved superhuman performance on many image classification benchmarks.
Techniques for reducing the file size of digital images while maintaining acceptable visual quality. Compression can be lossless (PNG) or lossy (JPEG), trading file size for image fidelity.

A standardized way to organize and store digital image data. Common formats include JPEG for photographs, PNG for lossless graphics, SVG for vectors, WebP for web optimization, and GIF for animations.

The manipulation and analysis of digital images using algorithms for tasks like filtering, enhancement, segmentation, and recognition. Image processing is fundamental to computer vision and medical imaging.
The ability of software to identify objects, places, people, or other elements within digital images. It uses deep learning models trained on large datasets to recognize visual patterns.
A storage and distribution system for container images, allowing developers to push, pull, and manage versioned container images. Docker Hub and Amazon ECR are popular image registries.
A computer vision technique that partitions an image into multiple segments or regions, each corresponding to different objects or parts of objects. It provides pixel-level understanding of images.
A single image file containing multiple smaller images arranged in a grid, used to reduce the number of HTTP requests. CSS sprites improve web page loading performance by batching image downloads.

A programming concept where data cannot be modified after creation. Instead of changing existing values, new values are created. Immutability prevents unexpected side effects, simplifies debugging, and is fundamental to functional programming and state management.
A property of data that cannot be changed after it is created. Immutable objects simplify concurrent programming, enable safe sharing between threads, and make program state easier to reason about.

A data structure whose state cannot be modified after it is created. Any modification produces a new data structure, which helps prevent bugs in concurrent programs and simplifies reasoning about code.
An approach where servers are never modified after deployment. Instead of updating existing servers, new servers are built from a common image and replace the old ones entirely.
A log storage mechanism where entries cannot be modified or deleted after creation. Immutable logs provide tamper-evident audit trails essential for compliance and forensic investigation.
An object whose state cannot be changed after creation. Immutable objects are inherently thread-safe and simplify program reasoning, making them valuable in concurrent and functional programming.

The process of assessing the potential consequences of a change to a system, project, or process. Impact analysis helps stakeholders understand risks and make informed decisions about proposed changes.
The conceptual difficulty of mapping between object-oriented programming models and relational database schemas. Object-relational impedance mismatch drives the need for ORM frameworks.
A programming paradigm that uses statements to change a program's state through sequences of commands. Languages like C, Java, and Python support imperative programming.

An OAuth 2.0 authorization flow designed for browser-based applications where the access token is returned directly to the client. It has been largely deprecated in favor of the authorization code flow with PKCE.

An OAuth 2.0 authorization flow designed for single-page applications where the access token is returned directly in the URL fragment. It has been largely superseded by the authorization code flow with PKCE.
A statement or mechanism that brings code from external modules or packages into the current scope. Import systems manage dependencies and enable modular code organization across files and libraries.

A situation where two or more modules depend on each other, creating a circular dependency that can cause compilation errors or runtime issues. Breaking import cycles requires restructuring code architecture.
A browser feature that allows developers to control the behavior of JavaScript imports by mapping module specifiers to URLs. Import maps enable using bare module specifiers without a build step.
The process of replacing missing data with substituted values using statistical methods. Common imputation techniques include mean imputation, regression imputation, and multiple imputation.
A transaction made within a mobile application that provides additional content or functionality. In-app purchases are a primary revenue model for freemium mobile apps and games.
A computing approach that stores data in RAM across a distributed cluster of servers rather than on disk. It enables real-time data processing and analytics at extremely high speeds.
A database management system that primarily relies on main memory for data storage rather than disk-based storage. In-memory databases like Redis and Memcached provide extremely fast data access.

A business methodology that attracts customers by creating valuable content and experiences tailored to them. Unlike outbound marketing, inbound marketing forms connections through content marketing, social media, and SEO.

The process of identifying, analyzing, and resolving incidents that disrupt normal service operations. Effective incident management minimizes business impact and restores services as quickly as possible.

A structured approach to handling and managing security breaches or cyberattacks. Incident response plans define roles, communication protocols, and step-by-step procedures for detection, containment, eradication, and recovery.

A classification system that categorizes incidents by their impact on users and business operations. Severity levels typically range from SEV-1 (critical) to SEV-4 (minor) and determine response protocols.
A build strategy that only recompiles the parts of a project that have changed since the last build. Incremental builds dramatically reduce compilation times in large projects.

A machine learning approach where the model is continuously updated as new data becomes available rather than being retrained from scratch. Incremental learning is useful for evolving data distributions.

A data structure that improves the speed of data retrieval operations on a database table at the cost of additional storage space and slower writes. Proper indexing is critical for database performance.
A database operation that retrieves rows by reading an index structure rather than scanning the entire table. Index scans are faster than full table scans for queries that match indexed columns.

A low-level browser API for storing significant amounts of structured data on the client side. IndexedDB is a transactional database system that supports indexes, cursors, and asynchronous operations for offline web applications.

The process of creating data structures that improve the speed of data retrieval operations in databases and search engines. Indexes work like a book's index, allowing systems to find specific records without scanning every entry.
A piece of forensic data that identifies potentially malicious activity on a system or network, such as unusual network traffic, unexpected file changes, or known malware signatures.
The process of using a trained machine learning model to make predictions on new, unseen data. Inference can run on CPUs, GPUs, or specialized hardware and is optimized differently from training.
A web design technique where content loads continuously as the user scrolls down the page, eliminating the need for pagination. Infinite scroll is common in social media feeds and content discovery.
A moment of significant change in the progress of a company, product, or technology where the trajectory shifts dramatically. Inflection points often determine whether a business scales or stagnates.
The structural design of shared information environments, focusing on organizing, structuring, and labeling content effectively. It helps users find information and complete tasks efficiently.
A measure of the uncertainty or randomness in a set of data, originally defined in information theory by Claude Shannon. It quantifies the expected amount of information contained in a message.
A measure used in decision tree algorithms to determine which feature provides the most useful information for classifying data. It is based on the concept of entropy from information theory.
A large, visible display that presents key project metrics and status to all team members in real time. Information radiators like dashboards and Kanban boards promote transparency in agile teams.
The science of searching for information in documents, searching for documents themselves, and searching for metadata that describes documents. Search engines are the most common application.

The practice of protecting information by mitigating risks related to unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction. It encompasses both digital and physical information.

A mathematical framework for quantifying information, developed by Claude Shannon. Information theory defines concepts like entropy, mutual information, and channel capacity, underpinning data compression and communication.

The practice of managing and provisioning computing infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files rather than manual processes. IaC tools like Terraform, Pulumi, and CloudFormation enable version-controlled, repeatable infrastructure deployments.

The gradual divergence between the actual state of infrastructure and its intended state as defined in code. Infrastructure drift can cause inconsistencies and is detected by tools like Terraform plan.
The practice of collecting, analyzing, and alerting on metrics from servers, containers, networks, and other infrastructure components. Tools like Prometheus, Datadog, and Nagios are widely used.

An object-oriented programming mechanism where a new class derives properties and methods from an existing class. Inheritance promotes code reuse and establishes a hierarchical relationship between classes.

A specialized container in Kubernetes that runs before the main application containers and must complete successfully before they start. Init containers handle setup tasks like database migrations or configuration.

A class of security vulnerabilities where an attacker supplies untrusted data that is interpreted as commands or queries. Common types include SQL injection, command injection, and cross-site scripting.
A class of security vulnerabilities where untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query. SQL injection, command injection, and XSS are common forms of injection attacks.
The practice of embedding assembly language instructions within higher-level language source code. Inline assembly allows performance-critical code sections to be optimized at the hardware level.
An optimization technique used in dynamic language runtimes that caches the result of method lookups at call sites. Inline caches significantly improve the performance of dynamically typed languages.
A SQL join operation that returns only the rows where there is a match in both tables being joined. Inner joins are the most common type of join used in relational database queries.
The use of open source software development best practices and the establishment of an open source-like culture within an organization. It promotes collaboration across teams and code reuse.

The process of creating new ideas, products, or methods that deliver value to users and organizations. Innovation in technology drives competitive advantage through new capabilities and improved processes.

A hardware peripheral that provides data and control signals to a computer, such as keyboards, mice, touchscreens, microphones, and cameras. Input devices translate human actions into digital signals.
The delay between a user action such as a keypress or mouse click and the corresponding response on screen. Minimizing input lag is critical for gaming, real-time applications, and responsive UIs.

A sequence of data elements made available over time for reading by a program. Input streams abstract data sources like files, network connections, and standard input into a uniform reading interface.
The process of ensuring that user-supplied data meets expected criteria before processing. Proper input validation prevents injection attacks, buffer overflows, and other security vulnerabilities.
A concrete occurrence of an object created from a class in object-oriented programming. Each instance has its own set of attribute values while sharing the methods defined in its class.

A technique used in CPU design where multiple instruction phases are overlapped in execution. Pipelining increases instruction throughput by allowing the CPU to work on several instructions simultaneously.
The complete collection of machine language instructions that a processor can execute. Instruction set architectures like x86 and ARM define the interface between software and hardware.
The abstract model of a computer that defines the supported data types, registers, memory management, and instruction set. ISA serves as the interface between hardware and software.
The process of adding code to an application to collect performance data, metrics, and traces at runtime. Proper instrumentation is foundational for observability and performance monitoring.

A data type representing whole numbers without fractional components. Integer types vary in size (8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit, 64-bit) and can be signed or unsigned, affecting their range of representable values.

A condition where an arithmetic operation produces a value that exceeds the maximum size the integer type can hold, wrapping around to an unexpected value. Integer overflows can cause security vulnerabilities.
The process of combining software components or systems to work together as a unified whole. Integration involves connecting APIs, databases, and services, and is tested through integration testing.
A middleware system that facilitates communication between different applications and services in an enterprise environment. It provides message routing, transformation, and protocol translation.

A software testing phase where individual modules or services are combined and tested as a group. Integration tests verify that components work correctly together, catching interface defects and interaction problems that unit tests cannot detect.

The assurance that data has not been altered or tampered with in an unauthorized manner. Data integrity is one of the three pillars of the CIA triad and is maintained through checksums, hashing, and digital signatures.
Legal rights that result from intellectual activity in the industrial, scientific, literary, and artistic fields. In technology, IP includes patents, copyrights, trademarks, and trade secrets.

The combination of artificial intelligence and automation technologies to streamline and optimize business processes. It goes beyond simple rule-based automation by incorporating learning and decision-making capabilities.
The use of AI technologies including OCR, NLP, and machine learning to automatically extract and process information from documents. IDP automates workflows involving invoices, forms, and contracts.

IntelliJ IDEA — an integrated development environment by JetBrains primarily for Java and Kotlin development. It provides intelligent code completion, refactoring tools, debugging, and built-in version control.
The process of identifying the purpose or goal behind a user's input in natural language processing systems. It is a core component of chatbots and virtual assistants.
A synchronization mechanism that coordinates access to shared resources between separate processes rather than threads. Inter-process locks use system-level primitives like file locks or semaphores.

The design of interactive products and systems focusing on how users interact with them. Interaction design defines behaviors, animations, transitions, and feedback that make interfaces intuitive and engaging.
A Git feature that allows developers to modify commits in various ways such as reordering, squashing, editing, or dropping commits. It helps maintain a clean and meaningful commit history.

A contract that defines a set of methods and properties that a class must implement, without specifying how they work. Interfaces enable polymorphism, dependency injection, and loose coupling in object-oriented and statically-typed languages.
A SOLID principle stating that no client should be forced to depend on methods it does not use. It promotes creating smaller, more focused interfaces rather than large monolithic ones.

A self-service layer built on top of infrastructure tooling that enables developers to independently provision and manage resources. It standardizes workflows and reduces cognitive load on development teams.

The process of designing software so it can be adapted to different languages and regions without engineering changes. Often abbreviated as i18n, it involves separating text from code and supporting various character sets.

A global network of interconnected computer networks that use the TCP/IP protocol suite to communicate. The internet provides infrastructure for the World Wide Web, email, file transfer, and countless other services.
A horizontally scaled, redundant, and highly available VPC component that allows communication between a virtual private cloud and the internet. It supports IPv4 and IPv6 traffic.

A network of physical devices embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity that enables them to collect and exchange data. IoT applications span smart homes, healthcare, agriculture, and industrial monitoring.
The principal communications protocol in the Internet protocol suite for relaying datagrams across network boundaries. IP addresses serve as unique identifiers for devices on a network.
The ability of different systems, devices, or applications to connect, communicate, and work together effectively. Standards and protocols enable interoperability across diverse technology platforms.

The degree to which a human can understand the cause of a machine learning model's decisions. Interpretable models help build trust, satisfy regulatory requirements, and enable debugging of AI systems.

A program that directly executes source code line by line without prior compilation to machine code. Interpreted languages like Python and Ruby offer faster development cycles and portability, though typically with slower execution than compiled languages.

A signal to the processor from hardware or software indicating an event that needs immediate attention. Interrupts allow the CPU to respond to asynchronous events without continuously polling for changes.

A browser API that provides a way to asynchronously observe changes in the intersection of a target element with an ancestor element or the viewport. It is used for lazy loading, infinite scrolling, and visibility tracking.
The ability of a program to examine its own structure, types, and properties at runtime. Many programming languages support introspection through reflection APIs.

The process of monitoring network traffic or system activities for signs of unauthorized access or malicious activity. Intrusion detection systems use signatures, anomaly detection, and behavioral analysis.
A device or software application that monitors a network or systems for malicious activity or policy violations. IDS can be network-based or host-based and uses signature or anomaly detection.
A network security tool that monitors network traffic and takes automated actions to block or prevent detected threats. IPS sits inline with traffic flow and can actively stop malicious packets.
Software used to track inventory levels, orders, sales, and deliveries. Modern inventory systems integrate with ERP, e-commerce platforms, and supply chain management tools.

A design principle where the flow of control is inverted compared to traditional programming. Instead of the programmer controlling the flow, a framework calls into custom code, enabling loose coupling.
A data structure that maps content to its location in a database or document, enabling fast full-text searches. Search engines use inverted indexes as their core data structure.

Apple's mobile operating system that powers iPhones and iPads. iOS provides a sandboxed app environment, consistent user experience, and extensive developer APIs through frameworks like UIKit and SwiftUI.

Internet of Things — the network of physical devices embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity that enables them to collect and exchange data. IoT spans smart homes, industrial sensors, wearables, and connected vehicles.

Computing infrastructure deployed at or near IoT devices that processes data locally before sending it to the cloud. Edge computing for IoT reduces latency, bandwidth usage, and improves real-time decision-making.
A physical device or software program that connects IoT sensors and devices to cloud platforms and applications. It performs protocol translation, data filtering, and local processing.
Communication protocols designed for the constraints of IoT devices, including MQTT, CoAP, and AMQP. These protocols optimize for low bandwidth, limited processing power, and unreliable networks.

Internet Protocol — the principal communications protocol for relaying datagrams across network boundaries. IP provides addressing and routing functions that enable internetworking and establish the internet.

Internet Protocol Address — a unique numerical label assigned to every device connected to a computer network. IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses (like 192.168.1.1), while IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses to support the growing number of connected devices.
A technique used in cyberattacks where the attacker sends packets with a forged source IP address to disguise their identity. IP spoofing is used in DDoS attacks and man-in-the-middle attacks.
Inter-Process Communication — mechanisms that allow processes to communicate and synchronize their actions. Common IPC methods include pipes, message queues, shared memory, and sockets.

InterPlanetary File System — a peer-to-peer distributed file system that connects all computing devices with the same system of files. IPFS uses content-addressed storage to create a permanent and decentralized web.

Internet Protocol Security — a suite of protocols that secures Internet Protocol communications by authenticating and encrypting each IP packet in a data stream. IPsec is widely used in VPN implementations.
The most recent version of the Internet Protocol that uses 128-bit addresses, providing a vastly larger address space than IPv4. IPv6 was designed to address the exhaustion of IPv4 addresses.
An international standard for managing information security that provides a framework for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an information security management system.

The principle of keeping components or processes separated so that failures or changes in one do not affect others. Isolation is applied in databases (ACID), containers, testing, and security contexts.

An anomaly detection algorithm that isolates observations by randomly selecting a feature and then randomly selecting a split value. Anomalies are easier to isolate and thus have shorter path lengths in the tree.

A database setting that defines how transaction integrity is visible to other concurrent transactions. The four standard levels are Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, and Serializable.

A method of visual representation that uses a form of parallel projection where the three coordinate axes appear equally foreshortened. Isometric design is popular in UI illustrations and game graphics.
A web application where the same code can run on both the server and the client. This approach improves initial page load times, SEO, and provides a seamless user experience.
Internet Service Provider — a company that provides customers with access to the internet and related services. ISPs operate network infrastructure including routers, switches, and DNS servers.

A software tool for recording, tracking, and managing bugs, feature requests, and tasks throughout the development lifecycle. Issue trackers like Jira, GitHub Issues, and Linear help teams organize and prioritize work.
The process of managing and maintaining lists of issues such as bugs, feature requests, and tasks in a software project. Tools like Jira, GitHub Issues, and Linear are widely used for issue tracking.

An open-source service mesh that provides a uniform way to connect, secure, control, and observe microservices. Istio handles traffic management, security, and observability without requiring code changes.
The framework that ensures IT investments support business objectives while managing risks. It encompasses policies, processes, and structures that guide decision-making about technology resources.
The practice of designing, delivering, managing, and improving the IT services an organization provides to its end users. ITSM frameworks like ITIL standardize service delivery processes.

A comprehensive plan that outlines how technology should be used to meet business goals and objectives. IT strategy aligns technology investments with organizational priorities and competitive requirements.
The repetition of a process or set of instructions in a computer program, typically implemented with loops. Iteration is fundamental to algorithms that process collections of data.

A software development approach where the project is divided into small iterations, each producing a working version of the software. Each iteration incorporates feedback and builds upon previous work.

An object that enables traversal of a container, particularly lists and other collections, by providing a standard interface for accessing elements sequentially. Iterators abstract the details of the underlying data structure.

Information Technology Infrastructure Library — a set of detailed practices for IT service management that focuses on aligning IT services with business needs. ITIL provides a framework for governance and lifecycle management.

J.definition: A statistic used for comparing the similarity of two sets, defined as the size of the intersection divided by the size of the union. It is widely used in text mining, recommendation systems, and clustering.
A high-performance JSON processor for Java that provides data binding between JSON and Java objects. Jackson is the default JSON serialization library in Spring Boot.
An open-source distributed tracing system originally developed by Uber Technologies. Jaeger is used for monitoring and troubleshooting microservices-based distributed systems.
An array of arrays where each inner array can have a different length, unlike rectangular multidimensional arrays. Jagged arrays are memory-efficient when rows have varying sizes.

The process of exploiting vulnerabilities to remove software restrictions imposed by the manufacturer on devices like smartphones. In AI, jailbreaking refers to circumventing safety guardrails on language models.

A web architecture based on JavaScript, APIs, and Markup that decouples the frontend from the backend. JAMstack sites are pre-rendered as static files and enhanced with JavaScript and APIs, offering superior performance, security, and scalability.

Visible stuttering or frame drops in user interface animations caused by the browser missing its rendering deadline. Jank degrades user experience and is typically caused by long-running JavaScript or layout thrashing.
Java Archive — a package file format used to aggregate many Java class files, associated metadata, and resources into one file for distribution. JAR files are based on the ZIP format.

A class-based, object-oriented programming language designed for portability across platforms through its write once, run anywhere philosophy. Java is widely used in enterprise applications, Android development, and web services.
Metadata that provides data about a program but is not part of the program itself. Java annotations are used for compiler instructions, build-time processing, and runtime processing by frameworks.

The intermediate representation of Java source code compiled by the Java compiler. Bytecode runs on the Java Virtual Machine, enabling platform-independent execution across different operating systems.

The Java platform's built-in support for concurrent programming through threads, locks, executors, and concurrent collections. The java.util.concurrent package provides high-level concurrency utilities.

Java Platform Enterprise Edition — a set of specifications extending Java SE with enterprise features such as distributed computing, web services, and servlet technology. It has been succeeded by Jakarta EE.

A low-overhead profiling and diagnostics framework built into the JVM that collects runtime information about Java applications. JFR captures events like garbage collection, thread activity, and I/O without significant performance impact.
A feature of the Java language that allows type parameters to be used in class, interface, and method definitions. Generics enable type-safe collections and eliminate the need for casting.

A specification that describes how threads in the Java language interact through memory and what behaviors are allowed in concurrent execution. It defines the rules for visibility and ordering of variables.

An immutable data carrier class introduced in Java 14 that automatically generates constructors, accessors, equals, hashCode, and toString methods. Records reduce boilerplate for simple data-holding classes.
A Java component that allows applications to implement a security policy controlling access to system resources. It restricts untrusted code from performing potentially dangerous operations.
A Java API introduced in Java 8 that supports functional-style operations on sequences of elements. Streams enable declarative data processing through operations like map, filter, and reduce.

An abstract computing machine that enables a computer to run Java programs and programs compiled to Java bytecode. The JVM provides platform independence and manages memory through garbage collection.
A reusable software component model for Java that follows specific conventions including a no-argument constructor, serializable interface, and getter/setter methods for properties.
A software platform for creating desktop applications and rich internet applications using Java. JavaFX provides a modern UI toolkit with CSS styling, FXML layout, and hardware-accelerated graphics.

A versatile, high-level programming language that powers interactive web pages, server-side applications, and mobile apps. Originally created for browsers, JavaScript has become the world's most widely used programming language through Node.js and modern frameworks.

A function that retains access to variables from its enclosing scope even after the outer function has returned. Closures are fundamental to JavaScript patterns like module encapsulation and callbacks.
A program that executes JavaScript code, typically found in web browsers. V8, SpiderMonkey, and JavaScriptCore are major engines that compile JavaScript to machine code for fast execution.
The mechanism that allows JavaScript to perform non-blocking operations despite being single-threaded. The event loop continuously checks the call stack and task queues to execute pending callbacks.
A mechanism for splitting JavaScript programs into separate files that can be imported when needed. ES modules use import/export syntax and are natively supported by modern browsers.
A built-in JavaScript object that enables creation of a proxy for another object, intercepting and redefining fundamental operations like property lookup, assignment, and function invocation.
An environment that provides the facilities needed to execute JavaScript code, including a JavaScript engine, event loop, and APIs. Node.js, Deno, and Bun are popular server-side runtimes.
A primitive data type introduced in ES6 that creates unique, immutable identifiers. Symbols are used as property keys to avoid naming collisions and to define well-known behaviors like iteration.

A collection of key-value pairs where the keys must be objects and are held weakly, allowing garbage collection if no other references exist. WeakMaps are useful for private data and DOM node metadata.
A graphical monitoring tool built into the JDK that uses JMX to provide information about the performance and resource consumption of Java applications, including memory usage and thread activity.
Java Database Connectivity — an API for the Java programming language that defines how a client may access a database. JDBC provides methods for querying and updating data in a database.
Java Development Kit — a software development environment used for developing Java applications. It includes the JRE, compiler, debugger, and other tools needed for Java development.

An open-source automation server widely used for building, testing, and deploying software through CI/CD pipelines. Jenkins supports hundreds of plugins and can orchestrate complex build workflows, though newer alternatives like GitHub Actions are gaining popularity.
A suite of Jenkins plugins that supports implementing and integrating continuous delivery pipelines as code. Jenkins pipelines define the build, test, and deployment workflow in a Jenkinsfile.
A JavaScript testing framework maintained by Meta that focuses on simplicity and support for large web applications. Jest provides built-in code coverage, mocking, and snapshot testing.
A software company known for creating integrated development environments including IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, and WebStorm. JetBrains tools are widely used for Java, Python, and web development.
A universal artifact repository manager that supports all major package formats and build tools. Artifactory manages binary artifacts throughout the software development lifecycle.
A modern and designer-friendly templating engine for Python, modeled after Django's templates. Jinja is widely used in Flask web applications and Ansible configuration management.
A modern and designer-friendly templating engine for Python, used by frameworks like Flask and Ansible. Jinja2 supports template inheritance, sandboxed execution, and automatic HTML escaping.
A project management and issue tracking tool developed by Atlassian, widely used in agile software development. JIRA supports Scrum, Kanban, and custom workflows for managing projects.

Just-In-Time compilation — a technique that compiles code at runtime rather than ahead of time, combining the flexibility of interpretation with the performance of compilation. JIT is used in the Java Virtual Machine, V8 JavaScript engine, and .NET CLR.

A runtime compiler that converts bytecode or intermediate code into native machine code during program execution. JIT compilers combine the portability of interpretation with the speed of ahead-of-time compilation.
Just-In-Time provisioning — the automatic creation of user accounts in a system when a user first authenticates through an identity provider. It eliminates the need for manual account creation.
The variation in the delay of received packets in a network, causing inconsistency in data delivery. High jitter degrades the quality of real-time applications like video calls and online gaming.
Apache JMeter — an open-source load testing tool designed to measure performance and test the functional behavior of web applications. It simulates heavy loads on servers to analyze performance.

Java Message Service — a messaging standard that allows Java application components to create, send, receive, and read messages. JMS supports both point-to-point and publish-subscribe messaging patterns.
Java Management Extensions — a Java technology that provides tools for managing and monitoring applications, system objects, devices, and service-oriented networks. JMX is used for JVM monitoring.

Java Naming and Directory Interface — a Java API for directory services that allows Java clients to discover and look up data and objects via a name. JNDI provides a standard interface for diverse naming systems.
A file or set of files produced by a CI/CD job that can be downloaded, passed to subsequent jobs, or archived. Job artifacts include build outputs, test reports, and deployment packages.
A relationship between tasks where one job must complete before another can begin. Managing job dependencies correctly is essential for building reliable data pipelines and CI/CD workflows.
Mechanisms and strategies for handling and recovering from failed batch jobs or automated tasks. Recovery approaches include retry logic, dead letter queues, and checkpoint-based resumption.
The alignment between a product or service and the needs of its target market. Achieving job market fit means the product effectively solves a problem that enough customers are willing to pay for.
The automated coordination of multiple jobs and workflows across systems. Orchestration tools manage dependencies between tasks, handle failures, and ensure jobs run in the correct order.

A data structure that holds tasks or jobs waiting to be processed by workers, enabling asynchronous task execution. Job queues decouple task submission from processing, handle load spikes, and ensure reliable task completion in distributed systems.

A mechanism that automatically re-executes failed jobs based on configurable retry policies including delay intervals and maximum attempts. Job retry improves reliability of batch processing and CI/CD pipelines.
The process of allocating system resources to tasks in a specific order and time. Job schedulers like cron, Airflow, and Kubernetes CronJobs automate the execution of recurring tasks.

A technique for distributing a large computational job across multiple workers or partitions for parallel processing. Job sharding improves throughput and reduces processing time for data-intensive operations.

A SQL operation that combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column. Join types include INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL OUTER, each producing different result sets based on matching criteria between tables.
An intermediary database table used to implement many-to-many relationships between two entities. The join table contains foreign keys referencing both related tables.
A JSON to JSON transformation library that specifies transformations using a JSON document. Jolt is used in data pipelines to restructure JSON data without writing custom code.
JSON Object Signing and Encryption — a framework for securely transferring claims between parties using JSON-based data structures. JOSE encompasses JWS, JWE, JWK, and JWA specifications.

A visualization of the process that a person goes through to accomplish a goal, documenting their experience across touchpoints. Journey maps help identify pain points and opportunities for improvement.
A philosophy emphasizing that programming should be an enjoyable and creative endeavor. Languages like Ruby and Kotlin were explicitly designed with developer happiness as a core principle.

Java Persistence API — a Java specification for managing relational data in applications. JPA provides an object-relational mapping approach that lets developers work with databases using Java objects.

Joint Photographic Experts Group — a commonly used method of lossy compression for digital images. JPEG balances image quality and file size, making it the most popular format for photographs on the web.
Java Persistence Query Language — a platform-independent object-oriented query language defined as part of JPA. JPQL queries operate on entity objects rather than directly on database tables.

A lightweight command-line JSON processor for parsing, filtering, and transforming JSON data. jq uses a domain-specific language for querying and manipulating JSON structures in shell scripts and data pipelines.

A fast, small, and feature-rich JavaScript library that simplifies HTML document traversal, event handling, and Ajax interactions. While less dominant than in its peak, jQuery remains widely deployed.
Java Runtime Environment — the minimum environment required to execute a Java application, consisting of the JVM, core classes, and supporting files. It does not include development tools.
A markup language used to annotate JavaScript source code with documentation comments. JSDoc comments are processed by documentation generators to produce API documentation.

JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight, human-readable data interchange format used extensively in web APIs and configuration files. JSON represents data as key-value pairs and arrays, making it the de facto standard for data exchange between web services.
A specification for building APIs in JSON that defines how clients should request resources and how servers should respond. It reduces the number of requests and the amount of data transmitted.
A text format where each line is a valid JSON value, also known as NDJSON. JSON Lines is convenient for storing structured data that can be processed one record at a time.
An RFC 7396 standard for partially updating JSON documents by sending only the changed fields. JSON Merge Patch is simpler than JSON Patch but cannot explicitly set values to null.
A format for describing changes to a JSON document using a series of operations such as add, remove, replace, move, copy, and test. It enables partial updates to resources in REST APIs.
A query language for extracting data from JSON documents, similar to XPath for XML. JSONPath expressions navigate through elements and arrays using dot notation and bracket syntax.

A vocabulary for annotating and validating the structure of JSON data. JSON Schema defines the expected format, types, and constraints of JSON documents, enabling automated validation, documentation generation, and code generation.
Techniques for processing JSON data incrementally as it arrives rather than waiting for the complete document. Streaming parsers reduce memory usage and enable processing of large JSON files.
A standard for encrypting content using JSON-based data structures. JWE provides confidentiality for the claims contained in a token, unlike JWS which only provides integrity.
A compact, URL-safe token format that represents claims to be transferred between two parties. JWTs are widely used for authentication and information exchange in web applications.
JSON for Linking Data — a method of encoding linked data using JSON format. JSON-LD is used to implement structured data markup on websites for search engine optimization.
A remote procedure call protocol encoded in JSON. JSON-RPC allows calling methods on remote servers using simple request and response JSON objects with minimal overhead.
An extension to JSON that aims to make it easier for humans to write and maintain by allowing comments, trailing commas, single-quoted strings, and other features inspired by ECMAScript 5.
A binary JSON data type in PostgreSQL that stores JSON data in a decomposed binary format. JSONB allows efficient querying and indexing of JSON documents within a relational database.
JSON with Padding — a historical technique for requesting data from a server in a different domain, bypassing same-origin policy restrictions. JSONP has been largely replaced by CORS.
JavaServer Pages — a technology for developing web pages that support dynamic content using special JSP tags. JSP pages are compiled into servlets and run on a Java application server.
Java Specification Request — the formal document used to propose additions and changes to the Java platform. JSRs go through the Java Community Process for review and approval.
A syntax extension for JavaScript used in React that allows writing HTML-like code within JavaScript. JSX makes it easier to create and visualize component structures in React applications.
Joint Test Action Group — an industry standard for testing and debugging printed circuit boards and integrated circuits. JTAG provides access to chip internals through a standardized interface.

A type of cyberattack where compromised public USB charging stations are used to install malware or steal data from connected devices. Users can protect themselves by using AC outlets or data-blocking adapters.

A high-level, high-performance programming language designed for technical computing. Julia combines the ease of use of Python with performance approaching C, making it popular for scientific and numerical computing.
A hardened and monitored server used to access and manage devices in a separate security zone. Jump servers provide a controlled means of access between different network segments.

A data structure of machine code addresses used to efficiently transfer program control to another part of a program based on an index value. Jump tables are commonly used to implement switch statements.
A widely used unit testing framework for Java applications. JUnit provides annotations and assertions for writing and running repeatable automated tests, and integrates with all major IDEs.

An open-source web application for creating and sharing documents that contain live code, equations, visualizations, and narrative text. Jupyter notebooks support over 40 programming languages and are widely used in data science.

A multi-user server for Jupyter notebooks that allows organizations to serve notebooks to multiple users. JupyterHub manages authentication, spawning individual notebook servers, and resource allocation.

An open-source web application for creating documents that combine live code, equations, visualizations, and narrative text. Jupyter notebooks are the standard tool for data science exploration, supporting Python, R, Julia, and other languages.
A security model where privileged access is granted only when needed and automatically revoked after a defined period. JIT access reduces the attack surface by minimizing standing privileges.
A production strategy that aligns raw material orders with production schedules to reduce inventory costs. In software, it refers to delivering features and updates incrementally as needed.
A production methodology aimed at reducing flow times within production systems and response times from suppliers. In software, it inspires just-in-time architecture decisions and lean development.

Java Virtual Machine — an abstract computing machine that enables Java programs to run on any platform. The JVM interprets compiled Java bytecode and provides memory management, garbage collection, and security.

The automatic memory management process in the Java Virtual Machine that identifies and reclaims memory occupied by objects that are no longer referenced. Different GC algorithms optimize for throughput or latency.
Programming languages that compile to Java bytecode and run on the Java Virtual Machine. Notable JVM languages include Kotlin, Scala, Clojure, and Groovy.

The process of adjusting JVM parameters to optimize application performance, including heap size, garbage collection strategy, and thread pool configuration. Proper tuning is critical for Java applications.

JSON Web Key — a JSON data structure that represents a cryptographic key used in JSON Web Tokens and related standards. JWK sets provide a standard way to share public keys for signature verification.

JSON Web Token — a compact, URL-safe token format for securely transmitting information between parties as a JSON object. JWTs are widely used for authentication and authorization, containing encoded claims that can be verified and trusted.

Pieces of information asserted about a subject encoded in a JSON Web Token. Standard claims include issuer, expiration time, subject, and audience, while custom claims carry application-specific data.
A token used to obtain a new access token when the current one expires without requiring the user to re-authenticate. Refresh tokens are stored securely and have a longer lifespan than access tokens.
A Java library for generating Excel reports by populating predefined Excel templates with data. JXLS simplifies the creation of complex Excel documents with formatting, formulas, and charts.

A privacy model that ensures each record in a dataset is indistinguishable from at least k-1 other records with respect to certain identifying attributes. It protects against re-identification attacks.
Apache Kafka — a distributed event streaming platform capable of handling trillions of events per day. Kafka is used for building real-time data pipelines and streaming applications.
A lean method for managing and improving work across human systems. Kanban uses visual boards with columns representing workflow stages to visualize work, limit work in progress, and maximize flow.

K.definition: A privacy-preserving technique where each record in a dataset is indistinguishable from at least k-1 other records with respect to certain identifying attributes. It helps prevent re-identification of individuals.

A k-dimensional binary search tree that partitions space by alternating split dimensions at each level, enabling efficient nearest-neighbor searches, range queries, and spatial lookups in O(log n) average time.
A model validation technique that divides the dataset into k equal parts, using each part as a test set while training on the remaining k-1 parts. It provides a robust estimate of model performance.

A popular unsupervised clustering algorithm that partitions data into K groups by iteratively assigning points to the nearest cluster center and recalculating centers. K-Means is computationally efficient and widely used for customer segmentation and data exploration.

An unsupervised machine learning algorithm that partitions data into K clusters by iteratively assigning points to the nearest centroid and updating centroids. It is widely used for customer segmentation and image compression.

A simple supervised machine learning algorithm that classifies a data point based on the majority class of its K closest neighbors in the feature space. KNN is used for both classification and regression tasks.
An algorithm that merges K sorted sequences into a single sorted sequence using a min-heap. K-way merge is used in external sorting, merge sort variants, and database query processing.
An open-source load testing tool built for developer happiness that uses JavaScript for writing test scripts. K6 is used for performance testing APIs, microservices, and websites.

A distributed hash table protocol used in peer-to-peer networks that locates nodes and data using XOR-based distance metrics, requiring only O(log n) routing hops. It powers BitTorrent's DHT and IPFS.
Apache Kafka — a distributed event streaming platform capable of handling trillions of events per day. It is used for building real-time data pipelines and streaming applications.
A framework for streaming data between Apache Kafka and other data systems such as databases, key-value stores, and file systems. It simplifies building and managing large-scale data pipelines.

A set of consumers that cooperate to consume messages from Kafka topics, with each partition assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. Consumer groups enable parallel processing and load balancing.
An ordered, immutable sequence of records within a Kafka topic that enables parallel processing. Each partition is replicated across brokers for fault tolerance and can be consumed independently.
A client library for building real-time streaming applications and microservices that process data stored in Apache Kafka. It supports stateful operations like windowed aggregations and joins.
An online community and platform for data science competitions, datasets, and notebooks. Kaggle hosts machine learning challenges and provides a collaborative environment for data scientists.
A Japanese philosophy of continuous improvement applied to business processes and manufacturing. In software development, Kaizen encourages teams to make small, incremental improvements regularly.

A visual workflow management method that uses boards and cards to track work items through defined stages. Kanban limits work-in-progress, visualizes bottlenecks, and promotes continuous delivery without the time-boxed iterations of Scrum.
A visual tool for managing work that displays tasks as cards moving through columns representing different stages of a process. Digital implementations include Trello, Jira, and Azure Boards.

A data processing architecture that simplifies Lambda Architecture by using a single stream processing layer for both real-time and batch computation, treating all data as an immutable append-only log.
A method of simplifying Boolean algebra expressions using a visual diagram. Karnaugh maps are used in digital circuit design to minimize the number of logic gates required.
A coding exercise designed to improve programming skills through practice and repetition. Code katas help developers refine their problem-solving abilities and learn new techniques.

An open-source Python framework for creating reproducible, maintainable, and modular data science code. Kedro provides a project template and pipeline abstraction for production-ready machine learning workflows.

An HTTP mechanism that reuses a single TCP connection for multiple requests and responses, avoiding the overhead of establishing new connections. Keep-alive connections reduce latency and server load.

A high-level neural network API written in Python that runs on top of TensorFlow, providing a user-friendly interface for building and training deep learning models. It emphasizes rapid experimentation.

A network authentication protocol that uses secret-key cryptography and a trusted third party to authenticate client-server applications. It is the default authentication protocol in Windows Active Directory.

The core component of an operating system that manages system resources and provides the lowest-level abstraction for hardware. The kernel handles memory management, process scheduling, device drivers, and system calls.

A networking technique that allows user-space applications to send and receive packets directly without traversing the kernel's network stack, dramatically reducing latency and increasing throughput for high-frequency trading and NFV.
A non-parametric method for estimating the probability density function of a continuous random variable. KDE is used in data visualization and statistical analysis to smooth distributions.
A piece of code that can be loaded into or removed from the kernel at runtime without rebooting the system. Kernel modules extend the functionality of the OS, such as adding device drivers.
A safety measure taken by an operating system's kernel upon detecting a fatal internal error from which it cannot safely recover. It is the Unix/Linux equivalent of the Windows Blue Screen of Death.

The protected memory area where the operating system kernel and its modules execute with full hardware access privileges. Kernel space is separated from user space to prevent applications from crashing the system.
A technique in machine learning that enables algorithms to operate in a high-dimensional feature space without explicitly computing the coordinates. It is fundamental to support vector machines.

A formal procedure for generating, distributing, or destroying cryptographic keys under strict security controls with multiple authorized participants. Key ceremonies are used for root certificate creation and HSM initialization.
A cryptographic function that derives one or more secret keys from a master key, password, or passphrase. KDFs like PBKDF2, bcrypt, and Argon2 are used for secure password storage.

The administration of cryptographic keys throughout their lifecycle, including generation, storage, distribution, rotation, and destruction. Proper key management is essential for maintaining the security of encrypted data.
A system that manages the creation, storage, distribution, and retirement of cryptographic keys. Cloud providers like AWS KMS and Azure Key Vault offer managed key management solutions.
A set of two cryptographic keys — a public key and a private key — used in asymmetric encryption. The public key encrypts data that only the corresponding private key can decrypt.
A measurable value that demonstrates how effectively an organization is achieving key business objectives. KPIs are used at multiple levels to evaluate success at reaching targets.
The practice of periodically replacing cryptographic keys with new ones to limit the exposure if a key is compromised. Key rotation is a security best practice enforced by compliance frameworks.

A technique used to make a weak key, such as a short password, more secure by increasing the time needed to test each possible key. It is implemented through iterated hashing or key derivation functions.

A type of NoSQL database that stores data as a collection of key-value pairs, where each unique key maps to a value. Key-value stores like Redis and DynamoDB offer extremely fast lookups and are ideal for caching, session management, and real-time data.

An input device consisting of keys used to enter text, numbers, and commands into a computer. Modern keyboards include mechanical, membrane, and virtual variants, each with different key switch mechanisms.
A combination of keys pressed simultaneously to trigger a specific command or action in software. Well-designed keyboard shortcuts improve productivity and accessibility for power users.

Key combinations that trigger specific actions in software applications, enabling faster interaction than using menus or buttons. Well-designed keyboard shortcuts improve productivity and accessibility for power users.

An open-source identity and access management solution that provides single sign-on, identity brokering, and social login capabilities. It supports standard protocols like OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0, and SAML.
An animation technique where the designer specifies key states (keyframes) and the software interpolates the frames in between. CSS keyframe animations enable complex visual effects on the web.

A type of surveillance software or hardware that records keystrokes made by a user. While sometimes used legitimately for monitoring, keyloggers are frequently deployed as malware to steal credentials.

A secure repository for storing cryptographic keys, certificates, and sensitive credentials. Keystores protect encryption keys from unauthorized access and are used in SSL/TLS configurations, code signing, and application security.
The automated process of identifying the most relevant words or phrases in a document that best describe its content. Keyword extraction uses techniques like TF-IDF, RAKE, and transformer models.

An open-source data visualization and exploration tool for Elasticsearch data. Kibana provides dashboards, charts, maps, and machine learning features for analyzing log data, monitoring infrastructure, and exploring large datasets in real time.

A cybersecurity model that describes the stages of a cyberattack from reconnaissance to data exfiltration. The Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain framework helps organizations identify and prevent intrusions.
A safety mechanism that immediately shuts down a system, feature, or process in case of emergency. In software, kill switches can disable features remotely without deploying new code.
Kubernetes IN Docker — a tool for running local Kubernetes clusters using Docker containers as nodes. Kind is primarily used for testing Kubernetes itself and for local development workflows.
Amazon Kinesis — a platform on AWS for collecting, processing, and analyzing real-time streaming data at any scale. It enables developers to build applications that process data as it arrives.
An AWS service that processes and analyzes streaming data in real time using SQL or Apache Flink. Kinesis Analytics enables building real-time dashboards, metrics, and anomaly detection.
An AWS service for loading streaming data into data stores and analytics services. It can capture, transform, and deliver streaming data to Amazon S3, Redshift, Elasticsearch, and Splunk.

An AWS service for real-time data streaming that continuously captures gigabytes of data per second from hundreds of thousands of sources. It enables real-time analytics and event-driven applications.
An animation technique that combines motion and text to express ideas and evoke emotions. It is used in video titles, advertisements, and interactive web experiences.
Keep It Simple, Stupid — a design principle stating that most systems work best when they are kept simple rather than made complex. KISS encourages avoiding unnecessary complexity in software design.

A unary operator in formal language theory and regular expressions denoting zero or more repetitions of the preceding element. The Kleene closure of a set S, written S*, includes the empty string and all finite concatenations.

A classic combinatorial optimization problem where given a set of items with weights and values, the goal is to determine which items to include in a collection so the total weight does not exceed a limit and the total value is maximized.

A graph structure where each node is connected to its k nearest neighbors in a feature space, used for approximate nearest neighbor search, manifold learning, community detection, and semi-supervised classification.
A method of filling in missing values by using the values of the k-nearest neighbors in feature space. KNN imputation considers the similarity between data points to estimate missing entries.
A centralized repository for information that is organized, searchable, and easily accessible. Knowledge bases are used for customer support, internal documentation, and AI-powered retrieval systems.
A self-service document that provides information about a product, service, or process to help users solve problems independently. Knowledge base articles reduce support ticket volume.

The process of transforming a knowledge representation into a form that supports efficient query answering. Compiled representations like decision diagrams trade preprocessing time for faster inference.

The process of extracting useful knowledge from large volumes of data through methods including data mining, machine learning, and statistical analysis. It encompasses the entire data analysis pipeline.

A model compression technique where a smaller student model is trained to replicate the behavior of a larger teacher model. It enables deployment of efficient models that retain much of the teacher's accuracy.

A structured representation of real-world entities and their relationships, stored as a network of interconnected nodes and edges. Knowledge graphs power search engines, recommendation systems, and AI assistants by encoding semantic relationships between concepts.
The process of creating, sharing, using, and managing knowledge and information within an organization. Effective knowledge management improves decision-making and reduces redundant work.

The field of AI concerned with how to formally represent information about the world so that computers can use it to solve complex tasks. It encompasses ontologies, frames, semantic networks, and logic.

The process of finding and extracting relevant information from large knowledge bases or document collections. Retrieval-augmented generation combines knowledge retrieval with language models for improved accuracy.
The systematic process of sharing expertise, skills, and information from one part of an organization to another. It ensures continuity when team members change roles or leave the organization.
An efficient string-matching algorithm that searches for occurrences of a pattern within a text by exploiting information about previous matches. KMP achieves linear time complexity.

The minimum length of a program that produces a given output string, measuring the string's inherent computational complexity. It provides a theoretical foundation for data compression and randomness.

An open-source API gateway and microservices management layer that provides load balancing, authentication, rate limiting, and analytics. Kong is built on NGINX and supports plugin-based extensibility.

Kubernetes Operations — a tool for deploying, upgrading, and managing production-grade Kubernetes clusters on cloud infrastructure. Kops automates the provisioning of compute, networking, and storage.

A modern, concise programming language developed by JetBrains that runs on the Java Virtual Machine. Kotlin is the preferred language for Android development and offers null safety, coroutines, and expressive syntax while maintaining full Java interoperability.
A Kotlin feature for writing asynchronous, non-blocking code in a sequential style. Coroutines simplify concurrent programming by providing structured concurrency primitives.

A Kotlin feature that allows sharing code between multiple platforms including JVM, JavaScript, iOS, and native targets. It enables writing business logic once and sharing it across mobile and web applications.

Key Performance Indicator — a measurable value that demonstrates how effectively an organization or individual is achieving key business objectives. KPIs vary by industry and role, from revenue growth and customer retention to deployment frequency and uptime.
A visual display that presents key performance indicators in an easily digestible format using charts, graphs, and gauges. Dashboards enable stakeholders to monitor business health at a glance.
Kusto Query Language — a read-only query language used in Azure Data Explorer and Azure Monitor for exploring data. KQL provides rich filtering, aggregation, and visualization capabilities.
A greedy algorithm for finding the minimum spanning tree of a connected weighted graph. It works by sorting edges by weight and adding them to the tree if they do not create a cycle.

An asynchronous web framework built by JetBrains in Kotlin using coroutines, designed for building connected applications including HTTP servers, clients, WebSocket services, and microservices with a lightweight plugin architecture.
