A network proxy that runs on each node in a Kubernetes cluster and maintains network rules for pod communication. It enables service discovery and load balancing within the cluster.
The command-line tool for interacting with Kubernetes clusters. It allows users to deploy applications, inspect and manage cluster resources, and view logs.
An open-source machine learning platform built on Kubernetes that makes it easy to deploy, scale, and manage ML workflows. It provides tools for model training, serving, and pipeline orchestration.

The primary node agent in Kubernetes that runs on each node and ensures containers are running in pods. It takes a set of pod specifications and ensures the containers described are healthy and running.

An open-source container orchestration platform that automates the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. Originally developed by Google, Kubernetes (K8s) has become the industry standard for running containers in production.

A piece of code that intercepts requests to the Kubernetes API server before object persistence but after authentication and authorization. Admission controllers enforce policies on cluster resources.
A set of nodes that run containerized applications managed by Kubernetes. A cluster consists of a control plane that manages the cluster and worker nodes that run the application workloads.
An API object used to store non-confidential configuration data in key-value pairs. ConfigMaps allow decoupling environment-specific configuration from container images.
Custom Resource Definition — a way to extend the Kubernetes API by defining new resource types. CRDs allow users to create custom objects that Kubernetes manages like built-in resources.
A workload controller that ensures a copy of a pod runs on all or selected nodes in a Kubernetes cluster. DaemonSets are used for cluster-wide services like log collection and monitoring agents.
A distributed, reliable key-value store used as the backing store for all Kubernetes cluster data. etcd stores configuration data, state, and metadata that the cluster components rely on.

A mechanism that prevents deletion of a Kubernetes resource until specific cleanup operations are completed. Finalizers ensure that dependent resources are properly handled before an object is removed.
A package manager for Kubernetes that simplifies deploying and managing applications on Kubernetes clusters. Helm uses charts — pre-configured packages of Kubernetes resources.
Horizontal Pod Autoscaler — a Kubernetes resource that automatically scales the number of pods in a deployment based on observed CPU utilization or custom metrics.
An API object that manages external access to services in a Kubernetes cluster, typically HTTP and HTTPS. Ingress provides load balancing, SSL termination, and name-based virtual hosting.
The process management system within Kubernetes containers responsible for reaping zombie processes and forwarding signals. Using a proper init system like tini prevents resource leaks in containers.
A workload resource that creates one or more pods and ensures that a specified number of them successfully terminate. Jobs are used for batch processing and one-time computational tasks.

A configuration management tool built into kubectl that lets you customize Kubernetes YAML configurations without using templates. Kustomize uses overlays to modify base configurations for different environments.

A diagnostic check performed by the kubelet to determine if a container is still running. If a liveness probe fails, the kubelet kills the container and it is restarted according to its restart policy.
A mechanism for isolating groups of resources within a single Kubernetes cluster. Namespaces provide a scope for names and can be used to divide cluster resources between multiple teams or projects.
A specification for how groups of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network policies provide fine-grained control over pod-level network traffic.

A method of packaging, deploying, and managing a Kubernetes application using custom resources and controllers. Operators encode operational knowledge to automate complex application lifecycle management.
A piece of storage in a Kubernetes cluster that has been provisioned by an administrator or dynamically provisioned using storage classes. PersistentVolumes decouple storage from pod lifecycle.
The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, consisting of one or more containers that share storage, network, and a specification for how to run. Pods are ephemeral by design.

Role-Based Access Control in Kubernetes — a method of regulating access to cluster resources based on the roles of individual users. RBAC policies define what actions users can perform on which resources.
A diagnostic check that determines whether a container is ready to accept traffic. Unlike liveness probes, a failing readiness probe removes the pod from service endpoints without killing it.
A deployment strategy in Kubernetes that incrementally replaces old pod instances with new ones. Rolling updates allow deployments to take place with zero downtime by updating a few pods at a time.
An object that stores sensitive information such as passwords, tokens, and keys in a Kubernetes cluster. Secrets are base64-encoded and can be mounted as volumes or exposed as environment variables.
An abstract way to expose an application running on a set of pods as a network service. Services provide stable endpoints for pod-to-pod communication even as pods are created and destroyed.

An infrastructure layer that manages service-to-service communication within a Kubernetes cluster using sidecar proxies. Service meshes like Istio and Linkerd provide traffic management and observability.
A container that runs alongside the main application container in a Kubernetes pod. Sidecars handle cross-cutting concerns like logging, monitoring, proxying, and configuration management.
A workload API object for managing stateful applications in Kubernetes. StatefulSets provide guarantees about the ordering and uniqueness of pods, essential for databases and distributed systems.

A property applied to a Kubernetes node that repels pods from being scheduled on it unless the pod has a matching toleration. Taints and tolerations work together to ensure pods are not scheduled on inappropriate nodes.

An abstraction that allows data to persist beyond the lifetime of a container and enables data sharing between containers in a pod. Kubernetes supports many volume types including local, NFS, and cloud storage.
Vertical Pod Autoscaler — a Kubernetes component that automatically adjusts CPU and memory resource requests for pods based on observed usage. VPA complements HPA by right-sizing individual pods.

A key-value data store provided by edge computing platforms like Cloudflare Workers for storing persistent data at the edge. KV namespaces provide low-latency reads for globally distributed applications.

Kernel-based Virtual Machine — a virtualization module in the Linux kernel that turns the kernel into a hypervisor. KVM allows running multiple virtual machines with unmodified Linux or Windows images.

A post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism selected by NIST as a standard for quantum-resistant cryptography. Kyber is based on the learning with errors problem and is designed to replace RSA and ECC.

L.definition: The fastest and smallest cache memory located directly on the CPU die. L1 cache provides single-cycle access to frequently used data and instructions, significantly reducing memory latency.
A text element in a user interface that identifies or describes an associated form field, button, or other interactive element. Proper labeling is essential for usability and accessibility.

A technique that converts categorical variables into numerical values by assigning each unique category an integer label. Label encoding is simpler than one-hot encoding but may imply an ordinal relationship.

An anonymous function — a small, unnamed function defined inline without a formal function declaration. Lambda expressions are used in functional programming, callbacks, and collection operations across languages like Python, JavaScript, Java, and C#.

A data-processing architecture designed to handle massive quantities of data by using both batch and stream processing methods. It provides comprehensive and accurate views of batch data while using real-time stream processing.
A formal system in mathematical logic for expressing computation based on function abstraction and application. It forms the foundation of functional programming languages and type theory.

An anonymous function defined without a name, commonly used for short, throwaway functions in languages like Python, JavaScript, and Java. Lambda functions enable concise functional-style programming.
A reusable package of code and dependencies that can be shared across multiple AWS Lambda functions. Lambda layers reduce deployment package size and enable code sharing between serverless functions.

A logical clock mechanism for ordering events in a distributed system without relying on synchronized physical clocks. Lamport timestamps assign a counter to each event to establish a partial ordering of events.

Local Area Network — a computer network that interconnects computers within a limited area such as a home, office, or building. LANs use Ethernet or Wi-Fi and provide high-speed, low-latency connectivity.

A framework for developing applications powered by large language models that provides tools for prompt management, chains, agents, and memory. It simplifies building complex LLM-powered applications.

A framework built on LangChain for building stateful, multi-agent applications using large language models. LangGraph models agent workflows as graphs with nodes representing actions and edges representing transitions.

A developer platform for debugging, testing, evaluating, and monitoring LLM applications built with LangChain. LangSmith provides tracing, dataset management, and evaluation tools for AI application development.

A probabilistic model that assigns probabilities to sequences of words and can generate text. Large language models like GPT and Claude are trained on vast amounts of text data and power modern AI assistants.

An open protocol that defines the communication between a code editor and a language server that provides features like auto-completion, go-to-definition, and error checking. LSP enables language support across editors.

A PHP web application framework known for its elegant syntax and comprehensive feature set including routing, authentication, ORM, and templating. Laravel follows the MVC pattern and emphasizes developer productivity.

A neural network trained on vast amounts of text data that can generate, understand, and manipulate human language. LLMs like GPT-4, Claude, and Llama power chatbots, coding assistants, and content generation tools.
The final step of the delivery process where a product moves from a transportation hub to its final destination. In technology, it refers to connecting end users to network infrastructure.

A conflict resolution strategy in distributed systems where the most recent write operation overwrites any concurrent conflicting writes. LWW is simple but can cause data loss in high-concurrency scenarios.

The time delay between a request and its response, typically measured in milliseconds. Low latency is critical for real-time applications, gaming, video calls, and financial trading where even small delays significantly impact user experience.
A compressed representation of data learned by a model, where similar data points are mapped to nearby points in the space. Latent spaces are central to autoencoders, GANs, and variational methods.

The technique used by attackers to progressively move through a network after initial access, searching for sensitive data and high-value assets. Detection of lateral movement is key to preventing data breaches.

A scaling approach that distributes workload across multiple independent systems rather than scaling up a single system. Lateral scaling provides resilience through redundancy and geographic distribution.

A document preparation system widely used in academia and scientific publishing for producing high-quality typeset documents. LaTeX excels at rendering mathematical equations, bibliographies, and complex layouts.

A design guideline for developing software that states each unit should have limited knowledge about other units. Also known as the principle of least knowledge, it promotes loose coupling between components.

A theorem stating that the average of results from a large number of trials converges to the expected value as the number of trials increases. It underpins statistical sampling and Monte Carlo methods.
A normalization technique applied across the features of a single training example rather than across the batch. Layer normalization is preferred in transformer architectures and recurrent networks.
A software architecture pattern that organizes components into horizontal layers, each providing services to the layer above it. Common layers include presentation, business logic, and data access.

The arrangement of visual elements on a page or screen, including spacing, alignment, and hierarchy. Layout design uses grids, flexbox, and other systems to create organized and responsive interfaces.
An unexpected movement of visible page content that occurs when elements are dynamically resized or inserted. Cumulative Layout Shift is a Core Web Vital metric that measures visual stability.

An evaluation strategy that delays the computation of an expression until its value is needed. Lazy evaluation can improve performance by avoiding unnecessary calculations and supporting infinite data structures.
A design pattern that defers initialization of an object or loading of a resource until it is actually needed. On the web, lazy loading images and components improves initial page load performance.

Lightweight Directory Access Protocol — an open, vendor-neutral protocol for accessing and maintaining distributed directory information services over a network. It is commonly used for authentication and user management.

An algorithm that designates a single node as the leader responsible for coordinating tasks in a distributed system. Leader election protocols like Raft and Paxos ensure consensus in the face of failures.
An open-source memory leak detection library for Android applications. LeakCanary automatically detects memory leaks during development and provides detailed reports of the leak trace.

A methodology for maximizing customer value while minimizing waste in product development and business processes. Lean principles emphasize continuous improvement, eliminating non-value-adding activities, and rapid iteration.

A software development methodology adapted from lean manufacturing principles that focuses on eliminating waste, delivering value quickly, and continuous improvement. It emphasizes efficiency and customer value.

A methodology for developing businesses and products that emphasizes rapid iteration, validated learning, and build-measure-learn feedback loops. It encourages testing hypotheses with minimum viable products.
The rate at which a user gains proficiency with a new tool, interface, or system over time. Designing for a gentle learning curve improves adoption and reduces training costs.

A security principle that requires giving users and processes only the minimum level of access necessary to perform their tasks. It limits the potential damage from accidents, errors, or unauthorized use.
A cache eviction policy that removes the item that has not been accessed for the longest time when the cache reaches capacity. LRU is one of the most common and effective caching strategies.

A database that provides a transparent, immutable, and cryptographically verifiable transaction log. AWS QLDB and Azure Confidential Ledger are examples used for audit trails and regulatory compliance.

An outdated computing system or application that continues to be used because it still serves business needs. Legacy systems pose challenges for maintenance, integration, and security but contain valuable business logic.

A natural language processing technique that reduces words to their base or dictionary form called a lemma. Unlike stemming, lemmatization considers the context and part of speech to produce valid words.
A tool for managing JavaScript projects with multiple packages in a single repository, known as monorepos. Lerna optimizes the workflow around managing multi-package repositories with git and npm.

A project structure managed by Lerna where multiple related JavaScript packages coexist in a single repository. This approach simplifies dependency management and enables atomic cross-package changes.
A monorepo management tool that optimizes the workflow of managing multi-package JavaScript projects. Lerna handles versioning, publishing, and dependency linking across packages in a monorepo.
A CSS pre-processor that extends CSS with features like variables, mixins, functions, and nested rules. Less compiles into standard CSS and helps organize and maintain large stylesheets.
A free, automated, and open certificate authority that provides TLS certificates to enable HTTPS on websites. It has dramatically increased the adoption of encrypted web traffic.

A technique in 3D graphics that reduces the complexity of rendered objects as they move farther from the camera. LOD improves rendering performance while maintaining visual quality where it matters most.

A string metric that measures the minimum number of single-character edits — insertions, deletions, or substitutions — required to change one word into another. It is used in spell checkers and fuzzy matching.

A program that performs lexical analysis by converting a sequence of characters into a sequence of tokens. The lexer is the first stage of a compiler or interpreter, categorizing input into meaningful symbols.

The process of converting a sequence of characters into a sequence of tokens during compilation. The lexer or tokenizer is the first phase of a compiler that breaks source code into meaningful symbols.
An acronym for Looks Good To Me used in code reviews to indicate approval of proposed changes. LGTM signifies that the reviewer has examined the code and finds it acceptable for merging.
A collection of pre-written code that developers can use to perform common tasks, avoiding the need to write code from scratch. Libraries provide reusable functions, classes, and modules.

A legal instrument governing the use, redistribution, and modification of software. Common software licenses include MIT, Apache 2.0, GPL, and proprietary licenses, each with different rights and obligations.

Light Detection and Ranging — a remote sensing technology that measures distances by illuminating a target with laser light and analyzing the reflected light. It is used in autonomous vehicles and mapping.
A set of 3D data points generated by a LIDAR sensor that captures the spatial coordinates of surfaces and objects. Point clouds are used in autonomous vehicles, surveying, and geospatial mapping.

The series of stages a software component goes through from creation to destruction. In frameworks like React, Vue, and Android, lifecycle methods allow developers to execute code at specific stages such as initialization, rendering, updating, and cleanup.
A callback function that allows developers to execute code at specific stages of a component's lifecycle in frameworks like Angular, React, and Vue. Common hooks include mount, update, and destroy.
A method that is automatically called at specific stages of a component's existence in frameworks like React and Vue. Lifecycle methods handle mounting, updating, unmounting, and error handling.

A migration strategy that involves moving an application and its associated data to the cloud with minimal or no changes. While quick to implement, it may not fully leverage cloud-native capabilities.
A metric in predictive modeling that measures how much better a model performs compared to random selection. A lift of 2.0 means the model is twice as effective as random chance.
An open-source automated tool by Google for improving the quality of web pages. It audits performance, accessibility, progressive web apps, SEO, and provides actionable recommendations.

A thread managed by the runtime environment rather than the operating system, offering lower overhead and higher scalability. Green threads, goroutines, and virtual threads are examples of lightweight threads.

The branch of mathematics dealing with vectors, matrices, and linear transformations. It is fundamental to machine learning, computer graphics, and data science for representing and manipulating data.

A collision resolution technique in hash tables where, upon collision, the algorithm checks the next sequential slot until an empty one is found. Linear probing is simple but can suffer from clustering.

A statistical method that models the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables using a straight line. It is one of the most basic and widely used predictive analysis techniques.
A technique for combining multiple network connections in parallel to increase throughput beyond what a single connection could sustain. It also provides redundancy in case one link fails.

A compiler optimization technique that performs interprocedural analysis and optimization across all translation units at link time. LTO enables optimizations that are impossible within a single source file.
A linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes, each pointing to the next node. Unlike arrays, linked lists allow efficient insertion and deletion but do not support random access.
A program that combines object files generated by a compiler into a single executable program. The linker resolves symbol references between modules and handles library dependencies.
A specific code quality check defined in a linter's configuration that flags patterns considered errors or bad practices. Teams customize lint rules to enforce consistent coding standards.
A static code analysis tool that flags programming errors, bugs, stylistic errors, and suspicious constructs. Popular linters include ESLint for JavaScript, Pylint for Python, and RuboCop for Ruby.

The automated analysis of source code to flag programming errors, bugs, stylistic inconsistencies, and suspicious constructs. Linters like ESLint, Pylint, and RuboCop enforce coding standards and catch issues before code reaches production.

An open-source, Unix-like operating system kernel that powers the majority of servers, cloud infrastructure, and Android devices worldwide. Linux distributions like Ubuntu, Fedora, and Arch provide complete operating systems for diverse computing needs.

An operating-system-level virtualization method that allows running multiple isolated Linux systems on a single host. LXC containers share the host kernel but have isolated process and network spaces.
A complete operating system built on the Linux kernel that includes system utilities, libraries, and application software. Popular distributions include Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, and Arch Linux.
A data optimization technique in data lakehouse platforms that automatically organizes data for optimal query performance without requiring manual partitioning or file compaction.
A template language created by Shopify for rendering dynamic content in web pages. Liquid is safe, stateless, and widely used in e-commerce platforms and static site generators like Jekyll.

The L in SOLID — a principle stating that objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of its subclasses without affecting the correctness of the program. It ensures proper inheritance hierarchies.
A type that represents a specific, exact value rather than a general type category. In TypeScript, literal types allow variables to hold only specific string, number, or boolean values.

The process of moving a running virtual machine or container from one physical host to another without downtime. Live migration enables hardware maintenance and load balancing without service interruption.
A development feature that automatically refreshes the browser or application when source code files are modified. It speeds up the development feedback loop by eliminating manual refresh cycles.

The practice of managing and updating a live software product or service after launch, including content updates, events, and real-time monitoring. It is especially common in game development and SaaS.
A family of open-source large language models developed by Meta AI. Llama models are available for research and commercial use, enabling widespread experimentation with and deployment of LLMs.

The process of further training a pre-trained large language model on a specific dataset to adapt it for a particular task or domain. Fine-tuning requires less data and compute than training from scratch.

A phenomenon where a large language model generates text that sounds plausible but is factually incorrect or fabricated. Hallucinations are a significant challenge in deploying LLMs for production applications.

A device or service that distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server bears too much load. Load balancers improve application availability, reliability, and performance through intelligent traffic distribution.

The process of distributing network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed. Load balancers improve application availability, reliability, and performance through various distribution algorithms.
The ratio of the number of stored entries to the total number of slots in a hash table. When the load factor exceeds a threshold, the hash table is resized to maintain performance.
A technique where a system intentionally drops some requests during periods of extreme load to maintain performance for remaining requests. It prevents cascading failures in distributed systems.
A type of performance testing that evaluates how a system behaves under expected and peak load conditions. It identifies performance bottlenecks before they affect end users in production.

A web browser feature that allows websites to store key-value pairs persistently on the user's device. Unlike cookies, local storage data is not sent with HTTP requests and has a larger storage capacity.
The tendency of a processor to access the same set of memory locations repetitively over a short period. Temporal and spatial locality are exploited by caching systems to improve performance.
The process of adapting software for a specific region or language, including translating text, adjusting date and number formats, and handling right-to-left scripts. Often abbreviated as l10n.

Technologies and APIs that determine the geographic position of a device using GPS, cell towers, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth signals. Location services power mapping, navigation, and location-based applications.
A synchronization mechanism for enforcing limits on access to a shared resource in a concurrent environment. Locks prevent race conditions but can lead to deadlocks if not managed carefully.

A file that records the exact versions of all dependencies installed in a project, ensuring consistent installations across environments. Examples include package-lock.json, yarn.lock, and Gemfile.lock.

A concurrent programming approach where threads make progress without using mutual exclusion locks. Lock-free algorithms use atomic operations like compare-and-swap to ensure thread safety without blocking.
A file that records the exact versions of all dependencies installed in a project, ensuring reproducible builds. Examples include package-lock.json for npm and Pipfile.lock for Python.

The process of collecting, normalizing, and consolidating log data from multiple sources into a centralized location. Tools like the ELK Stack, Splunk, and Datadog facilitate log aggregation at scale.

The practice of collecting, storing, analyzing, and retaining log data generated by applications and infrastructure. Log management tools help with debugging, compliance, security analysis, and operational monitoring.

A data structure used in databases that provides high write throughput by sequentially writing data to append-only logs before merging into sorted structures. It powers databases like RocksDB and Cassandra.

A widely used Java-based logging framework that provides flexible and configurable logging capabilities. The Log4Shell vulnerability in December 2021 highlighted the critical importance of supply chain security.

The practice of recording events, errors, and diagnostic information generated by software applications and systems. Structured logging with tools like ELK Stack or Datadog provides essential visibility for debugging, monitoring, and security analysis.
A classification of log messages by severity such as DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, and FATAL. Logging levels allow developers to control the verbosity of log output for different environments.
A fundamental building block of digital circuits that performs a basic logical operation on one or more binary inputs to produce a single output. Common gates include AND, OR, NOT, NAND, and XOR.

A programming paradigm based on formal logic where programs are written as a set of logical statements. Prolog is the most well-known logic programming language, used in AI and computational linguistics.

A statistical model used for binary classification that predicts the probability of an outcome using a logistic function. Despite its name, it is a classification algorithm rather than a regression method.

The detailed organization and implementation of complex operations related to the movement and storage of goods. In technology, logistics software optimizes supply chains, warehousing, and delivery routes.

A web communication technique where the client makes an HTTP request and the server holds the connection open until new data is available. It provides near-real-time updates without the complexity of WebSockets.

A type of recurrent neural network architecture designed to learn long-term dependencies in sequential data. LSTMs use gate mechanisms to control information flow and avoid the vanishing gradient problem.
A classic dynamic programming problem that finds the longest subsequence common to two sequences. It is used in version control diff algorithms and bioinformatics sequence alignment.

A parsing technique where the parser examines upcoming tokens in the input without consuming them to make parsing decisions. LL and LR parsers use different amounts of lookahead to resolve grammar ambiguities.
A business intelligence and data analytics platform acquired by Google Cloud that allows users to explore, share, and visualize data. Looker uses its own modeling language called LookML.
A control flow statement that repeatedly executes a block of code while a condition is true or for a specified number of iterations. Common loop types include for, while, and do-while loops.
A design principle where components have minimal dependencies on each other, making the system more modular and easier to maintain. Loosely coupled systems are more resilient to change.

Low-Rank Adaptation — a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that adds small trainable matrices to frozen pre-trained model weights. LoRA dramatically reduces the memory and compute needed for model fine-tuning.

A mathematical function that measures the difference between a model's predicted output and the actual target value. Minimizing the loss function during training is how machine learning models improve.
A class of data compression algorithms that allows the original data to be perfectly reconstructed from the compressed data. Formats like ZIP, PNG, and FLAC use lossless compression.
A class of data compression methods that reduces file size by permanently eliminating less important information. JPEG, MP3, and H.264 are common lossy compression formats.

An open-source animation file format and library that renders After Effects animations in real time on mobile and web. Lottie files are small, scalable, and interactive, making them ideal for UI animations.

A software development approach that uses visual interfaces and pre-built components to create applications with minimal hand-coded programming. Low-code platforms like Retool, Bubble, and Power Apps democratize development for non-technical users.
A software development approach that requires little to no coding to build applications and processes. Low-code platforms use visual development environments with drag-and-drop components.

A characteristic of a system or network that experiences minimal delay in processing or transmitting data. Low-latency systems are essential for high-frequency trading, gaming, and real-time communications.

Least Recently Used Cache — a caching strategy that evicts the least recently accessed items when the cache reaches capacity. LRU caches are fundamental to memory management in operating systems, databases, and web applications.

A program that implements the Language Server Protocol to provide language-specific features to code editors. LSP servers enable features like autocomplete, diagnostics, and refactoring across multiple editors.

Long Short-Term Memory — a type of recurrent neural network architecture designed to learn long-term dependencies in sequential data. LSTMs use gate mechanisms to control information flow and avoid the vanishing gradient problem.
Long-Term Support Release — a version of software that receives extended maintenance including bug fixes and security patches. LTS releases provide stability for production environments.

Lifetime Value — the total revenue a business can expect from a single customer account over the entire duration of their relationship. LTV helps companies determine how much to invest in customer acquisition and retention.

A lightweight, high-level scripting language designed for embedded use in applications. Lua is widely used in game development, web applications, and as an extension language in tools like Nginx and Redis.

An open-source full-text search library written in Java that provides powerful indexing and search capabilities. Apache Lucene is the foundation for search platforms like Elasticsearch and Apache Solr.
A simple checksum algorithm used to validate identification numbers such as credit card numbers. It detects accidental errors in data entry by verifying a check digit appended to the number.

M.definition: Apple's first custom ARM-based system-on-chip for Mac computers, integrating CPU, GPU, neural engine, and unified memory on a single die. M1 marked Apple's transition from Intel processors.

The lowest-level programming language consisting of binary instructions that a computer's CPU can execute directly. Each instruction performs a specific operation such as arithmetic, data movement, or control flow.

A branch of artificial intelligence where systems learn patterns from data to make predictions or decisions without being explicitly programmed. ML encompasses supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning approaches.
An end-to-end workflow that automates the sequence of steps from raw data to a deployed model, including data preprocessing, feature engineering, training, evaluation, and deployment.
The technology and methods used to provide imaging-based automatic inspection and analysis for applications such as automatic inspection, process control, and robot guidance in industry.
Apple's desktop operating system for Macintosh computers, based on a Unix foundation. It provides a graphical user interface, security features, and tight integration with Apple's ecosystem.
A rule or pattern that specifies how input should be mapped to an output, used to automate repetitive tasks. In programming, macros expand into code at compile time, enabling metaprogramming.
A hard-coded numeric value in source code that appears without explanation. Magic numbers reduce code readability and maintainability and should be replaced with named constants.
A sensor that measures magnetic fields, commonly used in smartphones for compass functionality, navigation, and detecting metallic objects. Magnetometers are also used in geophysical surveys.
Software that transfers email messages between servers using SMTP. MTAs like Postfix, Sendmail, and Exim handle the routing and delivery of email across the internet.

A large, powerful computer used primarily by large organizations for critical applications, bulk data processing, and enterprise resource planning. Mainframes are known for reliability, security, and high throughput.
A build automation tool that automatically builds executable programs and libraries from source code using directives specified in Makefiles. Make tracks dependencies to avoid redundant compilation.
The use of online advertising to spread malware by injecting malicious code into legitimate advertising networks. Malvertising can affect users without requiring them to click on an ad.
Software intentionally designed to cause damage to a computer, server, client, or network. Types include viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware, and adware.

The process of studying malicious software to understand its behavior, origin, and potential impact. Techniques include static analysis of code structure and dynamic analysis in sandboxed environments.

A cyberattack where the attacker secretly relays and possibly alters communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly. MITM attacks can intercept sensitive data like credentials.
Code that executes under the management of a runtime environment such as the .NET CLR or JVM, which provides services like garbage collection, type safety, and memory management.

A cloud computing model where the provider handles the maintenance, management, and support of the infrastructure or platform. This allows organizations to focus on their core business rather than IT operations.

The process of planning, organizing, directing, and controlling resources to achieve organizational goals. In technology, it encompasses project management, people management, and technical leadership.
A set of complex numbers that produces a connected fractal when visualized. The Mandelbrot set is a classic example in computational mathematics and is used to demonstrate iteration and complexity.

A file that contains metadata about a software package, application, or deployment, including dependencies, versions, and configuration. Examples include package.json, Cargo.toml, and AndroidManifest.xml.

An open-source bug tracking system that provides a web-based interface for managing software defects. Mantis supports project management features like issue tracking, notifications, and workflow customization.

A higher-order function that applies a given function to each element of a collection, returning a new collection with the transformed elements. Map is a fundamental operation in functional programming.
A pre-rendered image of a specific geographic area at a specific zoom level used in web mapping applications. Map tiles enable fast rendering of interactive maps by serving cached image fragments.

The process of transforming data from one format or structure to another, or the association between elements in different domains. In databases, object-relational mapping bridges objects and relational tables.

A programming model for processing and generating large data sets with a parallel, distributed algorithm on a cluster. The map step filters and sorts data, while the reduce step performs a summary operation.
A Java annotation processor that generates type-safe bean mapping code at compile time. MapStruct eliminates the need for hand-coded object mappings, reducing boilerplate and runtime overhead.
The space outside an element's border that separates it from adjacent elements. In CSS, margins create spacing between elements and can collapse vertically between adjacent block-level elements.
A community-developed fork of MySQL that aims to remain free and open-source. MariaDB maintains compatibility with MySQL while adding new features, storage engines, and performance improvements.

A lightweight markup language with plain text formatting syntax designed for easy readability and conversion to HTML. Markdown is widely used for documentation, README files, and content management systems.
The degree to which a product satisfies a strong market demand. Achieving product-market fit means that a product's value proposition resonates with a target customer segment willing to pay for it.

A stochastic model describing a sequence of possible events where the probability of each event depends only on the state attained in the previous event. Markov chains are used in modeling and simulation.

A class of algorithms for sampling from probability distributions based on constructing a Markov chain with the desired distribution as its stationary distribution. MCMC is foundational in Bayesian statistics.
A mathematical framework for modeling decision-making in situations where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of a decision maker. MDPs are foundational to reinforcement learning.

The process of transforming the memory representation of an object into a format suitable for storage or transmission. Marshalling is essential for remote procedure calls and inter-process communication.
The practice of creating and maintaining a single, consistent, and authoritative source of truth for critical business data such as customers, products, and locations across an organization.

A design language developed by Google that combines principles of good design with technological innovation. Material Design provides guidelines for visual, motion, and interaction design across platforms.

A technique for representing tree structures in relational databases by storing the full path from the root to each node as a string. Materialized paths enable efficient querying of hierarchical data.

A database object that contains the results of a precomputed query, stored as a physical table. Materialized views improve query performance by avoiding repeated computation of complex joins and aggregations.

A rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns, fundamental to linear algebra and machine learning. Matrix operations underpin neural networks, computer graphics, and data transformations.
A technique in linear algebra that decomposes a matrix into a product of smaller matrices. Matrix factorization is widely used in recommendation systems, dimensionality reduction, and topic modeling.
An open-source connectivity standard for smart home devices, backed by Apple, Google, Amazon, and Samsung. Matter enables interoperability across different ecosystems using IP-based networking.
A build automation and project management tool primarily used for Java projects. Maven uses a project object model file to manage project builds, reporting, and documentation.
Model Context Protocol server — a standardized interface that enables large language models to interact with external tools and data sources. MCP servers provide structured tool access for AI agents.

Multicast DNS — a protocol that resolves hostnames to IP addresses within small networks without a local name server. It allows devices to discover each other using human-readable names like device.local.
A measure of errors between paired observations expressing the same phenomenon. MAE is the average of the absolute differences between predictions and actual observations.
A reliability metric that measures the average time elapsed between inherent failures of a system during operation. MTBF helps predict system reliability and plan maintenance schedules.
A metric that measures the average time required to repair a failed system and restore it to full operation. MTTR is a key indicator of incident response effectiveness.

Writing software that works in harmony with the underlying hardware, particularly CPU caches, memory hierarchies, and instruction pipelines. Mechanical sympathy leads to significantly better application performance.
Microsoft's multimedia platform for building media applications on Windows, supporting video playback, capture, transcoding, and streaming. Media Foundation replaced the older DirectShow framework.
A CSS technique that applies styles based on device characteristics such as screen width, height, orientation, and resolution. Media queries are the foundation of responsive web design.
A behavioral design pattern that reduces coupling between components by having them communicate through a central mediator object. The mediator encapsulates interaction logic and coordination.
A distributed memory caching system used to speed up dynamic database-driven websites by caching data and objects in RAM. Memcached reduces the number of times an external data source must be read.
An optimization technique that stores the results of expensive function calls and returns the cached result when the same inputs occur again. Memoization trades space for time in dynamic programming.
Hardware components that store data and instructions for processing, including volatile memory like RAM that loses data when powered off, and non-volatile memory like flash storage that retains data.
A type of instruction that forces the CPU to enforce ordering constraints on memory operations. Memory barriers prevent the compiler and processor from reordering reads and writes across the barrier.

A type of resource leak that occurs when a program incorrectly manages memory allocations, failing to release memory that is no longer needed. Memory leaks can cause performance degradation and crashes.

The process of controlling and coordinating computer memory, assigning blocks to programs, and freeing them when no longer needed. Effective memory management prevents leaks and optimizes performance.

A pre-allocated block of memory from which smaller allocations are served, reducing the overhead of frequent allocation and deallocation. Memory pools improve performance in real-time and embedded systems.

A property of programming languages and systems that prevents programs from accessing memory in unsafe ways, such as buffer overflows and dangling pointers. Rust and Go provide memory safety guarantees.

A segment of virtual memory assigned a direct byte-for-byte correlation with a portion of a file. Memory-mapped files provide efficient random access to large files and shared memory between processes.
The process of combining changes from different branches or versions of code into a single unified version. Merge strategies include fast-forward, three-way merge, and rebase.
A situation that occurs when Git is unable to automatically resolve differences in code between two commits. Merge conflicts require manual intervention to decide which changes to keep.
A divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that divides the input array into halves, recursively sorts them, and then merges the sorted halves. It has a guaranteed O(n log n) time complexity.
A directed acyclic graph where each node is cryptographically linked to its children via content hashes. Merkle DAGs are the data structure behind IPFS, Git, and blockchain systems.

A tree data structure in which every leaf node is labeled with the hash of a data block, and every non-leaf node is labeled with the hash of its child nodes. Merkle trees verify data integrity efficiently.

A network topology where each node relays data for the network, allowing for self-healing and decentralized communication. Mesh networks provide redundancy and reliability for IoT and wireless applications.
An intermediary program that translates messages between formal messaging protocols, allowing different services to communicate. Popular message brokers include RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka, and ActiveMQ.
A wrapper around a message payload that contains routing information, metadata, and delivery instructions. Message envelopes separate transport concerns from business logic in messaging systems.

A form of communication used in concurrent and distributed computing where processes or objects exchange data by sending and receiving messages. It avoids shared state and the complexities of locking.

An asynchronous communication mechanism where messages are stored in a queue until the receiving service is ready to process them. Message queues like RabbitMQ and SQS enable reliable communication between distributed services and handle traffic spikes.
A communication pattern where components exchange information through discrete messages rather than direct calls. Message queues and brokers enable asynchronous, loosely coupled system architectures.
An area of machine learning focused on designing models that can learn new tasks quickly with minimal data by leveraging experience from previous tasks. Also known as learning to learn.
Data that describes and gives information about other data. Metadata helps organize, find, and understand data by providing context such as creation date, author, format, and purpose.

A Python framework originally developed at Netflix for building and managing real-life data science projects. Metaflow provides a human-centric API for managing dataflows from prototype to production.

A high-level strategy for exploring search spaces to find near-optimal solutions for complex optimization problems. Examples include genetic algorithms, simulated annealing, and particle swarm optimization.
A programming technique where programs have the ability to treat other programs as their data, allowing them to read, generate, or transform other programs at compile time or runtime.

A collective virtual shared space created by the convergence of virtually enhanced physical reality and persistent virtual spaces. The metaverse encompasses augmented reality, virtual reality, and the internet.
A function associated with an object or class that defines behavior. Methods operate on the data contained within the object and can access and modify the object's state.
A programming pattern where multiple method calls are linked together in a single statement by having each method return the object. This creates fluent interfaces that read like natural language.

A feature of some programming languages that allows multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists within the same class. The correct method is selected based on the arguments provided.
A feature in object-oriented programming that allows a subclass to provide a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its parent class. Overriding enables runtime polymorphism.
The algorithm used by a programming language to determine the order in which base classes are searched when executing a method. Python uses the C3 linearization algorithm for MRO.
The combination of a method's name, its parameter types, and their order, which uniquely identifies a method within a class. Signatures are used for method overloading and interface contracts.

A structured system of methods and principles for conducting work or research. In software development, methodologies like Agile, Scrum, and Waterfall define how teams plan, execute, and deliver projects.
The number of unique time series generated by a metric, determined by the combinations of label values. High metric cardinality can cause performance and cost issues in monitoring systems.

Quantitative measures used to track and assess the status of processes, systems, or business outcomes. In software, key metrics include response time, error rate, throughput, and deployment frequency.

Multi-Factor Authentication — a security mechanism that requires users to provide two or more verification factors to gain access to a resource. Factors typically include something you know, something you have, and something you are.

A processing technique that groups streaming data into small batches for near-real-time processing. Apache Spark Structured Streaming uses micro-batching to provide exactly-once processing guarantees.

An architectural approach that extends microservice principles to the frontend, where a web application is composed of semi-independent fragments owned by different teams. Each fragment can be developed and deployed independently.

An architectural approach that extends microservices principles to the frontend, where a web application is composed of semi-independent fragments owned by different teams. Each fragment can be developed and deployed independently.
Low-level instructions that implement higher-level machine code instructions within a CPU. Microcode acts as a translation layer between the instruction set architecture and the hardware.
A compact integrated circuit designed to govern a specific operation in an embedded system. Microcontrollers contain a processor, memory, and input/output peripherals on a single chip.

The small pieces of text in a user interface that guide users through interactions, including button labels, error messages, tooltips, and placeholder text. Good microcopy reduces friction and improves usability.

An operating system architecture that minimizes the kernel by moving most services to user space. Microkernels provide modularity and fault isolation but may have performance overhead from message passing.

Very small financial transactions, often fractions of a cent, enabled by digital payment systems. Micropayments allow monetization of content and services that would be impractical with traditional payment methods.

An integrated circuit that contains the functions of a central processing unit on a single chip. Microprocessors execute instructions, perform calculations, and manage data flow in computers and embedded devices.

An architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, loosely coupled services that communicate through APIs. Each service is independently deployable and focused on a specific business capability.

An architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, independent services, each running in its own process and communicating through APIs. Microservices enable independent deployment, scaling, and technology choices for each service.
Microsoft's cloud computing platform offering over 200 products and services for building, deploying, and managing applications through Microsoft-managed data centers worldwide.

Software that acts as an intermediary layer between different applications or between an application and the operating system. In web frameworks, middleware functions process requests and responses in a pipeline, handling tasks like logging, authentication, and error handling.

A software design pattern where functions are chained together to process requests sequentially. Each middleware function can modify the request, response, or pass control to the next function in the chain.

Musical Instrument Digital Interface — a technical standard for communication between electronic musical instruments, computers, and audio devices. MIDI transmits control data rather than audio signals.

The process of moving data, applications, or infrastructure from one system to another. Database migrations version-control schema changes, while cloud migrations move workloads from on-premises to cloud environments.

The process of removing unnecessary characters from source code without changing its functionality, including white space, comments, and shortening variable names. Minification reduces file size for faster loading.
A decision-making algorithm used in game theory and AI that minimizes the possible loss in a worst-case scenario. Minimax is the foundation of AI for two-player zero-sum games like chess.

A subset of edges in a weighted, connected, undirected graph that connects all vertices with the minimum possible total edge weight and no cycles. Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms find minimum spanning trees.
A version of a product with just enough features to be usable by early customers who can then provide feedback for future development. MVPs help validate ideas with minimal investment.
A high-performance, S3-compatible object storage system designed for large-scale AI and machine learning workloads. MinIO can be deployed on-premises or in any cloud environment.
A copy of a source code repository maintained on a different server for redundancy, performance, or compliance purposes. Mirror repos sync automatically and provide backup access to code.
Absent values in a dataset that can arise from collection errors, non-response, or system failures. Handling strategies include imputation, deletion, and using algorithms robust to missing values.
A family of open-weight large language models developed by Mistral AI, a French AI company. Mistral models are known for their strong performance relative to their parameter count.

A training technique that uses both 16-bit and 32-bit floating-point types to reduce memory usage and increase computational speed while maintaining model accuracy. Modern GPUs and TPUs optimize for mixed precision.
A blend of physical and digital worlds where real and virtual objects co-exist and interact in real time. Mixed reality spans the spectrum between fully real and fully virtual environments.
A class that provides methods to other classes without being a parent class. Mixins enable code reuse in languages that do not support multiple inheritance by composing behaviors.
A set of practices that combines Machine Learning, DevOps, and data engineering to deploy and maintain ML systems in production reliably and efficiently. MLOps automates the ML lifecycle.
A cloud computing model that provides backend services for mobile application development, including user management, push notifications, and cloud storage. Firebase is a popular MBaaS platform.
The process of creating software applications that run on mobile devices such as smartphones and tablets. Approaches include native development, cross-platform frameworks, and progressive web apps.
A design strategy that prioritizes designing for mobile devices before scaling up to larger screens. Mobile first ensures core functionality and content are accessible on the smallest screens.

A design strategy that prioritizes the mobile experience before scaling up to larger screens. Mobile-first design starts with the smallest screen constraints and progressively enhances for tablets and desktops.

A simulated object that mimics the behavior of real objects in controlled ways during testing. Mocks allow developers to isolate the code under test by replacing dependencies with predictable stand-ins.
A simulated object that mimics the behavior of a real object in controlled ways during testing. Mock objects help isolate the code under test by replacing dependencies with predictable substitutes.

A testing technique that creates simulated objects mimicking the behavior of real dependencies. Mock objects allow developers to test components in isolation, control test conditions, and verify interactions without relying on external services or databases.
A medium to high-fidelity static representation of a design that shows the visual appearance of a product. Mockups include realistic detail like colors, typography, and images but lack interactivity.
A mathematical representation learned from data that can make predictions or decisions. In machine learning, models are trained on datasets and then used for inference on new, unseen data.

Techniques for reducing the size and computational requirements of machine learning models while preserving accuracy. Methods include pruning, quantization, knowledge distillation, and low-rank factorization.

The process of assessing a trained model's performance using metrics and validation techniques. Common approaches include cross-validation, holdout testing, and metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.
The practice of tracking a deployed model's performance over time to detect degradation, data drift, or concept drift. Monitoring ensures models continue to make reliable predictions in production.

Techniques for improving a model's performance, efficiency, or resource utilization. This includes hyperparameter tuning, architecture search, quantization, and pruning to achieve better speed-accuracy trade-offs.

The process of deploying trained machine learning models to production environments where they can receive input data and return predictions. Model serving infrastructure handles scaling, versioning, and monitoring.

The process of feeding data into a machine learning algorithm so it can learn patterns and build a predictive model. Training involves iteratively adjusting model parameters to minimize a loss function.

A software design pattern that separates an application into three interconnected components: the Model for data, the View for user interface, and the Controller for business logic. MVC promotes separation of concerns.
An architectural pattern that facilitates separation of the graphical user interface from business logic. MVVM is widely used in modern UI frameworks like WPF, SwiftUI, and Vue.js.

A software design approach that divides a system into separate, interchangeable modules, each containing everything necessary to execute one aspect of functionality. Modularity promotes maintainability and reusability.

A design principle that divides a system into separate, interchangeable components called modules, each responsible for a specific functionality. Modularity improves maintainability, reusability, and testability.

A self-contained unit of code that encapsulates related functions, classes, and variables. Modules promote code organization and reuse, and most languages provide module systems for managing dependencies.

A design pattern in functional programming that defines how functions, operations, and side effects can be composed. Monads provide a way to chain operations while managing side effects like I/O or errors.
The process of generating revenue from a product, service, or asset. Common strategies include subscriptions, advertising, freemium models, micropayments, and licensing.

A popular open-source NoSQL document database that stores data in flexible, JSON-like BSON documents. MongoDB excels at handling unstructured data, horizontal scaling, and rapid development where schema flexibility is important.

The continuous observation of system performance, availability, and health through metrics, logs, and alerts. Monitoring tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and Datadog provide real-time visibility into application behavior and infrastructure status.
A technique to dynamically modify or extend code at runtime without changing the original source. While useful for quick fixes, monkey patching can make code harder to understand and maintain.

A software architecture where all components of an application are tightly coupled and deployed as a single unit. Monolithic architectures are simpler to develop initially but can become difficult to scale.
A software development strategy where code for many projects is stored in a single version control repository. Monorepos simplify code sharing, dependency management, and cross-project changes.

A broad class of computational algorithms that rely on repeated random sampling to obtain numerical results. Monte Carlo methods are used in optimization, numerical integration, and probabilistic analysis.

The observation that the number of transistors on a microchip doubles approximately every two years, while the cost of computers is halved. This trend has driven exponential growth in computing power.

Message Queuing Telemetry Transport — a lightweight messaging protocol designed for constrained devices and low-bandwidth networks. MQTT is widely used in IoT applications for device-to-cloud communication.
A problem in which a decision maker must choose between multiple options with uncertain rewards to maximize total payoff. It balances exploration of new options with exploitation of known good ones.

A strategy that uses cloud computing services from multiple providers to avoid vendor lock-in and optimize for cost, performance, and reliability. Multi-cloud architectures increase flexibility and resilience.

A security method requiring users to provide two or more verification factors to access an account — typically something you know (password), something you have (phone), and something you are (biometrics). MFA dramatically reduces unauthorized access.
A software architecture where a single instance of software serves multiple customers or tenants. Each tenant's data is isolated and invisible to other tenants, while sharing the same infrastructure.

A network communication method where data is transmitted from one sender to multiple specific receivers simultaneously. Multicast is efficient for streaming media, software updates, and group communication.
Artificial intelligence systems that can process and integrate information from multiple types of input such as text, images, audio, and video. GPT-4V and Gemini are examples of multimodal models.
A programming technique that allows a single process to execute multiple threads concurrently, sharing the same memory space. Multithreading improves performance for I/O-bound and CPU-bound tasks.
A method of software testing where small modifications are made to a program's source code to evaluate the quality of existing tests. Good tests should detect and fail on these mutations.
A mutual exclusion object used in concurrent programming to prevent multiple threads from simultaneously accessing a shared resource. Mutexes help avoid race conditions and ensure data consistency.

Model-View-Controller — an architectural pattern that separates an application into three interconnected components: the model (data and business logic), the view (user interface), and the controller (input handling). It promotes separation of concerns.

Multi-Version Concurrency Control — a database concurrency control method that maintains multiple versions of data to allow concurrent read and write operations without locking. PostgreSQL and MySQL InnoDB use MVCC.

Minimum Viable Product — the simplest version of a product with just enough features to satisfy early customers and provide feedback for future development. The MVP approach reduces risk and development time by validating ideas before full investment.

Model-View-ViewModel — an architectural pattern that facilitates separation of the development of the graphical user interface from business logic. It is particularly popular in WPF, iOS, and Android development.
An open-source relational database management system that uses SQL for data management. MySQL is one of the most popular databases worldwide, powering web applications and enterprise systems.

N.definition: A contiguous sequence of N items from a given sample of text or speech used in natural language processing and text analysis. N-grams are foundational for language models and text classification.
A software architecture pattern that separates an application into multiple logical layers or tiers, each responsible for a specific aspect such as presentation, business logic, and data access.

A family of probabilistic classifiers based on applying Bayes' theorem with strong independence assumptions between features. Despite its simplicity, Naive Bayes performs well on text classification and spam filtering.
A domain name without the www prefix, such as example.com instead of www.example.com. Configuring naked domains requires A records or ALIAS records rather than standard CNAME records.

A technique used by compilers to encode additional information into function or variable names, such as namespace, type, or parameter information. Name mangling enables function overloading in compiled languages.
A subtask of natural language processing that identifies and classifies named entities in text into predefined categories such as persons, organizations, locations, and dates.
An extension of the traditional pipe concept that provides a named, persistent connection between processes for inter-process communication. Named pipes allow unrelated processes to exchange data.

A container that provides scope and organization for identifiers like variables, functions, and classes, preventing naming conflicts. Namespaces are used in programming languages, Kubernetes, XML, and DNS to create hierarchical, non-overlapping naming contexts.

Not a Number — a special floating-point value representing an undefined or unrepresentable numerical result. NaN is returned by operations like dividing zero by zero or taking the square root of a negative number.
A simple, user-friendly command-line text editor for Unix systems. Nano provides basic text editing with keyboard shortcuts displayed on screen, making it accessible to beginners.

The manipulation of matter at the atomic and molecular scale, typically below 100 nanometers. In computing, nanotechnology enables smaller transistors, novel memory technologies, and quantum computing components.
The process of refining a broad type to a more specific type based on runtime checks. In TypeScript, narrowing occurs through type guards, typeof checks, and control flow analysis.

Network Address Translation — a method of mapping one IP address space into another by modifying network address information in packet headers. NAT enables multiple devices on a private network to share a single public IP address.

A managed network address translation service that enables instances in a private subnet to connect to the internet while preventing inbound connections. NAT gateways are essential for secure cloud architectures.
Techniques for establishing and maintaining network connections across devices behind NAT gateways. NAT traversal is essential for peer-to-peer applications, VoIP, and online gaming.

An application developed specifically for a particular operating system or platform using its native development tools and languages. Native apps offer the best performance and full access to device capabilities.
A module written in a compiled language like C or C++ that can be loaded and used by a higher-level runtime such as Node.js or Python. Native modules provide performance-critical functionality.

A lightweight, high-performance messaging system for cloud-native applications that supports publish-subscribe, request-reply, and queue-based messaging patterns. NATS is designed for simplicity and speed.
A database key formed from data that naturally exists in the real world, such as Social Security numbers or email addresses. Natural keys contrast with surrogate keys that are artificially generated.
A subfield of artificial intelligence that focuses on generating natural language text from structured data or other inputs. NLG is used in chatbots, report generation, and content creation.

A field of AI focused on enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language. NLP powers applications like machine translation, sentiment analysis, chatbots, and voice assistants through techniques ranging from rule-based to deep learning.

A subtopic of natural language processing that focuses on machine reading comprehension — the ability to understand the meaning and intent behind human language, including context, sentiment, and nuance.
A sorting algorithm that orders strings containing numbers in the way a human would expect, so that 'item2' comes before 'item10'. Natural sort is preferred in user interfaces and file managers.
The system of menus, links, and interactive elements that allow users to move through a website or application. Effective navigation is intuitive, consistent, and helps users find content quickly.
A navigation mesh — a data structure used in game development and robotics that represents the walkable surfaces in an environment. NavMeshes enable AI agents to find paths through complex 3D spaces.

A set of communication protocols enabling two electronic devices to establish communication within a range of about 4 centimeters. NFC is used for contactless payments, access control, and data sharing.
An algorithm that finds the closest data point to a given query point in a dataset. Approximate nearest neighbor search techniques like HNSW and Annoy enable efficient similarity search at scale.
The practice of caching negative responses such as 404 errors or empty results to prevent repeated lookups for non-existent resources. Negative caching reduces load on backend systems.
A testing technique that verifies the system behaves correctly when receiving invalid, unexpected, or erroneous input. Negative testing ensures the application handles error conditions gracefully.
A native graph database management system that uses graph structures with nodes, relationships, and properties to represent and store data. Neo4j excels at queries involving connected data.
A modern, extensible fork of the Vim text editor that adds features like built-in LSP support, Lua scripting, and asynchronous plugin architecture while maintaining backward compatibility with Vim.
A progressive Node.js framework for building efficient, reliable, and scalable server-side applications. Nest.js uses TypeScript by default and is inspired by Angular's modular architecture.
A function defined within the body of another function. Nested functions can access variables from their enclosing scope, enabling closures and encapsulation in languages that support them.
A query within another SQL query, also known as a subquery. Nested queries allow complex data retrieval by using the results of one query as input to another.

A management tool that measures customer loyalty by asking customers how likely they are to recommend a product or service to others on a scale of 0 to 10. NPS categorizes respondents as promoters, passives, or detractors.
A framework within the Linux kernel that provides packet filtering, network address translation, and connection tracking. Netfilter is the foundation for firewall tools like iptables and nftables.

A collection of open-source software tools developed by Netflix for building distributed systems. Components include Eureka for service discovery, Zuul for API gateway, and Hystrix for circuit breaking.

A collection of interconnected devices that can communicate and share resources with each other. Networks range from local area networks connecting nearby devices to the global internet connecting billions.
A network access control list that acts as a stateless firewall for controlling traffic in and out of subnets in a virtual private cloud. Network ACLs provide an additional layer of security.

A method of mapping an IP address space into another by modifying network address information in the IP header of packets while they are in transit. NAT is widely used in home and enterprise networks.
The process of automating the configuration, management, and operations of a computer network using software tools. Network automation reduces human error and speeds up network provisioning.
A phenomenon where a product or service gains additional value as more people use it. Network effects are a key driver of growth for social media platforms and communication tools.
A network topology where all nodes are interconnected through a series of switches forming a mesh-like structure. Network fabrics provide high bandwidth and low latency in data centers.
The monitoring and analysis of computer network traffic for the purposes of information gathering, evidence collection, and intrusion detection. It is a sub-branch of digital forensics.
An approach to network architecture that uses virtualization technologies to manage core networking functions via software rather than proprietary hardware. NFV reduces costs and increases agility.
A hardware component that connects a computer to a network, providing a physical interface for network communication. Modern NICs support high-speed Ethernet and wireless connectivity.
The time delay experienced in transmitting data across a network from source to destination. Low latency is critical for real-time applications like video conferencing, gaming, and financial trading.

A load balancer that operates at the transport layer (Layer 4) of the OSI model to distribute incoming network traffic across multiple targets. It handles millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency.
The systematic tracking of a computer network's components and performance to detect slow or failing systems. Network monitoring tools alert administrators to outages and unusual patterns.
A Linux kernel feature that provides isolated network stacks with their own interfaces, routing tables, and firewall rules. Network namespaces are fundamental to container networking.

The ability to understand the internal state of a network from its external outputs including logs, metrics, and packet captures. Network observability goes beyond monitoring to provide deep analytical insights.
A set of rules and conventions that govern how data is transmitted and received across a network. Protocols like TCP/IP, HTTP, and FTP define communication standards between devices.

Policies, practices, and tools designed to protect the integrity, confidentiality, and accessibility of computer networks and data. Network security includes firewalls, intrusion detection systems, VPNs, and network segmentation.
The practice of dividing a computer network into smaller subnetworks to improve security and performance. Segmentation limits the spread of attacks and helps enforce access control policies.
A form of virtual network architecture that allows multiple virtual networks to be created on top of a shared physical infrastructure. Network slicing is a key feature of 5G technology.
An endpoint for sending and receiving data across a computer network. Sockets provide the mechanism for inter-process communication using standard network protocols like TCP and UDP.
The arrangement of the elements of a communication network, including nodes and connecting lines. Common topologies include star, bus, ring, mesh, and hybrid configurations.

The process of combining hardware and software network resources into a single, software-based administrative entity. It decouples network services from the underlying hardware for greater flexibility and efficiency.
The practice of connecting computer systems and devices to share data and resources. Networking encompasses protocols, hardware, topologies, and the design of communication systems.
An automated machine learning technique for finding optimal neural network architectures. NAS uses search algorithms to explore the design space of possible architectures for a given task.
An approach to machine translation that uses neural networks to predict the likelihood of a sequence of words in the target language. NMT has largely replaced statistical machine translation methods.

A computing system inspired by biological neural networks, consisting of interconnected nodes (neurons) organized in layers that process information. Neural networks learn by adjusting connection weights during training, enabling pattern recognition and prediction.

A method for synthesizing novel views of complex 3D scenes by representing the scene as a continuous volumetric function using a neural network. NeRFs generate photorealistic images from limited input views.
