A technique that applies the artistic style of one image to the content of another using convolutional neural networks. It enables creating images that combine the content and style of different sources.
A computing approach that uses electronic circuits to mimic the neuro-biological architectures present in the human brain. Neuromorphic chips are designed for energy-efficient AI processing.
A technique for reducing the size of trained neural networks by removing redundant weights or neurons that contribute minimally to the output. Pruning reduces model size and inference latency.

A software analytics platform that provides real-time monitoring and performance management for web and mobile applications. New Relic offers application performance monitoring, infrastructure monitoring, and log management.

A React-based web framework that enables server-side rendering, static site generation, and full-stack development. Next.js provides built-in routing, API routes, and performance optimization features.

Non-Fungible Token — a unique digital identifier recorded on a blockchain that certifies ownership and authenticity of a specific digital asset. NFTs are used for digital art, collectibles, and gaming items.

A high-performance web server, reverse proxy, and load balancer known for its stability, low resource consumption, and ability to handle massive concurrent connections. Nginx serves a significant portion of the world's busiest websites.
A dynamic web and application server designed to run multiple languages and frameworks. NGINX Unit supports configuration changes without service disruption and manages application processes.
An automated build of a software project that runs every night using the latest source code. Nightly builds catch integration issues early and provide daily snapshots for testing.
The National Institute of Standards and Technology — a U.S. federal agency that develops technology standards and guidelines including cybersecurity frameworks used worldwide for risk management.
A powerful package manager for Linux and other Unix systems that makes package management reliable and reproducible. Nix ensures that installing or upgrading one package cannot break other packages.

Natural Language Processing — a branch of artificial intelligence that enables computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language. Applications include chatbots, translation, sentiment analysis, and text summarization.
Network Mapper — an open-source tool for network discovery and security auditing. Nmap scans networks to identify hosts, services, operating systems, and potential vulnerabilities.

Development platforms that allow users to create applications through visual interfaces and configuration rather than traditional programming. No-code tools democratize software creation for non-technical users.
A fundamental unit of a data structure such as a linked list, tree, or graph. In networking, a node refers to any device connected to a network that can send, receive, or forward information.
A Kubernetes scheduling constraint that attracts pods to nodes with matching labels. Node affinity rules can be required (hard) or preferred (soft), providing flexible workload placement control.

A Prometheus exporter that collects hardware and operating system metrics from Linux hosts, including CPU, memory, disk, and network statistics. Node Exporter is essential for infrastructure monitoring.
A visual representation of nodes connected by edges that depicts relationships between entities. Node graphs are used in social network analysis, recommendation systems, and knowledge representation.

A JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine that enables server-side JavaScript execution. Node.js uses an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model that makes it efficient for building scalable network applications, APIs, and real-time services.

A regularization technique that adds random noise to inputs, weights, or gradients during neural network training to improve generalization. Noise injection prevents overfitting by making the model robust to perturbations.
The process of removing unwanted random variations from data or signals to improve quality and accuracy. Noise reduction techniques are used in image processing, audio processing, and data cleaning.
An I/O processing model that allows other processing to continue before a transmission has finished. Non-blocking I/O is essential for building high-performance, concurrent applications.
A property of algorithms or systems whose behavior is not predictable from the same set of inputs. Non-determinism arises from concurrency, randomness, or external inputs.
Specifications that describe the system's operational qualities rather than specific behaviors. Examples include performance, scalability, security, reliability, and maintainability requirements.
A hardware interrupt that cannot be ignored or disabled by the processor, typically reserved for critical events like hardware failures or watchdog timer expiry. NMIs demand immediate attention.
A database that does not use the tabular schema of rows and columns found in relational databases. Non-relational databases are designed for flexible data models and horizontal scaling.
Computer memory that retains stored information even when power is removed. Examples include flash memory, ROM, and newer technologies like Intel Optane that bridge the gap between DRAM and storage.

A number used once — a random or pseudo-random value that is used only once in a cryptographic communication to prevent replay attacks. Nonces are essential in authentication protocols, blockchain mining, and secure API communications.
A no-operation instruction that performs no action and is used as a placeholder. Noops maintain code structure when an operation is syntactically required but no action is needed.

A concept where IT environments are so automated and abstracted from the underlying infrastructure that operations teams are no longer needed to manage them. NoOps leverages cloud and serverless technologies.

In databases, the process of organizing data to reduce redundancy and improve integrity through a series of normal forms. In data science, normalization scales numerical features to a standard range, ensuring fair comparison across different measurements.
Specific stages of database normalization, including First Normal Form through Fifth Normal Form. Each form addresses specific types of data redundancy and update anomalies.
A single metric that best captures the core value a product delivers to customers. The North Star metric guides a company's long-term growth strategy and aligns teams around a common goal.

A category of databases that store data in formats other than traditional relational tables, including documents, key-value pairs, graphs, and wide columns. NoSQL databases like MongoDB, Redis, and Cassandra excel at handling unstructured data and horizontal scaling.
An attack that exploits vulnerabilities in NoSQL databases by injecting malicious code through user input. Similar to SQL injection but targets document-oriented and key-value databases.
A type of NoSQL database that stores data in columns rather than rows, optimized for queries over large datasets. Cassandra and HBase are wide-column stores suited for time-series and IoT data.
A database constraint that ensures a column cannot contain null values. NOT NULL constraints enforce data integrity by requiring that every row has a valid value for the specified column.

The practice of recording information for later reference, supported by digital tools like Notion, Obsidian, and Roam Research. Modern note-taking apps support linking, search, and multimedia content.
An interactive computing environment that allows users to combine code execution, rich text, mathematics, plots, and media. Jupyter Notebooks are widely used in data science and machine learning.

A plugin or add-on that enhances the functionality of computational notebooks like Jupyter. Extensions can add features like table of contents, code formatting, variable inspectors, and collaboration tools.
A managed service that provides message delivery from publishers to subscribers. Cloud notification services like AWS SNS and Google Cloud Pub/Sub enable decoupled communication between components.

A class of computational problems that are at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP. No known polynomial-time algorithm exists for NP-hard problems, and they often require approximation algorithms.

Node Package Manager — the default package manager for Node.js and the world's largest software registry. npm enables developers to share and reuse JavaScript packages, managing dependencies and scripts for millions of open-source projects.
The default public registry for Node.js packages where developers publish and download JavaScript modules. The npm registry hosts over two million packages and serves billions of downloads monthly.
Custom scripts defined in a Node.js project's package.json file that automate common development tasks like building, testing, and deploying. NPM scripts provide a simple task runner mechanism.
Neural Processing Unit — a specialized processor designed to accelerate neural network computations. NPUs are optimized for the matrix operations common in machine learning inference.

Network Time Protocol — a networking protocol for clock synchronization between computer systems over packet-switched networks. NTP ensures all machines in a distributed system agree on the current time.
The package manager for .NET that enables developers to create, share, and consume useful code packages. NuGet hosts thousands of packages for the .NET ecosystem.
An operator that returns the right-hand operand when the left-hand operand is null or undefined. Null coalescing operators (?? in JavaScript and C#) simplify default value assignments.
A design pattern that uses a special object to represent the absence of a value instead of using null references. The null object implements the expected interface but performs no operations.
A runtime error that occurs when a program attempts to use a reference that points to no location in memory. Null pointer exceptions are among the most common bugs in languages like Java and C++.
A programming language feature that prevents null reference errors by distinguishing between nullable and non-nullable types at compile time. Languages like Kotlin and Dart have built-in null safety.

A type modifier that indicates a variable can hold either a value of its declared type or a null/nil reference. Nullable types help prevent null reference errors, and many languages now include null safety features.
A branch of pure mathematics devoted to the study of integers and integer-valued functions. In computer science, number theory is fundamental to cryptography, hashing, and random number generation.
The study of algorithms that use numerical approximation for mathematical analysis problems. Numerical methods are essential when exact solutions are impossible or impractical to compute.

Algorithms for solving mathematical problems using numerical approximation rather than exact symbolic computation. Common applications include root-finding, optimization, interpolation, and differential equation solving.
The degree of exactness with which a number is represented in a computing system. Limited numerical precision can cause rounding errors in floating-point arithmetic, affecting scientific computing.
A property of algorithms that ensures small errors in input data do not cause large errors in the output. Numerically stable algorithms are crucial for scientific computing and machine learning.
A fundamental Python library for scientific computing that provides support for large multidimensional arrays and matrices along with a collection of mathematical functions to operate on them.
A framework built on top of Vue.js that enables server-side rendering, static site generation, and full-stack development. Nuxt.js provides a structured way to build Vue.js applications.
A parallel computing platform and API model created by NVIDIA that allows software developers to use NVIDIA GPUs for general-purpose processing. CUDA is widely used for deep learning training.

NVIDIA's high-bandwidth interconnect technology for linking GPUs together or connecting GPUs to CPUs. NVLink provides significantly faster data transfer than PCIe, enabling efficient multi-GPU computing.

Non-Volatile Memory Express — a host controller interface and storage protocol for accessing non-volatile storage media attached via PCI Express bus. NVMe provides significantly faster data transfer than SATA.

O.definition: An open standard for access delegation that allows users to grant third-party applications limited access to their accounts without sharing passwords. OAuth 2.0 is the industry standard for authorization, used by Google, Facebook, GitHub, and most web platforms.
A consolidation of OAuth 2.0 best practices and security recommendations into a single specification. OAuth 2.1 deprecates insecure flows like implicit grant and requires PKCE for public clients.
The deliberate act of making source code or data difficult to understand. Code obfuscation protects intellectual property and makes reverse engineering more challenging.

An instance of a class that bundles data (attributes) and behavior (methods) into a single entity. Objects are the fundamental building blocks of object-oriented programming and model real-world entities.
A computer vision technique that identifies and locates objects within images or video frames by drawing bounding boxes around them. Popular architectures include YOLO, SSD, and Faster R-CNN.

A creational design pattern that maintains a set of initialized objects ready for use rather than creating and destroying them on demand. Object pools improve performance for expensive object creation.
A technique that lets you query and manipulate data from a database using an object-oriented paradigm. ORM libraries like Hibernate, SQLAlchemy, and ActiveRecord map database tables to classes.

A storage architecture that manages data as objects rather than file hierarchies or block addresses. Each object includes the data, metadata, and a unique identifier, making it ideal for unstructured data at scale.

A programming paradigm based on the concept of objects that contain data (attributes) and code (methods). OOP principles — encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction — promote code organization, reuse, and maintainability.
A mathematical function that a machine learning algorithm seeks to minimize or maximize during training. The objective function, also called a loss or cost function, quantifies model performance.

The ability to understand the internal state of a system by examining its external outputs — metrics, logs, and traces. Observability goes beyond monitoring to help teams debug complex distributed systems and understand why problems occur.
A data source that emits values over time and can be subscribed to by observers. Observables are the foundation of reactive programming libraries like RxJS and are used extensively in Angular.

A design pattern where an object maintains a list of dependents that are automatically notified of state changes. The observable pattern is widely used in reactive programming and event-driven systems.
A behavioral design pattern where an object maintains a list of dependents and notifies them automatically of any state changes. It is widely used in event-driven systems and GUI frameworks.
A decentralized data exchange protocol that enables sharing and monetization of data while preserving privacy. Ocean Protocol uses blockchain technology to create data marketplaces.

Open Container Initiative — an open governance structure for creating industry standards around container formats and runtimes. OCI specifications ensure container portability across different platforms.

Optical Character Recognition — technology that converts different types of documents such as scanned paper documents, PDFs, or images into editable and searchable data by recognizing text characters.
A logic error where a loop iterates one time too many or too few, or an index is shifted by one from the correct value. Off-by-one errors are among the most common programming mistakes.
A design approach where applications are built to work without an internet connection, syncing data when connectivity is restored. Offline-first architecture uses service workers and local storage.
A value used to specify a position within a data structure or result set. In database queries, offset is used with limit for pagination to skip a specified number of rows.

Objectives and Key Results — a goal-setting framework that defines clear objectives and measurable key results to track progress. Popularized by Google, OKRs align team efforts, promote transparency, and focus organizations on outcomes rather than outputs.
A cloud-based identity and access management platform that provides single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, and lifecycle management for applications and users.

Online Analytical Processing — a category of software that allows users to analyze information from multiple database systems simultaneously. OLAP tools enable complex analytical queries with fast execution times.
A multidimensional data structure optimized for fast analysis along multiple dimensions. OLAP cubes pre-aggregate data to enable rapid slicing, dicing, and pivoting in business intelligence queries.

Online Transaction Processing — a class of systems that manages transaction-oriented applications, typically for data entry and retrieval. OLTP systems are optimized for fast query processing and maintaining data integrity.
A mathematical notation that describes the lower bound of an algorithm's growth rate. While Big O describes the worst case, Omega notation describes the best-case time complexity.

A rotation where engineers are designated to respond to production incidents outside normal working hours. On-call responsibilities include monitoring alerts, diagnosing issues, and restoring service availability.

Computing infrastructure that is physically located within an organization's facilities, as opposed to being hosted in the cloud. On-premises deployments give organizations full control over hardware and data but require significant capital investment and maintenance.
A representation method that converts categorical variables into binary vectors where only one element is active. It is commonly used in machine learning to handle categorical features.
A password that is valid for only one login session or transaction, generated by a time-based or counter-based algorithm. OTPs provide an additional factor of authentication beyond static passwords.
A relationship type in database design where a single record in one table can be associated with multiple records in another table. It is the most common relationship type in relational databases.

A software architecture pattern that places the domain model at the center with layers of application logic around it. Dependencies always point inward, promoting separation of concerns and testability.
A technique for anonymous communication over a network where messages are encrypted in layers, each peeled by successive relay nodes. Tor is the most widely known implementation of onion routing.

A category of software tools that enable analysis of data stored in databases in real time for complex queries and multidimensional analysis. OLAP provides fast query response times for business intelligence.

A machine learning approach where the model is updated incrementally as new data arrives rather than being retrained on the entire dataset. Online learning is suited for streaming data and evolving distributions.
A formal representation of knowledge as a set of concepts within a domain and the relationships between those concepts. In AI, ontologies are used for knowledge representation and semantic reasoning.
Object-Oriented Programming — a paradigm that organizes software design around data, or objects, rather than functions and logic. OOP languages include Java, Python, C++, and C#.

Open Policy Agent — a general-purpose policy engine that enables unified policy enforcement across the stack. OPA uses a declarative language called Rego to define policies for Kubernetes, APIs, and microservices.
A data type whose concrete implementation is hidden from the user, exposing only its interface. Opaque types enforce encapsulation by preventing external code from depending on internal structure.
The portion of a machine language instruction that specifies the operation to be performed. Opcodes are fundamental to how processors execute programs at the hardware level.

A specification for building and documenting RESTful APIs that provides a standard, language-agnostic interface description. Formerly known as Swagger, it enables both humans and machines to understand API capabilities.
A web security vulnerability that occurs when a web application redirects users to an external URL specified by user input without proper validation. Attackers exploit it for phishing attacks.

Software whose source code is freely available for anyone to view, modify, and distribute. Open source powers the majority of the internet's infrastructure, from Linux and Kubernetes to React and TensorFlow, fostering collaboration and innovation.

A publicly available specification that is developed and maintained through a collaborative process and is freely implementable. Open standards like HTTP, HTML, and JSON promote interoperability and prevent vendor lock-in.

An open-source observability framework for cloud-native software that provides a collection of tools, APIs, and SDKs for generating, collecting, and exporting telemetry data such as traces, metrics, and logs.

An artificial intelligence research organization that develops and deploys AI models including GPT and DALL-E. OpenAI provides APIs that enable developers to integrate AI capabilities into applications.

A specification for describing RESTful APIs in a machine-readable format (formerly known as Swagger). OpenAPI definitions enable automatic documentation generation, client SDK creation, testing, and API gateway configuration.
A tool that automatically generates client SDKs, server stubs, and documentation from OpenAPI specifications. It supports over 40 programming languages and frameworks, accelerating API integration.
An open-source computer vision and machine learning software library that provides a common infrastructure for computer vision applications. It includes over 2500 optimized algorithms.
A cross-language, cross-platform API for rendering 2D and 3D vector graphics. It is widely used in CAD, virtual reality, scientific visualization, and video games.
An identity layer built on top of the OAuth 2.0 protocol that allows clients to verify the identity of end-users and obtain basic profile information. It is widely used for single sign-on.
An open-source search and analytics suite derived from Elasticsearch. It provides full-text search, log analytics, and visualization capabilities through its Dashboards interface.

Red Hat's enterprise Kubernetes platform that provides a consistent application platform for managing hybrid cloud and multicloud deployments. It adds developer and operations-centric tools on top of Kubernetes.
A widely used open-source software library for applications that secure communications over computer networks. It implements the SSL and TLS protocols and provides cryptographic functions.
A free and open-source cloud computing infrastructure software project that provides IaaS. It controls large pools of compute, storage, and networking resources managed through APIs or a dashboard.
A vendor-agnostic component that receives, processes, and exports telemetry data including traces, metrics, and logs. The collector decouples data generation from backend-specific export formats.
A fork of Terraform created after HashiCorp changed Terraform's license. Now known as OpenTofu, it is a community-driven open-source infrastructure as code tool under the Linux Foundation.
A value or variable upon which an operation is performed by an operator in a programming expression. Operands can be constants, variables, function calls, or sub-expressions.
System software that manages computer hardware and software resources and provides common services for computer programs. Examples include Linux, Windows, and macOS.
A symbol or keyword that performs an operation on one or more operands, such as arithmetic operators (+, -, *), comparison operators (==, <), logical operators (&&, ||), and assignment operators (=).
A feature that allows operators like +, -, and == to be redefined for user-defined types. It enables intuitive syntax for custom classes in languages like C++, Python, and Kotlin.

A Kubernetes design pattern that uses custom controllers to encode operational knowledge for managing complex stateful applications. Operators automate deployment, scaling, and recovery of applications.
A centralized directory of Kubernetes operators that extends the Kubernetes API to manage complex applications. It provides a standard way to discover, install, and manage operators on clusters.
Operating Expenditure — ongoing costs for running a business or system, as opposed to capital expenditure. Cloud computing shifts IT spending from CapEx to OpEx through pay-as-you-go models.
Short for Operations — the discipline responsible for managing and maintaining IT infrastructure, ensuring system availability, and supporting production environments.
A design pattern requiring users to explicitly choose to participate in a feature, service, or data collection. Opt-in respects user autonomy and is required by many privacy regulations like GDPR.
Technology that converts images of typed, handwritten, or printed text into machine-readable text data. Modern OCR systems use deep learning to achieve high accuracy across fonts and languages.

A computing approach that uses photons (light) instead of electrons for data processing. Optical computing promises higher bandwidth, lower power consumption, and faster speeds for specific computational tasks.
A flexible, transparent fiber made of glass or plastic that transmits data as pulses of light. Fiber optic cables provide high bandwidth and long-distance data transmission with minimal signal loss.
A concurrency control method that allows multiple transactions to proceed without locking resources, checking for conflicts only at commit time. It works well when conflicts are rare.
A concurrency control strategy that allows multiple transactions to proceed without locking resources, checking for conflicts only at commit time. It works well when collisions are infrequent.

A user interface pattern that immediately reflects changes as if they succeeded before receiving server confirmation. It provides a snappier feel but requires rollback handling if the server rejects the change.
The process of modifying a system to make it work more efficiently or use fewer resources. In software, optimization targets execution speed, memory usage, network bandwidth, or energy consumption.
A type that represents a value that may or may not be present, replacing null references with explicit handling. Option types (Maybe in Haskell, Optional in Java) prevent null pointer exceptions.
A multi-model relational database management system produced by Oracle Corporation. It is widely used in enterprise environments for transaction processing, data warehousing, and mixed workloads.

The challenge in reinforcement learning and testing where no perfect reference exists to evaluate the correctness of a system's output. The oracle problem makes validation of AI systems fundamentally difficult.
Object Request Broker — middleware that manages communication between distributed objects, allowing programs to make requests to objects located on other machines in a network.

The automated configuration, coordination, and management of complex computer systems, services, and workflows. Container orchestration (Kubernetes), workflow orchestration (Airflow), and service orchestration coordinate multiple components working together.
A system that manages the deployment, scaling, and operation of containerized applications across clusters of machines. Kubernetes is the most widely adopted container orchestrator.
A property of a messaging system that ensures messages are delivered in the same order they were sent. Kafka provides order guarantees within a partition, while some systems offer global ordering.
Software that tracks sales, orders, inventory, and fulfillment across channels. OMS platforms coordinate the entire order lifecycle from placement to delivery and returns.
The set of rules that defines the sequence in which operations are evaluated in an expression. Understanding operator precedence is essential for writing correct arithmetic and logical expressions.
Business expansion achieved through increasing output, customer base, or revenue internally rather than through mergers or acquisitions. Organic growth indicates sustainable business development.
Website visitors that arrive through unpaid search engine results rather than paid advertisements. Growing organic traffic requires effective SEO strategies and quality content.
A CDN configuration where the edge server fetches content from the origin server on a cache miss rather than having content pushed to it. Origin pull simplifies CDN configuration for dynamic content.
The server that hosts the original, authoritative version of web content. CDNs cache content from origin servers to deliver it faster from edge locations closer to end users.

Object-Relational Mapping — a technique that converts data between incompatible type systems in object-oriented programming languages and relational databases. ORMs like Prisma, SQLAlchemy, and Hibernate let developers interact with databases using their programming language.
A process whose parent process has terminated while the child is still running. In Unix systems, orphan processes are adopted by the init process to ensure proper cleanup.
A design principle where components are independent and changes to one do not affect others. Orthogonal systems are easier to design, test, and maintain because they minimize unexpected interactions.
The Open Systems Interconnection model — a conceptual framework that standardizes communication functions into seven abstraction layers. It guides the design and understanding of network protocols.

Open Source Intelligence — the collection and analysis of information gathered from publicly available sources for intelligence purposes. OSINT techniques are used in threat intelligence, investigations, and security assessments.
Open Source Software — software released under a license that grants users the rights to use, study, modify, and distribute the software and its source code to anyone for any purpose.

One-Time Password — a password that is valid for only one login session or transaction. OTPs are commonly delivered via SMS, email, or authenticator apps as part of multi-factor authentication schemes.

Communication that occurs outside the primary communication channel. In networking, out-of-band management provides a separate channel for managing network devices when the main network is unavailable.
Techniques for processing datasets that are too large to fit in main memory by streaming data from disk. Libraries like Dask and Vaex enable out-of-core computation on very large datasets.
An error condition that occurs when a program requests more memory than the system can allocate. OOM errors can crash applications and are addressed through memory profiling and optimization.
An unplanned interruption in service availability that prevents users from accessing a system or application. Outage management involves incident response, communication, and post-mortem analysis.
A SQL join operation that returns all rows from one or both tables, filling in nulls where no matching row exists in the other table. Outer joins include LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL variants.
A data point that differs significantly from other observations in a dataset. Outliers can indicate measurement errors, data entry mistakes, or genuinely unusual phenomena that warrant investigation.
The process of identifying data points that deviate significantly from the expected pattern. Methods include statistical tests, clustering-based approaches, and isolation forests.
The practice of temporarily storing output data in memory before writing it to the destination. Output buffering improves I/O performance by reducing the number of write operations.
The process of converting data into a safe format before rendering it in a web page or other output context. It prevents cross-site scripting attacks by neutralizing potentially dangerous characters.
A component of an LSTM neural network cell that controls which parts of the cell state are output. The output gate uses a sigmoid function to determine what information passes through.
The final layer of a neural network that produces the result for a given input. The output layer's activation function depends on the task — softmax for classification or linear for regression.
An abstraction that represents a destination for writing data sequentially, such as a file, network connection, or console. Output streams are part of the I/O model in most programming languages.

A machine learning problem where a model performs exceptionally well on training data but poorly on new, unseen data. Overfitting occurs when a model learns noise and details specific to the training set rather than general patterns.
A UI pattern that hides less frequently used actions behind a three-dot or hamburger icon to keep the interface clean. Overflow menus balance discoverability with visual simplicity.
The additional time, memory, or other resources required beyond what is strictly necessary to perform a computation. Reducing overhead is a key goal in system optimization.
A virtual network built on top of an existing physical network infrastructure. Overlay networks are used in SDN, VPNs, and container networking to provide flexible connectivity.
The ability to create multiple methods or functions with the same name but different parameter lists. Method overloading allows a class to have multiple implementations of the same logical operation.
The practice of allocating more resources than currently needed to handle unexpected spikes in demand. While it ensures availability, overprovisioning increases costs and wastes resources.
A feature that allows a subclass to provide a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its superclass. Method overriding is a key mechanism for achieving polymorphism.

A technique for addressing class imbalance in training data by creating more instances of the minority class. Methods like SMOTE generate synthetic samples to improve model performance on rare classes.

The Open Web Application Security Project — a nonprofit foundation that works to improve the security of software. OWASP is known for its Top 10 list of the most critical web application security risks.
A regularly updated list of the ten most critical web application security risks. It includes threats like injection, broken authentication, cross-site scripting, and security misconfiguration.

In software development, the person or team responsible for a particular product, feature, or service. Product owners define requirements, prioritize backlogs, and make decisions about the product direction.
A memory management concept in Rust where each value has a single owner, and the value is dropped when the owner goes out of scope. Ownership eliminates data races and memory leaks at compile time.
A memory management approach used in Rust where each value has a single owner, and the value is dropped when the owner goes out of scope. Ownership eliminates memory leaks without garbage collection.

P.definition: One of the most important unsolved problems in computer science: whether every problem whose solution can be quickly verified can also be quickly solved. If P equals NP, many currently intractable problems would become solvable.

The probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as the observed results, assuming the null hypothesis is true. A small p-value (typically below 0.05) suggests evidence against the null hypothesis.

Peer-to-Peer — a distributed network architecture where participants share resources directly with each other without a central server. P2P is used in file sharing, blockchain, and decentralized applications.
The 99th percentile latency, meaning 99% of requests are served faster than this value. P99 is a more meaningful performance metric than averages because it captures the worst-case user experience.

Platform as a Service — a cloud computing model that provides a complete development and deployment platform without managing underlying infrastructure. PaaS providers like Heroku, Railway, and Google App Engine handle servers, storage, and networking automatically.

A collection of related code modules bundled together for distribution and reuse. Package managers like npm, pip, and cargo handle downloading, installing, and managing dependencies in software projects.
A file (package-lock.json or yarn.lock) that records the exact versions of every installed dependency. Lock files ensure consistent, reproducible installs across different environments and machines.

A tool that automates the process of installing, updating, configuring, and removing software packages and their dependencies. Package managers like npm, pip, cargo, and apt ensure consistent, reproducible environments across development teams.

The process of capturing and examining network packets to diagnose problems, detect intrusions, or understand network behavior. Tools like Wireshark and tcpdump provide detailed packet inspection capabilities.
The interception and logging of network traffic for later analysis. Packet capture files (pcap) record complete network conversations and are essential for forensic investigation and troubleshooting.

The failure of data packets to reach their destination in a network transmission. Packet loss degrades voice calls, video streams, and real-time applications and is caused by congestion or hardware issues.

A method of data transmission where messages are broken into small packets that are sent independently across the network and reassembled at the destination. The internet is fundamentally a packet-switched network.
The space between an element's content and its border in CSS and UI design. Proper padding improves readability and visual hierarchy by providing breathing room around content.
A data structure used by the operating system to map virtual addresses to physical memory addresses. Page tables are essential for implementing virtual memory and process isolation.
A practice of assigning on-call engineers to respond to production alerts and incidents outside business hours. Pager duty rotations ensure continuous system monitoring and rapid incident response.
An algorithm used by Google Search to rank web pages in search engine results. It measures the importance of a page based on the number and quality of links pointing to it.
A technique for dividing large sets of data into discrete pages for display. Pagination improves performance and usability by loading only a subset of results at a time.
An agile software development technique where two programmers work together at one workstation. One writes code while the other reviews each line, promoting knowledge sharing and reducing bugs.
A sequence that reads the same forwards and backwards. Palindrome detection is a common coding interview question and has applications in DNA sequence analysis and string processing.
A Python library providing high-performance, easy-to-use data structures and data analysis tools. Pandas introduces DataFrame and Series objects that simplify tabular data manipulation.
A text rendering library used in GNOME and GTK applications that supports internationalization and complex text layouts. Pango handles bidirectional text, ligatures, and multiple scripts.
A type of computation where many calculations are carried out simultaneously, operating on the principle that large problems can be divided into smaller ones solved concurrently.
A Java Stream API feature that automatically partitions data and processes elements concurrently across multiple threads. Parallel streams simplify parallelism but require awareness of thread safety.

The simultaneous execution of multiple computations, typically across multiple processor cores or machines. Unlike concurrency, which is about structure, parallelism is about actual simultaneous execution for speed.
A variable listed in a function definition that represents a value the function expects to receive when called. Parameters define the interface of a function and enable code reuse.
Variables declared in a function definition that receive values (arguments) when the function is called. Parameters define the input interface of a function and enable code reuse with different data.

A design approach that uses parameters and rules to define and manipulate the geometry of a design. Changes to parameters automatically propagate through the design, enabling rapid exploration of variations.
A form of polymorphism where code is written generically so it can handle values of different types uniformly. Generics in Java and templates in C++ are implementations of parametric polymorphism.
A zero-configuration web application bundler that automatically detects and transforms assets including JavaScript, CSS, HTML, and images. Parcel provides fast builds using multicore processing.
An error detection method that adds an extra bit to data to make the total number of 1-bits either even or odd. Parity checks detect single-bit errors in data storage and transmission.
An open-source columnar storage file format designed for efficient data storage and retrieval. Parquet provides excellent compression and encoding schemes, making it ideal for big data analytics.
A tree structure that represents the syntactic structure of an input string according to a formal grammar. Parse trees are constructed by parsers and converted to ASTs for further processing.
A software component that takes input data and builds a data structure such as a parse tree or abstract syntax tree. Parsers are essential in compilers, interpreters, and data processing.
A technique in functional programming where a function is applied to some of its arguments, producing a new function that takes the remaining arguments. It enables function composition and reuse.
A program optimization technique that specializes a program with respect to some of its inputs at compile time. Partial evaluation generates faster residual programs by pre-computing known values.
The division of a database or dataset into distinct independent parts. Partitioning improves query performance, enables parallel processing, and simplifies data management.

The division of a database or dataset into distinct, manageable segments. Partitioning strategies include horizontal partitioning (sharding), vertical partitioning, and range-based or hash-based partitioning.

The process of gathering information about a target without directly interacting with the target system. It involves collecting publicly available data from DNS records, social media, and public databases.

A passwordless authentication credential based on FIDO2/WebAuthn standards that uses public-key cryptography stored on user devices. Passkeys replace passwords with biometric or device-based authentication.
A secret string of characters used to authenticate a user's identity. Strong passwords use a mix of character types, sufficient length, and uniqueness to resist brute-force and dictionary attacks.

The process of transforming a password into a fixed-length string using a cryptographic hash function. Algorithms like bcrypt, scrypt, and Argon2 are designed specifically for secure password storage.

Practices and technologies for protecting passwords, including hashing, salting, key stretching, password managers, and policies enforcing complexity and rotation. Modern approaches favor passphrases and passwordless authentication.
A piece of software designed to update, fix, or improve a computer program. Patches address security vulnerabilities, fix bugs, and occasionally add features to existing software.

The process of distributing and applying updates to software to fix vulnerabilities, bugs, or performance issues. Effective patch management balances security urgency with stability and testing requirements.

A legal right granted to an inventor that provides exclusive use of an invention for a limited period. Software patents cover novel technical processes, algorithms, or systems and can be contentious in the industry.
A technique for determining the maximum transmission unit size that can be sent across a network path without fragmentation. PMTUD optimizes packet sizes for efficient network communication.
A web security vulnerability that allows attackers to access files and directories outside the intended directory. Also known as directory traversal, it exploits insufficient input validation.

Algorithms that find the shortest or most efficient route between two points in a graph or grid. Common algorithms include A*, Dijkstra's, and breadth-first search, widely used in games and navigation.
A collection of reusable UI design components with documentation on their usage, behavior, and implementation. Pattern libraries promote consistency across products and accelerate development.
A mechanism for checking a value against a pattern. Modern languages like Rust, Scala, and Python use pattern matching for destructuring data and controlling program flow based on data shapes.

The automated identification of patterns, regularities, and anomalies in data using algorithms. Pattern recognition is fundamental to machine learning applications including image recognition, speech processing, medical diagnosis, and fraud detection.
The actual data being carried by a network packet or message, excluding headers and metadata. In API communication, the payload typically contains the request body or response data.

Principal Component Analysis — a statistical technique that reduces the dimensionality of data by transforming it into a new set of uncorrelated variables called principal components, ordered by the amount of variance they capture.

Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard — a set of security standards designed to ensure that all companies that accept, process, store, or transmit credit card information maintain a secure environment.
A distributed application architecture that partitions tasks among equally privileged participants. P2P networks allow direct sharing of resources without a central server.

A pre-trained model by Google for abstractive text summarization that uses gap-sentence generation as a pre-training objective. PEGASUS achieves state-of-the-art results on many summarization benchmarks.

An authorized simulated cyberattack performed to evaluate the security of a system. Pen testers use the same tools and techniques as malicious hackers to discover vulnerabilities before they can be exploited, providing actionable remediation guidance.
A statistical measure indicating the value below which a given percentage of observations fall. Percentiles are used in performance monitoring (P50, P95, P99) and standardized testing.
The ability of an AI system to interpret sensory data such as images, sounds, or touch to understand its environment. Computer vision and speech recognition are key perception capabilities in AI.

The simplest type of artificial neural network, consisting of a single neuron that computes a weighted sum of inputs and applies an activation function. Perceptrons are the foundational building block of deep learning.

The speed, efficiency, and resource utilization of a software system. Performance engineering involves profiling, benchmarking, and optimizing code, algorithms, and infrastructure to meet response time and throughput goals.
Testing conducted to evaluate the speed, responsiveness, and stability of a system under various workload conditions. It includes load testing, stress testing, and endurance testing.
An external device connected to a computer that provides input, output, or storage functionality. Common peripherals include keyboards, mice, monitors, printers, and external storage drives.
A permanent URL that points to a specific web page or piece of content, designed to remain unchanged over time. Permalinks ensure that links to content remain valid indefinitely.
An IAM feature that sets the maximum permissions an identity-based policy can grant to an IAM entity. Permission boundaries limit the scope of privileges without directly granting any permissions.
A network connection that remains open for multiple request-response cycles rather than being closed after each exchange. HTTP keep-alive and WebSockets are examples of persistent connections.
A fictional character created to represent a user type that might use a product or service. Personas help design teams understand user goals, behaviors, and pain points.

A concurrency control strategy that locks a resource for exclusive use by one transaction, preventing other transactions from accessing it until the lock is released. It avoids conflicts but can reduce throughput.
A unit of digital information storage equal to 1,000 terabytes or approximately one quadrillion bytes. Petabyte-scale storage is common in large enterprises, scientific research, and cloud providers.
Pretty Good Privacy — an encryption program that provides cryptographic privacy and authentication for data communication. PGP is used for signing, encrypting, and decrypting emails and files.

A social engineering attack where attackers disguise themselves as trustworthy entities to trick victims into revealing sensitive information. Phishing is commonly conducted through email and fake websites.
A web framework written in Elixir that provides high performance and fault tolerance through the Erlang VM. Phoenix is known for real-time features through channels and LiveView.
An Elixir library for building real-time interactive web interfaces without writing client-side JavaScript. LiveView uses WebSockets to sync server-rendered HTML to the browser dynamically.
The application of editing techniques to alter photographs digitally, including retouching, compositing, and color correction. Professional photo manipulation uses tools like Photoshop and GIMP.
A widely-used open-source scripting language especially suited for web development. PHP powers many major platforms including WordPress, Laravel-based applications, and Facebook's early codebase.

The lowest layer of the OSI model that handles the transmission of raw bitstreams over a physical medium. The physical layer defines electrical, optical, and radio specifications for network hardware.

A proportional-integral-derivative controller used in industrial control systems to maintain a desired output by adjusting inputs based on error measurement. PIDs are fundamental in automation and robotics.

An automated sequence of steps that code goes through from development to production, including building, testing, security scanning, and deployment. CI/CD pipelines ensure consistent, reliable software delivery with minimal manual intervention.

The practice of defining continuous integration and deployment pipelines in version-controlled configuration files rather than through manual UI configuration. Jenkinsfile, GitHub Actions, and GitLab CI use this approach.

A model parallelism technique that distributes different layers of a neural network across multiple devices, processing different micro-batches simultaneously. Pipeline parallelism enables training very large models.
The smallest addressable element in a digital image or display screen. Pixel density and resolution directly affect the quality and clarity of digital visual output.
The number of pixels per unit of physical length on a display, typically measured in pixels per inch. Higher pixel density produces sharper images and is a key specification in retina displays.

Public Key Infrastructure — a framework of policies, procedures, and technologies for managing digital certificates and public-key encryption. PKI enables secure communication, digital signatures, and identity verification.

A foundational technology or service upon which other applications, processes, or technologies are built. Platforms range from operating systems and cloud services to developer tools and marketplace ecosystems.

A cloud computing model that provides a platform allowing customers to develop, run, and manage applications without managing the underlying infrastructure. Heroku and Google App Engine are PaaS examples.
The discipline of designing and building toolchains and workflows that enable self-service capabilities for software engineering organizations. Platform teams create internal developer platforms.
A cross-browser automation framework by Microsoft for reliable end-to-end testing of web applications. Playwright supports Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit with a single API.
A software component that adds a specific feature to an existing computer program. Plugins enable customization without modifying the core application, following the open-closed principle.
The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes that can contain one or more containers that share storage and network resources. Pods represent a single instance of a running process in a cluster.
A variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Pointers enable dynamic memory allocation, efficient array handling, and data structure implementation in languages like C and C++.
A technique where a client repeatedly checks a server for new data at regular intervals. While simple to implement, polling is less efficient than event-driven approaches like WebSockets.
A piece of code that provides the functionality of a newer web feature on older browsers that do not natively support it. Polyfills enable developers to use modern APIs across all browsers.

The ability of objects of different types to be treated as objects of a common base type. Polymorphism enables code flexibility and extensibility through method overriding and interface implementation.
A virtual point where network connections start and end. Ports are identified by numbers and help computers sort network traffic — for example, HTTP uses port 80 and HTTPS uses port 443.
A method of determining which ports on a network are open and could be receiving or sending data. Port scanning is used by administrators for security auditing and by attackers for reconnaissance.
A CSS mechanism that determines how an element is placed in the document flow. Values include static, relative, absolute, fixed, and sticky, each offering different layout control capabilities.

A process conducted after an incident or project completion to identify what went wrong, what went right, and how to improve. Blameless post-mortems focus on systemic issues rather than individual fault.

Cryptographic algorithms designed to be secure against attacks by quantum computers. As quantum computing advances, post-quantum algorithms are being standardized to replace vulnerable RSA and ECC schemes.

A spatial database extension for PostgreSQL that adds support for geographic objects, allowing location queries to be run in SQL. PostGIS enables geographic information systems and spatial analysis in relational databases.

A powerful, open-source relational database system known for its reliability, feature richness, and standards compliance. PostgreSQL supports advanced data types, full-text search, JSON, and extensions, making it suitable for everything from small apps to enterprise systems.
An API development platform for building, testing, and documenting APIs. Postman provides tools for sending HTTP requests, writing test scripts, and collaborating on API development.

A structured review conducted after an incident to identify root causes, document lessons learned, and implement preventive measures. Blameless postmortems focus on system improvements rather than individual fault.
A business analytics service by Microsoft that provides interactive visualizations and business intelligence capabilities. It enables users to create reports and dashboards from various data sources.

An established method or custom in a professional discipline. In software engineering, best practices represent proven approaches to common problems, though they should be adapted to specific contexts.
A script that runs automatically before a git commit is finalized. Pre-commit hooks enforce code quality by running linters, formatters, and tests before code enters the repository.

A classification metric that measures the proportion of true positive predictions among all positive predictions. High precision means few false positives, which is critical in applications like spam detection.
A function that returns a boolean value, used to test whether something meets a specific condition. Predicates are commonly used in filtering, searching, and conditional logic.
The use of statistical algorithms and machine learning techniques to identify the likelihood of future outcomes based on historical data. It is used in marketing, finance, healthcare, and operations.
A technique that loads resources before they are needed to improve perceived performance. Browser prefetching downloads pages, scripts, or data that the user is likely to request next.

A program that processes source code before it is passed to the compiler, performing transformations like macro expansion, file inclusion, and conditional compilation. C/C++ preprocessors and CSS preprocessors like Sass are common examples.
A technique that renders pages on the server or at build time to improve initial load performance. Prerendering generates static HTML that can be served immediately to users.
A column or set of columns in a relational database table that uniquely identifies each row. Primary keys enforce entity integrity and serve as the main reference for relationships between tables.
A statistical technique for dimensionality reduction that transforms a set of correlated variables into a smaller set of uncorrelated components. PCA is widely used in exploratory data analysis.
A data structure where each element has a priority, and elements are served according to their priority rather than insertion order. Priority queues are typically implemented as binary heaps.

The right of individuals to control how their personal information is collected, used, and shared. Privacy regulations like GDPR and CCPA mandate data protection practices and give users rights over their data.

An approach to systems engineering that takes privacy into account throughout the entire development process. It ensures privacy is embedded into IT systems, business practices, and networked infrastructure.
A cloud computing environment dedicated exclusively to a single organization. Private clouds offer greater control, security, and customization compared to public clouds.

An attack that exploits a vulnerability to gain elevated access to resources that are normally protected. It can be vertical (gaining higher privileges) or horizontal (accessing another user's resources).

A programming paradigm where probability distributions are first-class citizens and programs can include random variables. It is used for Bayesian inference, generative modeling, and uncertainty quantification.
A mathematical framework for quantifying uncertainty and the likelihood of events. Probability theory underpins statistics, machine learning, risk assessment, and decision-making under uncertainty.

A mathematical function that describes the likelihood of different outcomes in a random experiment. Common distributions include normal, binomial, Poisson, and uniform, each modeling different types of random phenomena.
An instance of a computer program that is being executed, containing the program code and its current activity. Each process has its own memory space, resources, and execution context.

The process of acquiring goods, services, or technology from external sources. In IT, procurement involves vendor evaluation, contract negotiation, and managing supplier relationships for hardware and software.

A component that generates and sends data or messages to a queue, stream, or topic for consumption by other components. Producers are one half of the producer-consumer pattern used in event-driven architectures.

The practice of strategically guiding the development, launch, and continuous improvement of a product. Product managers define the vision, prioritize features, and align stakeholders around user needs and business goals.
A role in Scrum responsible for maximizing the value of the product by managing the product backlog. The product owner defines user stories, prioritizes features, and makes scope decisions.

The state where a product fully satisfies market demand, indicated by strong organic growth, high retention, and customers who would be very disappointed without the product. It is a key milestone for startups.
The efficiency of converting inputs into useful outputs, or tools and practices that help individuals and teams accomplish more. Productivity tools automate repetitive tasks and streamline workflows.

The practice of measuring the performance characteristics of a program, including execution time, memory usage, and function call frequency. Profiling identifies bottlenecks and guides optimization efforts.
The automated buying and selling of online advertising using algorithms and real-time bidding. Programmatic platforms use data to target specific audiences and optimize ad placement and pricing.
The process of writing instructions that a computer can execute to perform specific tasks. Programming involves problem-solving, algorithm design, coding in a language, testing, and debugging.
Jokes, memes, and cultural references shared among programmers about common coding experiences. Themes include off-by-one errors, naming things, and the eternal debate between tabs and spaces.

A formal system of notation for writing instructions that can be translated into machine code for execution. Languages vary in paradigm, type system, and abstraction level, from assembly to high-level languages.
A fundamental style or approach to programming that provides a way of structuring and organizing code. Major paradigms include imperative, object-oriented, functional, and declarative programming.
A web design strategy that starts with basic content and functionality accessible to all browsers, then adds enhanced features for browsers that support them. It ensures broad accessibility.

A web application that uses modern web technologies to deliver app-like experiences, including offline functionality, push notifications, and home screen installation. PWAs bridge the gap between web and native apps without requiring app store distribution.

The application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to project activities to meet requirements. In software, common frameworks include Agile, Scrum, Kanban, and traditional waterfall approaches.

A logic programming language associated with artificial intelligence and computational linguistics. Prolog programs define relations and query them, making it well-suited for expert systems and symbolic reasoning.

An open-source systems monitoring and alerting toolkit designed for reliability and scalability. It features a multi-dimensional data model, a flexible query language, and pull-based metrics collection.

An object representing the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation and its resulting value. Promises provide a cleaner alternative to callbacks for handling async code, and form the basis for the async/await syntax.

The practice of designing and refining input prompts to effectively communicate with and elicit desired outputs from large language models. It involves techniques like few-shot learning and chain-of-thought.
A testing approach where tests are defined by properties that should hold true for all valid inputs, and the testing framework generates random inputs to verify those properties.
A set of rules and conventions that govern how data is transmitted and received over a network. Common protocols include HTTP, TCP, UDP, and SMTP.
Google's language-neutral, platform-neutral mechanism for serializing structured data. Protobufs are smaller, faster, and simpler than XML and JSON for inter-service communication.

Google's language-neutral, platform-neutral serialization format for structured data. Protocol Buffers (protobuf) are smaller, faster, and more strongly typed than JSON or XML, commonly used with gRPC.
An object-oriented programming model where objects inherit directly from other objects rather than from classes. JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance as its primary inheritance mechanism.
An early model or release of a product built to test a concept or process. In design, prototypes range from low-fidelity wireframes to interactive high-fidelity mockups.

An intermediary server that sits between clients and destination servers, forwarding requests on behalf of clients. Proxies serve various purposes including caching, load balancing, security filtering, and anonymizing client connections.
A structural design pattern that provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it. Proxy patterns are used for lazy loading, access control, and logging.
An informal high-level description of a computer program's operating principle using structural conventions of a programming language but intended for human reading rather than machine execution.

Publish/Subscribe — a messaging pattern where senders (publishers) broadcast messages to topics without knowledge of receivers (subscribers). Pub/Sub decouples components and enables scalable event-driven architectures.
A cloud computing model where services are delivered over the public internet and shared across organizations. Major providers include AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud Platform.

The openly shared component of an asymmetric cryptographic key pair, used for encrypting data or verifying digital signatures. The corresponding private key is kept secret and used for decryption or signing.
A framework for managing digital certificates and public-key encryption that enables secure electronic transfer of information. PKI is the foundation of HTTPS and secure email communication.

A messaging pattern where senders publish messages to topics without knowledge of receivers, and subscribers receive only messages from topics they have subscribed to. It decouples message producers and consumers.

The process of making software, content, or data available to users. In software, publishing includes deploying applications, releasing packages to registries, and distributing content through platforms.
A mechanism in version control systems where a developer requests that their code changes be reviewed and merged into a target branch. Pull requests facilitate code review and collaboration.
An open-source configuration management tool that automates the provisioning and management of infrastructure. Puppet uses a declarative language to describe system configuration.
A Node.js library that provides a high-level API to control headless Chrome or Chromium browsers. Puppeteer is used for web scraping, automated testing, screenshot generation, and PDF creation.
A function that always produces the same output for the same input and has no side effects. Pure functions are a cornerstone of functional programming and enable easier testing and reasoning.
A message that pops up on a user's device from an application, even when the app is not actively in use. Push notifications are used for real-time alerts, updates, and engagement.
Progressive Web App — a web application that uses modern web capabilities to deliver an app-like experience to users. PWAs work offline, can be installed on devices, and support push notifications.

The Python API for Apache Spark that enables Python developers to use the Spark framework for large-scale data processing. PySpark combines Python's simplicity with Spark's distributed computing power.

A versatile, high-level programming language known for its readable syntax and extensive ecosystem. Python dominates data science, AI/ML, web development (Django, Flask), automation, and scientific computing, making it one of the world's most popular languages.

An open-source machine learning framework developed by Facebook's AI Research lab. PyTorch provides tensor computation with GPU acceleration and a dynamic computational graph for building neural networks.

Q.definition: A model-free reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the value of actions in states without requiring a model of the environment. It builds a Q-table mapping state-action pairs to expected cumulative rewards.

A lookup table used in Q-learning where rows represent states and columns represent actions, storing expected future rewards. As the agent explores, Q-values converge to optimal action-value estimates.

Quality Assurance — the systematic process of ensuring that software products meet specified requirements and quality standards. QA encompasses test planning, test execution, defect tracking, and process improvement.

The use of specialized software tools to execute pre-scripted tests on a software application before it is released into production. QA automation increases test coverage and reduces manual testing effort.

An automated sequence of quality assurance stages that code changes pass through before deployment. It typically includes linting, unit tests, integration tests, and acceptance tests to ensure code quality.
Amazon Quantum Ledger Database — a fully managed ledger database providing a transparent, immutable, and cryptographically verifiable transaction log owned by a central trusted authority.
A business intelligence platform that uses an in-memory associative data model to let users explore data relationships dynamically without predefined queries or hierarchies.
An open-source firmware for custom mechanical keyboards and input devices, supporting programmable key layers, macros, tap-dance keys, and RGB lighting through a C-based configuration system.

Quality of Service — a set of technologies and policies that manage network traffic to ensure reliable performance for critical applications. QoS prioritizes certain types of traffic, guaranteeing bandwidth, reducing latency, and minimizing packet loss.
Quantum Processing Unit — a specialized processor that performs quantum computations using qubits. QPUs are the core hardware component of quantum computers, distinct from classical CPUs.

Quick Response Code — a two-dimensional barcode that stores data in a matrix of black and white squares. QR codes can encode URLs, text, contact information, and more, readable by smartphone cameras for instant access to digital content.

A matrix factorization technique that decomposes a matrix into an orthogonal matrix Q and an upper triangular matrix R. It is fundamental to solving linear least-squares problems and eigenvalue computations.

A cross-platform application development framework for creating graphical user interfaces and applications that run on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS. It uses C++ and offers QML for declarative UI design.

A tree data structure in which each internal node has exactly four children, used to partition a two-dimensional space by recursively subdividing it into four quadrants. Quadtrees are used in spatial indexing and collision detection.
Non-numerical information that describes qualities, characteristics, or categories. In tech research, qualitative data includes user interviews, open-ended survey responses, and observational notes.

A systematic process of ensuring that software products and services meet specified requirements and quality standards. QA encompasses test planning, test execution, bug tracking, and process improvement throughout the software development lifecycle.
A discipline that focuses on building quality into the software development process rather than testing it in after the fact. It encompasses test automation, observability, and process improvement.

A set of predefined quality criteria that a software project must meet before it can proceed to the next stage of development or deployment. Quality gates enforce minimum standards for code coverage, bugs, and vulnerabilities.
Quantitative measures used to assess the quality of software products and development processes. Common metrics include code coverage, defect density, mean time to failure, and cyclomatic complexity.
A measure of the delight or annoyance of a user's experiences with a service or application. QoE goes beyond technical metrics to capture subjective user satisfaction and perceived performance.
The description or measurement of the overall performance of a service, particularly network performance metrics such as latency, bandwidth, throughput, and error rate.

The inverse of a cumulative distribution function that returns the value below which a given percentage of data falls. Also called the percent-point function, it is used in statistical analysis and risk modeling.

A type of regression analysis that estimates the conditional quantiles of a response variable distribution, rather than the conditional mean. It provides a more complete view of the relationship between variables.
Numerical information that can be measured and expressed as numbers, enabling statistical analysis. Metrics like page load time, conversion rate, and error count are quantitative data.

Research methods that collect numerical data about user behavior and preferences through surveys, analytics, A/B testing, and task completion metrics. It complements qualitative methods for evidence-based design decisions.

The process of reducing the precision of a neural network's weights and activations from floating-point to lower-bit representations. Quantization reduces model size and inference time while maintaining acceptable accuracy.

A technique that simulates the effects of quantization during model training so the model learns to maintain accuracy despite reduced numerical precision. It produces models better suited for edge deployment.
The point at which a quantum computer can solve a problem faster or more efficiently than any classical computer. It differs from quantum supremacy by focusing on practical, useful computations.

An algorithm designed to run on a quantum computer, exploiting quantum phenomena like superposition and entanglement. Notable examples include Shor's algorithm for factoring and Grover's search algorithm.
A quantum computing approach that uses quantum fluctuations to find the global minimum of a function. D-Wave systems use quantum annealing to solve optimization problems.

A hybrid quantum-classical variational algorithm designed to find approximate solutions to combinatorial optimization problems by alternating between problem-specific and mixer unitaries on a quantum circuit.

A metric that quantifies the error rate in quantum communication channels, measuring the fraction of bits that are incorrectly received. QBER is critical for assessing the security and reliability of quantum key distribution.

A communication channel that transmits quantum information, typically encoded in the quantum states of photons. Quantum channels are the backbone of quantum networking and quantum key distribution systems.
A model for quantum computation in which a computation is a sequence of quantum gates applied to qubits. Quantum circuits are the most commonly used model for describing quantum algorithms.

The property of quantum systems where particles maintain a definite phase relationship, enabling superposition and entanglement. Maintaining coherence is a central challenge in building practical quantum computers.

A type of computing that uses quantum mechanical phenomena — superposition, entanglement, and interference — to process information. Quantum computers can potentially solve certain problems exponentially faster than classical computers, impacting cryptography and optimization.
The science of exploiting quantum mechanical properties to perform cryptographic tasks. Quantum key distribution allows two parties to produce a shared random secret key known only to them.
The process by which a quantum system loses its coherence and quantum behavior due to interaction with its environment. Decoherence is a primary obstacle to building large-scale quantum computers.
A nanoscale semiconductor particle with quantum mechanical properties used in displays, solar cells, and quantum computing. Quantum dots emit light of specific wavelengths depending on their size.

A quantum mechanical phenomenon where two or more particles become interconnected so that the quantum state of each particle cannot be described independently. It is a key resource in quantum computing and communication.
A set of techniques used to protect quantum information from errors due to decoherence and other quantum noise. Quantum error correction is essential for building reliable quantum computers.
The quantum analogue of the classical discrete Fourier transform, operating on quantum states. It is a key subroutine in many quantum algorithms, including Shor's factoring algorithm.
A basic quantum circuit operating on a small number of qubits, analogous to classical logic gates. Common quantum gates include the Hadamard gate, CNOT gate, and Pauli gates.
A proposed network that would use quantum signals and entanglement to connect quantum devices over long distances. It would enable ultra-secure communication and distributed quantum computing.

A secure communication method that uses quantum mechanics to enable two parties to produce a shared random secret key. Any attempt to eavesdrop on the key exchange can be detected due to quantum properties.

An emerging field that explores the intersection of quantum computing and machine learning. It investigates how quantum algorithms can speed up machine learning tasks and how classical ML can be applied to quantum systems.
A device that stores quantum information in the form of quantum states for later retrieval. Quantum memories are essential components for quantum repeaters and scalable quantum networks.
The field of connecting quantum processors and devices using quantum communication channels. It enables distributed quantum computing, secure communication, and the future quantum internet.

Random fluctuations in quantum systems that arise from the inherent uncertainty of quantum mechanics. Quantum noise is a major challenge in quantum computing, affecting qubit fidelity and gate accuracy.

The ability of a quantum computer to evaluate a function on many different inputs simultaneously by exploiting superposition. It is one of the key advantages that gives quantum computers their computational power.
A system of multiple qubits that together represent quantum information. Quantum registers are the quantum analogue of classical processor registers and form the working memory of quantum algorithms.
