The combined discipline of User Experience and User Interface design that creates products which are both visually appealing and easy to use. UX/UI professionals bridge the gap between user needs and visual design.

V.definition: A software development methodology that extends the waterfall model by associating each development phase with a corresponding testing phase. It emphasizes verification and validation at every stage of the development lifecycle.

Google's open-source JavaScript and WebAssembly engine written in C++ that powers Chrome and Node.js. V8 compiles JavaScript to machine code using just-in-time compilation for high-performance execution.
Google's open-source high-performance JavaScript and WebAssembly engine, written in C++. V8 powers Chrome and Node.js, using just-in-time compilation to execute JavaScript at near-native speed.

Variational Autoencoder is a generative model that learns a compressed latent representation of input data by encoding it into a probability distribution. VAEs can generate new data samples by sampling from the learned latent space.

An open-source tool for building and managing portable virtual development environments. Vagrant uses configuration files called Vagrantfiles to automate the setup of virtual machines with consistent, reproducible environments.

The process of checking that data meets specified requirements before processing. Input validation prevents errors and security vulnerabilities by ensuring data conforms to expected formats, types, ranges, and constraints at both client and server levels.

The full range of activities a company performs to deliver a product or service from conception to delivery. Value chain analysis identifies opportunities to create competitive advantage by optimizing each activity.

An immutable object in domain-driven design that represents a descriptive aspect of the domain with no conceptual identity. Value objects are compared by their attributes rather than by identity, like a Money or Address type.

A clear statement that explains how a product or service solves a customer problem, delivers benefits, and why a customer should choose it over competitors. Value propositions guide product development and marketing strategy.

A lean management technique that analyzes the flow of materials and information through a process to identify waste and optimize delivery. In software, it maps the steps from code commit to production deployment.

Measurements that look impressive but do not provide actionable insights or correlate with business success, such as total page views or app downloads without retention data. Effective teams focus on actionable metrics instead.
A server-side Swift web framework that provides a clean API for building web applications, APIs, and HTTP servers. It leverages Swift's type safety and performance for backend development.
A named storage location in a program that holds a value which can be modified during execution. Variables have a type, scope, and lifetime that determine how and where they can be used in a program.

An encoding scheme where different symbols are represented by codes of different lengths, with more frequent symbols using shorter codes. Huffman coding and UTF-8 are examples of variable-length encoding.
A function that accepts a variable number of arguments. Languages implement this differently—C uses va_list, Python uses *args, and JavaScript uses rest parameters (...args).

A statistical measure of how far data points are spread out from their mean, calculated as the average squared deviation. In machine learning, variance describes how much a model's predictions change with different training data.
A method for approximating complex probability distributions by finding the closest member of a simpler family of distributions. It converts intractable inference problems into optimization problems.

A high-performance HTTP accelerator and reverse proxy cache designed for content-heavy dynamic websites. Varnish stores copies of web pages in memory, dramatically reducing backend server load and response times.

HashiCorp Vault is a secrets management tool that provides secure storage, dynamic secrets generation, encryption as a service, and identity-based access. It is widely used to manage API keys, passwords, and certificates in cloud-native environments.
Version Control System tracks and manages changes to source code over time, enabling collaboration and history tracking. Common VCS types include centralized (SVN) and distributed (Git) systems.
Platforms that host version control repositories and provide collaboration features like pull requests, code review, and CI/CD integration. Major providers include GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket.

Virtual Desktop Infrastructure hosts desktop environments on a centralized server, allowing users to access their full desktop remotely from any device. VDI provides consistent environments, centralized management, and enhanced security.
A dynamic array data structure that automatically resizes as elements are added or removed. In machine learning, vectors are one-dimensional arrays of numbers representing features or embeddings.

A mechanism for tracking causality and ordering events in distributed systems by assigning each process a vector of logical timestamps. Vector clocks detect concurrent events and resolve conflicts in distributed databases.

A specialized database designed to store, index, and query high-dimensional vector embeddings efficiently. Vector databases like Pinecone, Weaviate, and Chroma enable semantic search, recommendation systems, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for AI applications.

A numerical representation of data (text, images, audio) as a dense vector in a high-dimensional space where similar items are positioned closer together. Embeddings enable semantic search, recommendation systems, and transfer learning.

A form of computer graphics in which visual images are created from geometric primitives like points, lines, curves, and shapes defined by mathematical expressions. Vector graphics scale without loss of quality.

A CPU instruction that operates on multiple data elements simultaneously using SIMD registers. Vector instructions like AVX and SSE accelerate numerical computations, media processing, and machine learning workloads.

A data compression technique that represents a large set of vectors by a smaller codebook of representative vectors. Vector quantization is used in image compression, speech coding, and efficient nearest-neighbor search.

A search technique that finds items similar to a query by comparing vector embeddings using distance metrics like cosine similarity. Vector search powers semantic search, recommendation systems, and RAG applications.

In Agile development, the total amount of work a team completes during a sprint, typically measured in story points. Velocity helps teams predict how much work they can commit to in future iterations.
The practice of measuring the amount of work a development team completes in each sprint or iteration. Velocity helps teams forecast future capacity, plan releases, and identify process improvements.

A situation where a customer becomes dependent on a specific vendor's products or services, making it difficult and costly to switch to a competitor. It is a key consideration when choosing cloud providers and proprietary tools.

The process of evaluating, selecting, and managing third-party suppliers and service providers to optimize value and mitigate risk. In tech, it includes managing SaaS subscriptions, cloud providers, and outsourced services.

An approach to technology selection that avoids dependence on a single vendor's proprietary products or standards. Vendor-neutral strategies use open standards and portable solutions to maintain flexibility and prevent lock-in.

The practice of copying third-party dependencies directly into a project's source tree rather than fetching them at build time. Vendoring ensures reproducible builds and removes external dependency on package registries.

A form of private equity financing provided to startups and early-stage companies with high growth potential in exchange for equity ownership. VC firms provide funding, mentorship, and industry connections.

A cloud platform optimized for deploying frontend frameworks and serverless functions, known for its seamless integration with Next.js. Vercel provides edge deployment, preview environments, and automatic scaling.

Two complementary quality assurance processes — verification checks that the product is built correctly according to specifications, while validation confirms the product meets the actual user needs and requirements.

A hardware description language used to model electronic systems at various levels of abstraction, from gate level to behavioral. Verilog is widely used in FPGA and ASIC design for digital circuit implementation and simulation.

A content delivery network service that distributes web content across globally distributed edge servers for faster delivery. CDN services reduce latency and server load by caching content closer to end users.

A system that records changes to files over time so you can recall specific versions later. Version control systems like Git enable collaboration, track history, manage branches, and provide a safety net for code changes across teams.

The practice of locking dependencies to specific versions to ensure reproducible builds and prevent unexpected breaking changes. Lock files in package managers like npm and pip implement version pinning.

An extension of vector clocks that tracks the version history of replicated data across multiple nodes. Version vectors detect conflicts and enable eventual consistency in distributed storage systems.

A polyglot event-driven application framework that runs on the JVM, supporting Java, Kotlin, Groovy, and JavaScript. It is designed for building reactive, high-performance microservices and web applications.

Google Cloud's machine learning platform that provides tools for building, deploying, and scaling ML models. Vertex AI offers AutoML, custom training, model management, and integration with Google's AI services including Gemini.

A database design technique that splits a table into multiple tables containing fewer columns. Vertical partitioning improves query performance by keeping frequently accessed columns together and separating rarely used data.
Increasing the capacity of a single server by adding more CPU, RAM, or storage resources. Also known as scaling up, it is simpler than horizontal scaling but has physical hardware limits.

A software architecture pattern where code is organized by feature rather than by technical layer. Each vertical slice contains all layers needed for a feature, from UI to database, promoting cohesion and independence.

VHSIC Hardware Description Language is used for describing the behavior and structure of electronic systems. It is commonly used alongside Verilog for FPGA programming, ASIC design, and digital system simulation.
The recording, processing, storage, and playback of moving visual images. Digital video encompasses encoding formats, streaming protocols, compression algorithms, and rendering technologies.

The use of computer vision and machine learning to automatically analyze video content for extracting information, detecting events, or identifying objects. Applications include surveillance, traffic monitoring, and retail analytics.

Software or hardware that compresses and decompresses digital video data using algorithms like H.264, H.265/HEVC, VP9, and AV1. Codecs balance file size, quality, and encoding/decoding speed for efficient video delivery.

Technology that allows two or more people in different locations to communicate in real time using video and audio transmission. Video conferencing platforms like Zoom and Teams support remote collaboration.

The process of converting raw video data into a compressed digital format using codecs like H.264, H.265, VP9, or AV1. Video encoding balances file size, quality, and playback compatibility for streaming and storage.

The manipulation and analysis of video data using algorithms for tasks like encoding, decoding, transcoding, filtering, and computer vision. Video processing is used in streaming, surveillance, and content creation.

The continuous delivery of video content over the internet where playback begins before the entire file is downloaded. Adaptive bitrate streaming protocols like HLS and DASH adjust quality based on network conditions.

The process of converting a video file from one format or codec to another, often to optimize for different devices, bandwidths, or platforms. Transcoding is essential for adaptive streaming and content delivery.
A design pattern component that exposes data and commands for the view layer while remaining independent of the UI framework. The View Model mediates between the model and view in MVVM architecture.

The visible area of a web page in the browser window, which varies by device and screen size. The viewport meta tag controls how content scales on mobile devices and is essential for responsive design.

A highly configurable, modal text editor built for efficiency, available on virtually every Unix system. Its modal editing paradigm separates navigation, editing, and command modes for fast text manipulation.

The keyboard-based navigation commands in Vim that allow rapid cursor movement without a mouse, using keys like h/j/k/l, w/b, and search patterns. Many modern editors offer Vim motion plugins for efficient text navigation.

A software architecture pattern for iOS development that separates concerns into View, Interactor, Presenter, Entity, and Router components. VIPER provides better testability and modularity than the traditional MVC pattern.

A growth pattern where a product or service spreads rapidly through user referrals and word-of-mouth, with each user bringing in multiple new users. Viral growth is measured by the viral coefficient and cycle time.

The set of memory addresses available to a process, mapped by the operating system to physical memory or disk. Virtual address spaces give each process the illusion of having its own contiguous memory block.

An AI-powered software agent that can perform tasks and answer questions through natural language interaction. Examples include Apple's Siri, Amazon's Alexa, and AI chatbots that handle customer support.
An abstraction of a physical CPU core allocated to a virtual machine or container. vCPUs allow cloud providers to divide physical hardware resources among multiple tenants and workloads.

A lightweight in-memory representation of the actual DOM that frameworks like React use to optimize UI updates. Changes are first applied to the virtual DOM, then efficiently reconciled with the real DOM through a diffing algorithm.

An isolated Python runtime environment that maintains its own set of installed packages, independent of the system Python and other projects. Tools like venv and virtualenv create these environments to avoid dependency conflicts.

An abstraction layer in an operating system that provides a uniform interface to different types of file systems. The VFS allows applications to access local, network, and special-purpose file systems through the same API.

A technique that allows multiple websites to be served from a single web server, distinguished by domain name (name-based) or IP address (IP-based). It is how shared hosting providers serve thousands of sites from one machine.

A software emulation of a physical computer that runs an operating system and applications as if they were on dedicated hardware. VMs provide isolation and flexibility, though containers have largely replaced them for application deployment.

A memory management technique that provides each process with an idealized abstract view of storage, mapping virtual addresses to physical memory. Virtual memory enables running programs larger than available RAM.
A software-defined network that provides isolated network segments within a cloud environment, enabling resources to communicate securely. Examples include AWS VPC, Azure VNet, and GCP VPC networks.

A security policy rule applied at the web application firewall or intrusion prevention system level to block exploitation of a known vulnerability without modifying the application code. Virtual patches provide immediate protection while permanent fixes are developed.
A logically isolated section of a public cloud that provides private, configurable networking for cloud resources. VPCs allow users to define IP ranges, subnets, route tables, and security groups.

A computer-generated simulation of a three-dimensional environment that users can interact with using specialized hardware like headsets and controllers. VR is used in gaming, training, therapy, and architectural visualization.

A software-based network switch that operates within a virtualization environment to manage traffic between virtual machines. Virtual switches like Open vSwitch provide network connectivity, VLAN tagging, and traffic monitoring.

Also known as a vtable, it is a mechanism used in C++ and similar languages to support dynamic dispatch of virtual functions. Each class with virtual methods has a vtable containing pointers to the appropriate function implementations.

A lightweight thread managed by the runtime rather than the operating system, enabling millions of concurrent threads with minimal overhead. Java's Project Loom introduced virtual threads to simplify concurrent programming.

The creation of virtual versions of physical resources including servers, storage, networks, and operating systems. Virtualization improves resource utilization, enables rapid provisioning, and forms the foundation of cloud computing infrastructure.

A period during which a message retrieved from a queue is hidden from other consumers, preventing duplicate processing. If the consumer fails to delete the message within the timeout, it becomes visible again for retry.

The collection and analysis of data about website visitors including page views, session duration, bounce rates, and conversion paths. Tools like Google Analytics and Plausible provide insights to optimize user experience and marketing.

A behavioral design pattern that separates an algorithm from the object structure it operates on, allowing new operations to be added without modifying existing classes. It uses double dispatch to select the correct method at runtime.
The use of imagery, color, shapes, typography, and form to enhance usability and improve the user experience. Visual design establishes brand identity and creates aesthetically pleasing interfaces.

A programming approach where users create programs by manipulating graphical elements rather than writing text code. Visual programming tools like Scratch, Node-RED, and Unreal Blueprints lower the barrier to programming.

An automated testing approach that captures screenshots of UI components and compares them against baseline images to detect unintended visual changes. Tools like Percy and Chromatic integrate this into CI pipelines.

A comprehensive integrated development environment from Microsoft for building applications across multiple platforms and languages including C#, C++, Python, and JavaScript. It includes debugging, profiling, and design tools.

The graphical representation of data and information using charts, graphs, maps, and dashboards to make complex data more accessible and understandable. Tools like D3.js, Matplotlib, and Tableau are commonly used for data visualization.

Vision Transformer is a model architecture that applies transformer mechanisms, originally designed for NLP, to image classification by treating image patches as tokens. ViT has shown that pure transformer models can match or exceed CNNs on vision tasks.

A modern frontend build tool that provides fast development server startup and hot module replacement using native ES modules. Vite supports React, Vue, Svelte, and other frameworks, offering a significantly faster developer experience than traditional bundlers.

A fast Vite-native unit testing framework that provides Jest-compatible APIs with out-of-the-box TypeScript and ESM support. It shares Vite's configuration and transformation pipeline for a seamless development experience.

Virtual Local Area Network — a logical subdivision of a physical network that groups devices together regardless of physical location. VLANs segment network traffic to improve security, reduce broadcast domains, and simplify management.

Very Long Instruction Word is a processor architecture that packages multiple independent operations into a single wide instruction, relying on the compiler to find parallelism. It simplifies hardware design at the cost of compiler complexity.

A spreadsheet function that searches for a value in the first column of a range and returns a value from a specified column in the same row. While widely used in Excel, it has been largely superseded by XLOOKUP and INDEX/MATCH.

A security exploit where an attacker breaks out of a virtual machine to interact with the host operating system or hypervisor. VM escape vulnerabilities are critical because they compromise the isolation that virtualization provides.

The process of moving a running virtual machine from one physical host to another with minimal or no downtime. Live VM migration enables hardware maintenance, load balancing, and disaster recovery without service interruption.

Virtual Machine Disk is a file format that describes containers for virtual hard disk drives used by VMware virtual machines. VMDK files store the contents of a VM's hard drive and can be converted to other formats.

A leading provider of virtualization and cloud computing software, known for products like vSphere, ESXi, and vCenter. VMware's technology enables organizations to run multiple virtual machines on a single physical server.

Virtual Network Computing is a graphical desktop-sharing system that uses the RFB protocol to remotely control another computer's display. VNC transmits screen updates and keyboard/mouse events between client and server.

An AI-powered software agent that can interpret and respond to voice commands, performing tasks like answering questions, controlling devices, and managing schedules. Examples include Alexa, Siri, and Google Assistant.

An AI technique that replicates a specific person's voice from audio samples, enabling text-to-speech synthesis that sounds like the original speaker. It raises ethical concerns around deepfakes and consent.

A technology that identifies and processes human speech, converting spoken words into text or commands. It differs from speaker recognition, which identifies who is speaking rather than what is being said.

A user interface that allows interaction through spoken language, powered by speech recognition and natural language processing. Voice UIs are used in virtual assistants like Alexa, Siri, and Google Assistant.

A type that represents the absence of a value, used in languages like C, Java, and TypeScript for functions that do not return a value. In TypeScript, void indicates a function's return value should not be used.

Voice over Internet Protocol is a technology that delivers voice communication over the internet rather than traditional phone lines. VoIP services like Zoom and Teams have become essential for remote work and business communication.

A keyword in languages like Java and C that indicates a variable may be modified by multiple threads, preventing the compiler from caching its value. It ensures reads always fetch the latest value from main memory.

Computer memory that requires power to maintain stored data, losing all contents when power is removed. RAM is the most common example of volatile memory, used for fast temporary storage during computation.

A mechanism in containerization that maps a directory on the host filesystem to a directory inside a container, enabling data persistence and sharing. Docker volumes can be managed, bind-mounted, or tmpfs-based.

A point-in-time copy of a storage volume that captures its state for backup or replication purposes. Cloud providers offer snapshot capabilities for EBS, persistent disks, and managed storage services.

A display technology that creates visual representations of objects in three physical dimensions, allowing viewers to see the image from any angle. Unlike VR headsets, volumetric displays create free-standing 3D images visible to multiple viewers.

A volumetric pixel representing a value on a regular three-dimensional grid, analogous to a 2D pixel. Voxels are used in medical imaging, 3D modeling, and game engines for representing volumetric data.

Virtual Private Cloud — a logically isolated section of a cloud provider's network where you can launch resources in a virtual network that you define. VPCs provide control over IP addressing, subnets, routing, and security.

A networking connection between two Virtual Private Clouds that enables resources in each to communicate using private IP addresses. VPC peering is non-transitive and can be established within or across cloud regions.

Virtual Private Network — a technology that creates a secure, encrypted connection over a public network. VPNs protect privacy by masking IP addresses, securing data in transit, and enabling remote access to private networks.

A dedicated networking device that manages a large number of VPN tunnels simultaneously. VPN concentrators handle authentication, encryption, and decryption for enterprise remote access and site-to-site connections.

A VPN configuration that routes only specific traffic through the encrypted VPN tunnel while allowing other traffic to access the internet directly. It improves performance but can expose the network to security risks.
An encrypted connection between two network endpoints that creates a secure pathway through a public network. VPN tunnels encapsulate traffic using protocols like IPsec, WireGuard, or OpenVPN.

A form of motion sickness experienced during virtual reality use, caused by a mismatch between visual input and the vestibular system. Reducing latency, maintaining high frame rates, and limiting artificial locomotion help mitigate it.

Video Random Access Memory is dedicated high-speed memory on a graphics card used to store image data and textures for rendering. VRAM capacity is critical for running large AI models and high-resolution gaming.

Visual Studio Code is a free, open-source code editor developed by Microsoft, known for its extensive extension marketplace, integrated terminal, and IntelliSense. It has become the most popular code editor among developers.

Intel's performance profiling tool that helps developers optimize application performance by analyzing CPU usage, threading, memory access, and microarchitecture-level bottlenecks. VTune provides deep hardware-level insights.
The official routing library for Vue.js that enables navigation between pages in single-page applications. Vue Router supports nested routes, route guards, lazy loading, and dynamic route matching.

A progressive JavaScript framework for building user interfaces and single-page applications. Vue.js features a gentle learning curve, reactive data binding, component-based architecture, and an excellent developer experience.
A Material Design component framework for Vue.js that provides a comprehensive collection of pre-built UI components. Vuetify offers responsive layouts, theming, and accessibility out of the box.

A state management library for Vue.js applications that serves as a centralized store for all components. It follows a unidirectional data flow pattern with mutations, actions, and getters for predictable state changes.

A low-overhead, cross-platform graphics and compute API developed by the Khronos Group. Vulkan provides high-performance access to GPUs with more explicit control than OpenGL, making it popular for games and GPU computing.

A weakness or flaw in a system's design, implementation, or configuration that can be exploited by threat actors. Vulnerability management involves regular scanning, assessment, prioritization, and patching to maintain security posture.

A systematic process of identifying, quantifying, and prioritizing security vulnerabilities in systems, networks, and applications. It differs from penetration testing in that it focuses on discovery rather than exploitation.

A repository that catalogs known security vulnerabilities with standardized identifiers, descriptions, and severity ratings. Major databases include the National Vulnerability Database (NVD) and the CVE system.

The practice of reporting security vulnerabilities to affected vendors or the public, following responsible disclosure policies. Coordinated disclosure gives vendors time to patch before details are published.

The process of fixing or mitigating identified security vulnerabilities through patches, configuration changes, or compensating controls. Effective remediation requires prioritization based on risk severity and asset criticality.

Automated testing that probes systems, networks, and applications for known security weaknesses using tools like Nessus, OpenVAS, or Qualys. Regular scanning is a fundamental practice in security operations and compliance.

Virtual Extensible LAN is a network virtualization technology that encapsulates Layer 2 Ethernet frames in Layer 3 UDP packets. VXLAN enables creating up to 16 million virtual networks, solving the VLAN 4096 limit.

W.definition: The World Wide Web Consortium is the main international standards organization for the web, developing protocols and guidelines like HTML, CSS, and accessibility standards. W3C ensures long-term web interoperability.

Web Application Firewall — a security system that monitors and filters HTTP traffic between a web application and the internet. WAFs protect against common web exploits like SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), and DDoS attacks.

A Go framework for building desktop applications using web technologies for the frontend. Wails provides a lightweight alternative to Electron by using the system's native webview instead of bundling Chromium.

A concurrent algorithm that guarantees every thread will complete its operation in a bounded number of steps regardless of other threads' behavior. Wait-free algorithms provide the strongest progress guarantee in concurrent programming.
A specific phrase or word that activates a voice assistant or smart device, triggering it to begin listening for commands. Wake word detection runs continuously using lightweight on-device models.

Web Application Archive is a file format used to package and deploy Java web applications, containing servlets, JSP files, and configuration. WAR files are deployed to Java application servers like Tomcat or WildFly.

A cache that has been pre-populated with data, enabling faster response times for subsequent requests. Cache warming strategies preload frequently accessed data to avoid cold start latency after deployments or restarts.

A disaster recovery strategy where a scaled-down version of a production environment runs continuously and can be quickly scaled up during a failover. It balances cost and recovery time between cold and hot standby approaches.

The period after application startup during which performance gradually improves as caches are populated, JIT compilation occurs, and connection pools are established. Warm-up time is a consideration in scaling and deployment.

A modern, GPU-accelerated terminal emulator built with Rust, featuring AI-powered command suggestions, collaborative features, and a block-based interface. It aims to modernize the command-line experience.

A cloud storage service that provides S3-compatible hot storage at a significantly lower price than major cloud providers. Wasabi offers no egress fees and no API request charges, making it attractive for storage-heavy workloads.

WebAssembly System Interface — a modular system interface for WebAssembly that provides a standardized API for accessing operating system features like files, networking, and clocks in a portable and secure way.

WebAssembly — a binary instruction format for a stack-based virtual machine that enables near-native performance for web applications. WASM allows languages like C, C++, and Rust to run in the browser alongside JavaScript.

A specification that defines how WebAssembly modules can be composed together with well-defined interfaces. The Component Model enables language-agnostic interoperability and reusable Wasm components.
A virtual machine that executes WebAssembly bytecode outside the browser, enabling server-side and edge computing use cases. Popular runtimes include Wasmtime, Wasmer, and WasmEdge.

A fast and secure WebAssembly runtime developed by the Bytecode Alliance that runs Wasm outside the browser. Wasmtime supports the WASI standard for system interface access and is designed for production server-side use.

A development feature where tools automatically detect file changes and re-execute tasks like compilation, testing, or bundling. Watch mode provides instant feedback during development without manual rebuilds.

A hardware or software timer that triggers a system reset or corrective action if the monitored system fails to respond within a specified time. Watchdog timers prevent systems from hanging indefinitely.

A sequential software development methodology where each phase—requirements, design, implementation, testing, deployment—must be completed before the next begins. It contrasts with iterative approaches like Agile.

A visible or invisible marker embedded in digital content to indicate ownership, authenticity, or copyright. Digital watermarking is increasingly used in AI-generated content to distinguish it from human-created media.
A graphical representation of how a signal's amplitude varies over time, commonly used in audio processing and electronics. In software, waveform displays help users visualize and edit audio content.

A mathematical technique that decomposes a signal into components at different frequency scales, providing both time and frequency information. Wavelet transforms are used in image compression, denoising, and signal analysis.

A display server protocol intended as a simpler, more secure replacement for X11 on Linux. Wayland compositors handle both display server and window manager responsibilities, offering better performance and security.

Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — a set of international standards for making web content accessible to people with disabilities. WCAG defines principles (Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust) and success criteria at A, AA, and AAA conformance levels.

A consistency model where no guarantees are made about when writes become visible to other nodes in a distributed system. Weak consistency offers maximum performance at the cost of potentially stale reads.

A collection data structure in JavaScript where keys must be objects and are held weakly, meaning they don't prevent garbage collection. WeakMaps are useful for associating metadata with objects without causing memory leaks.

A reference to an object that does not prevent it from being garbage collected. Weak references are useful for caches and observer patterns where you want to reference objects without extending their lifetime.

A type system characteristic where the language performs implicit type conversions and allows operations between different types without explicit casting. JavaScript and PHP are examples of weakly typed languages.

A technique used in solid-state storage to distribute write and erase cycles evenly across memory cells, extending the device's lifespan. Without wear leveling, frequently written blocks would fail prematurely.

The World Wide Web — a system of interlinked hypertext documents and resources accessed via the internet using web browsers. The web is built on technologies including HTML, CSS, JavaScript, HTTP, and URLs.

The inclusive practice of ensuring websites and web applications are usable by people of all abilities and disabilities. Web accessibility follows standards like WCAG and involves semantic HTML, keyboard navigation, and screen reader support.

A browser API that provides a programmatic interface for creating and controlling animations, combining the performance of CSS animations with the flexibility of JavaScript. It offers fine-grained control over timing and playback.
An application programming interface exposed over HTTP that allows different software systems to communicate. Web APIs typically use REST or GraphQL patterns and exchange data in JSON or XML format.

A software application that runs in a web browser and is delivered over the internet. Web apps range from simple static sites to complex single-page applications and progressive web apps with offline capabilities.

A software application that runs in a web browser and is accessed over the internet, requiring no installation on the user's device. Modern web apps can offer experiences rivaling native applications through technologies like PWAs.

A stored snapshot of web content captured at a specific point in time for preservation and historical reference. Web archives use formats like WARC and are maintained by organizations like the Internet Archive.

A binary instruction format that enables high-performance code execution in web browsers. WebAssembly (Wasm) allows languages like C++, Rust, and Go to run in the browser at near-native speed, enabling complex applications like games, CAD tools, and video editors.

The use of software tools and scripts to automate interactions with web applications, including testing, scraping, form filling, and workflow automation. Tools like Selenium, Puppeteer, and Playwright enable browser automation.

A tiny, often invisible image or pixel embedded in web pages or emails to track user behavior like page visits and email opens. Also known as tracking pixels, they are commonly used in analytics and advertising.

An application for accessing and displaying content on the World Wide Web, interpreting HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Major browsers include Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge, each using different rendering engines.

A mechanism that stores copies of web resources to reduce server load, bandwidth usage, and latency for subsequent requests. Web caches operate at browser, proxy, and CDN levels using HTTP cache headers.

A set of standardized browser APIs including Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, and HTML Templates that enable the creation of reusable, encapsulated UI components. Web Components work across frameworks without library dependencies.
A set of web platform APIs that allow developers to create reusable custom HTML elements with encapsulated functionality. Web Components use Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, and HTML Templates standards.

JavaScript libraries that provide Web Components support for older browsers that don't natively implement Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, or HTML Imports. Polyfills enable wider browser compatibility for component-based development.

An automated program that systematically browses the web to index content for search engines or extract data. Also called spiders or bots, crawlers follow hyperlinks and respect rules defined in robots.txt.

A JavaScript API that provides cryptographic operations in web applications including hashing, signature generation, encryption, and key management. It runs in the browser without requiring external libraries.

The process of building and maintaining websites and web applications, encompassing front-end development, back-end development, and full-stack development. Web development uses HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and server-side technologies.

A browser add-on built using standard web technologies (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) and the WebExtensions API. The cross-browser standard allows extensions to work across Chrome, Firefox, and Edge with minimal changes.

A tracking technique that identifies users by collecting characteristics of their browser and device such as screen resolution, installed fonts, and WebGL rendering. Web fingerprinting can track users even without cookies.

A font file loaded over the internet for use on web pages, enabling consistent typography across devices regardless of locally installed fonts. Services like Google Fonts and Adobe Fonts host web fonts for easy integration.

A software framework that provides structure, tools, and libraries for building web applications, handling common tasks like routing, templating, and database interaction. Examples include Django, Rails, Express, and Next.js.

A service that provides the infrastructure and resources needed to make websites accessible on the internet. Hosting types range from shared and VPS hosting to dedicated servers and cloud-based solutions.

A built-in browser developer tool that allows inspection and modification of web page elements, CSS styles, JavaScript, network requests, and performance. Safari's Web Inspector and Chrome DevTools are popular implementations.

A JSON file that provides metadata about a progressive web application, including its name, icons, theme colors, and display mode. The manifest enables browsers to install PWAs and display them like native apps.

A proposed browser API that enables website owners to receive streaming micropayments from visitors as an alternative to advertising. It uses the Interledger Protocol to facilitate small, real-time payments.

The speed and efficiency with which web content is delivered and rendered in a browser, measured by metrics like LCP, FID, and CLS. Optimizing web performance improves user experience, SEO rankings, and conversion rates.

An intermediary server that forwards client requests to destination servers and returns the responses. Web proxies are used for caching, content filtering, anonymization, and bypassing geographic restrictions.
A message sent from a server to a user's browser or device even when the website is not open, using the Push API and Service Workers. Web push enables re-engagement without requiring a native app.

The software component in a browser responsible for parsing HTML, CSS, and JavaScript to display web pages visually. Major rendering engines include Blink (Chrome), Gecko (Firefox), and WebKit (Safari).

The automated extraction of data from websites using programs that parse HTML and simulate browser interactions. Web scraping is used for price monitoring, research, content aggregation, and training data collection, subject to legal and ethical considerations.

The practice of protecting websites, web applications, and web services from cyber threats including injection attacks, cross-site scripting, and data breaches. It encompasses secure coding, WAFs, HTTPS, and security headers.
Software that handles HTTP requests from clients and serves web content such as HTML pages, images, and API responses. Popular web servers include Nginx, Apache, and Caddy.
A software system designed to support interoperable machine-to-machine interaction over a network, using standard protocols like HTTP. Web services can follow REST, SOAP, or GraphQL paradigms.

Software systems designed to support interoperable machine-to-machine interaction over a network. Web services use standardized protocols like REST, SOAP, and GraphQL to exchange data between applications.

A period of sustained interaction between a user and a web application, tracked through session identifiers. Web sessions maintain state like authentication status, shopping cart contents, and user preferences across page loads.

Technical specifications published by standards bodies like W3C and WHATWG that define how web technologies should work. Adherence to web standards ensures cross-browser compatibility and accessibility.

A browser API that allows web applications to store key-value data locally on the client, including localStorage for persistent data and sessionStorage for session-scoped data. It provides more capacity than cookies.

A set of metrics defined by Google that measure real-world user experience on the web, including loading performance (LCP), interactivity (INP), and visual stability (CLS). Core Web Vitals are a ranking factor in Google Search.
A browser API that enables running JavaScript in background threads separate from the main execution thread. Web Workers prevent CPU-intensive operations from blocking the user interface.

A vision for a decentralized internet built on blockchain technology where users own their data and digital assets. Web3 encompasses decentralized applications, cryptocurrencies, NFTs, and decentralized autonomous organizations.

A portable binary code format and compilation target that enables high-performance applications on web pages. WebAssembly runs alongside JavaScript and provides near-native execution speed for computation-intensive tasks.

A modular system interface for WebAssembly that provides access to operating system-like features such as file systems, networking, and clocks. WASI enables Wasm to run outside the browser as a portable runtime.

A W3C standard that enables passwordless authentication using public key cryptography and hardware authenticators like security keys and biometric sensors. It is the foundation of passkey authentication.

A browser API that provides low-level access to built-in media encoders and decoders for video and audio processing. WebCodecs enables efficient media manipulation for applications like video editing and real-time communication.

Web Distributed Authoring and Versioning is an extension of HTTP that allows clients to create, modify, and move documents on a web server. It enables collaborative editing and file management over the web.

A protocol for discovering information about people and resources using their URI, commonly used in the Fediverse for user discovery. WebFinger enables looking up user profiles across federated social networks like Mastodon.

A JavaScript API for rendering interactive 2D and 3D graphics in the browser without plugins, based on OpenGL ES. WebGL enables GPU-accelerated content for games, data visualization, and creative applications.

A modern browser API that provides access to GPU capabilities for both rendering and general-purpose computation, succeeding WebGL. WebGPU offers a more explicit programming model aligned with Vulkan, Metal, and Direct3D 12.

A Web API that enables web applications to communicate with Human Interface Devices like game controllers, keyboards, and sensors over USB or Bluetooth. WebHID allows rich hardware interactions directly from the browser.

An HTTP callback that automatically sends data to a specified URL when a specific event occurs. Webhooks enable real-time communication between services — for example, notifying your app when a payment is received or a repository is updated.

A cryptographic signature included with webhook payloads that allows the receiver to verify the message's authenticity and integrity. Validating webhook signatures prevents accepting forged or tampered webhook deliveries.

A Web API that provides access to MIDI devices connected to a user's computer, enabling web applications to send and receive MIDI messages. WebMIDI powers browser-based music production and instrument control applications.
An image format developed by Google that provides superior lossless and lossy compression compared to PNG and JPEG. WebP images are typically 25-34% smaller, improving web page loading performance.

A powerful module bundler that processes and bundles JavaScript, CSS, images, and other assets for web applications. Webpack transforms, optimizes, and splits code for production, though newer tools like Vite and esbuild offer faster alternatives.

A Webpack 5 feature that allows multiple independently deployed builds to form a single application at runtime. Module Federation enables micro-frontend architectures by sharing code between separately built applications.

Web Real-Time Communication is an open-source project that provides browsers and mobile apps with real-time audio, video, and data communication capabilities via simple APIs. It enables peer-to-peer connections without plugins.

A communication protocol that provides full-duplex, bidirectional communication channels over a single TCP connection. WebSockets enable real-time features like live chat, gaming, collaborative editing, and streaming data without the overhead of repeated HTTP requests.

An open protocol for distributed publish-subscribe communication on the web, formerly known as PubSubHubbub. It enables real-time content delivery by notifying subscribers when publishers update their feeds.

A web API that provides low-latency, bidirectional client-server messaging using HTTP/3 and QUIC. WebTransport offers both reliable streams and unreliable datagrams, making it suitable for real-time applications.

An embeddable browser component that allows native mobile applications to display web content within the app. WebViews are used in hybrid app frameworks and for rendering HTML-based content in native apps.

A Web API that enables immersive virtual reality and augmented reality experiences in web browsers. WebXR provides access to VR headsets and AR capabilities without requiring native application installation.

A regularization technique that adds a penalty proportional to the magnitude of model weights to the loss function during training. Weight decay prevents overfitting by discouraging large weight values.

The method used to set the initial values of neural network parameters before training begins. Proper initialization (Xavier, He, etc.) is critical for stable training and avoiding vanishing or exploding gradients.

A graph where each edge has an associated numerical value representing cost, distance, or capacity. Weighted graphs are used with algorithms like Dijkstra's and Bellman-Ford for shortest path calculations.

An automatic speech recognition model developed by OpenAI, trained on a large dataset of diverse audio. Whisper provides robust multilingual transcription and translation capabilities across many languages and accents.

A software testing method where the tester has full knowledge of the internal structure and implementation of the code being tested. White box testing examines code paths, logic branches, and internal data flows.

A technical interview format where candidates solve programming problems or design systems on a whiteboard or shared document. While controversial, it remains common in tech hiring to assess problem-solving skills.
A product or service produced by one company that is rebranded and sold by another company as its own. Whitelabel solutions allow businesses to offer products without building them from scratch.

A security approach that explicitly permits only approved entities—such as IP addresses, applications, or email senders—while blocking everything else. Also called an allowlist, it provides stricter control than blacklist approaches.

A query and response protocol used to look up registration information for domain names, IP addresses, and autonomous systems. WHOIS records include registrant contact details, registration dates, and nameserver information.

A family of wireless networking protocols based on the IEEE 802.11 standards that allow devices to connect to local area networks and the internet without cables. Wi-Fi operates on 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz frequency bands.

A telecommunications network that extends over a large geographic area, connecting local area networks across cities, countries, or continents. The internet is the largest WAN, while enterprises use private WANs and SD-WAN.

A reusable UI element that performs a specific function, such as a date picker, slider, or progress bar. In mobile development, widgets can also appear on home screens to display information from apps.

A special character that matches one or more characters in pattern matching operations. Common wildcards include * (any characters) and ? (single character), used in file globbing, SQL queries, and DNS certificates.

An SQL function that performs calculations across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them into a single output. Window functions like ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and LAG enable complex analytics queries.
A technique in stream processing that groups events into finite time-based or count-based windows for aggregation. Common window types include tumbling, sliding, and session windows.

Microsoft's family of operating systems that dominates the desktop computing market. Windows provides a graphical user interface, multitasking capabilities, and a vast ecosystem of software and hardware support.
An AI-powered code editor built on top of VS Code that provides inline code generation and an integrated AI chat assistant. It represents the trend of AI-native development environments.

Windows Forms is a .NET GUI class library for building desktop applications on Windows. While older than WPF and .NET MAUI, WinForms remains popular for its simplicity in creating data-driven business applications.

A simplified visual guide that represents the skeletal framework of a website or application page. Wireframes focus on layout, content placement, and functionality without detailed visual design, serving as blueprints for development and stakeholder discussion.

A modern, lightweight VPN protocol designed for simplicity and high performance, using state-of-the-art cryptography. WireGuard's small codebase makes it easier to audit and faster than IPsec and OpenVPN.

An open-source network protocol analyzer that captures and inspects network traffic in real time. Wireshark is an essential tool for network troubleshooting, security analysis, and protocol development.
A UI design pattern that guides users through a complex process by breaking it into sequential steps. Wizards reduce cognitive load by presenting one manageable chunk of information at a time.

Web Open Font Format is a compressed font file format designed for use on the web. WOFF and WOFF2 provide smaller file sizes than raw font formats, improving page load performance while maintaining font quality.

A learned representation of text where words with similar meanings have similar vector representations. Word2Vec, GloVe, and FastText are popular algorithms that map words into dense, low-dimensional vector spaces.

A group of neural network models that produce word embeddings by training on large text corpora. Word2Vec represents words as dense vectors in a continuous vector space where semantically similar words are closer together.

An open-source content management system that powers over 40% of all websites on the internet. Originally a blogging platform, WordPress has evolved into a full CMS with extensive plugin and theme ecosystems.

A scheduling algorithm for parallel computing where idle threads steal tasks from busy threads' work queues. It improves load balancing and CPU utilization in multi-threaded runtimes like Java's ForkJoinPool and Tokio.

A background process or thread that performs tasks independently from the main execution context. Web Workers enable CPU-intensive JavaScript processing without blocking the UI, while service workers power offline functionality and push notifications.

A background thread dedicated to performing tasks from a queue, separating task submission from execution. Worker threads are the building blocks of thread pools used in server applications and parallel processing.

A defined sequence of automated tasks that execute in response to triggers, commonly used in CI/CD pipelines and business process automation. GitHub Actions, Argo Workflows, and Apache Airflow are popular workflow engines.

The use of technology to automate repetitive business processes, reducing manual effort and human error. Workflow automation tools like Zapier, n8n, and Make connect applications and trigger actions based on predefined rules and conditions.
Software that manages and executes defined sequences of tasks, handling scheduling, dependency resolution, retries, and error handling. Examples include Temporal, Apache Airflow, and Prefect.
In Git, the directory where you edit files, representing the current state of your project's files on disk. The working tree is compared against the staging area and the last commit to show changes.

A distinct capability or unit of work running on cloud infrastructure, such as a web server, database, or batch processing job. Cloud providers offer different instance types and services optimized for various workload patterns.

A cloud security feature that allows Kubernetes pods and cloud services to authenticate to APIs using managed identities instead of static credentials. It eliminates the need to store and rotate service account keys.

A feature in package managers and IDEs that allows managing multiple related projects in a single repository. Monorepo tools like npm workspaces, Yarn workspaces, and Cargo workspaces share dependencies across packages.

A high-performance computer designed for technical or scientific applications requiring significant processing power, memory, and graphics capabilities. Workstations are used for software development, 3D rendering, and data analysis.

The third generation of Wi-Fi Protected Access security protocol, providing stronger encryption, protection against brute-force attacks, and improved security for open networks. WPA3 uses SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) for key exchange.

Windows Presentation Foundation is a Microsoft UI framework for building desktop applications with rich visuals using XAML markup. It supports data binding, hardware-accelerated graphics, and the MVVM pattern.
Words Per Minute is a measure of typing or reading speed commonly used in productivity assessments. Average typing speed is about 40 WPM, while professional typists often exceed 80 WPM.

The official CLI tool for developing and deploying Cloudflare Workers, managing KV stores, R2 buckets, and other Cloudflare services. It provides local development, testing, and deployment capabilities.

A function, class, or library that encapsulates and simplifies the interface to another piece of code. Wrappers provide abstraction layers, adapt incompatible interfaces, or add functionality to existing components.

A phenomenon where the actual amount of data written to storage media is significantly larger than the data the application intended to write. Write amplification is a key concern for SSD performance and longevity.

A concurrency anomaly in database systems where two transactions read overlapping data, make decisions based on what they read, and write non-overlapping data, leading to an inconsistent state. Serializable isolation prevents write skew.

A condition in concurrent systems where writer threads are perpetually blocked because reader threads continuously hold shared locks. Write starvation occurs in reader-writer lock implementations that prioritize readers.

A technique used in databases where changes are written to a log before being applied to the main data files, ensuring durability and crash recovery. WAL is fundamental to databases like PostgreSQL and SQLite.

A caching strategy where data is written to the cache first and only written to the backing store at a later time. It offers better write performance than write-through but risks data loss on cache failure.
A caching strategy where data is written to both the cache and the backing store simultaneously. It ensures consistency between cache and storage at the cost of higher write latency.
Web Services Description Language is an XML-based language for describing the functionality, messages, and bindings of web services. WSDL is primarily associated with SOAP-based web services.

Web Server Gateway Interface is a specification that defines how web servers communicate with Python web applications. WSGI enables interoperability between different Python web frameworks and servers.

What You See Is What You Get refers to editors where content displayed during editing closely resembles the final output. WYSIWYG editors are common in web design, document editing, and email marketing tools.

X.definition: A system service that manages graphical login sessions on X Window System displays. Display managers like GDM, LightDM, and SDDM present the login screen and start the desktop environment after authentication.

The display server component of the X Window System that manages graphical output and input devices on Unix-like systems. The X Server handles window rendering, keyboard, and mouse events for client applications.

A windowing system for bitmap displays that provides the framework for GUI environments on Unix and Linux. The X Window System uses a client-server model where the display server manages screen output and user input.

An Nginx feature that allows backend applications to delegate file serving to Nginx by setting a special response header. It enables efficient protected file downloads without tying up application processes.

A custom HTTP header commonly used to pass an API key for authentication when making API requests. While simpler than OAuth, API keys provide basic access control for identifying calling applications.

An HTTP security header that prevents browsers from MIME-sniffing the content type away from the declared Content-Type. Setting it to 'nosniff' mitigates attacks that exploit incorrect content type interpretation.

An HTTP header used to identify the originating IP address of a client connecting through proxies or load balancers. It contains a comma-separated list of IP addresses from the client to the last proxy.

An HTTP response header that controls whether a browser is allowed to render a page inside a frame, iframe, or object element. X-Frame-Options prevents clickjacking attacks by restricting page embedding.

AWS X-Ray is a distributed tracing service that helps developers analyze and debug microservices architectures by tracking requests as they flow through the system. It provides a service map and identifies performance bottlenecks.
An HTTP header commonly set to XMLHttpRequest by JavaScript frameworks to identify AJAX requests. Servers use this header to distinguish between regular page loads and asynchronous API calls.

An HTTP response header that controls how search engine crawlers index and display content from a page. It provides the same directives as the robots meta tag but can be applied to non-HTML resources like PDFs.

An early packet-switched network protocol standard from the ITU-T that was widely used before TCP/IP became dominant. X.25 provided reliable communication over unreliable physical networks and influenced later protocol development.

An international standard defining the format of public key certificates used in SSL/TLS encryption and digital signatures. X.509 certificates verify the identity of entities on the internet and are essential for HTTPS, email security, and code signing.

An ITU-T standard defining the format of public key certificates used in TLS/SSL, code signing, and email encryption. Each certificate binds a public key to an identity via a certificate authority's signature.

The eleventh version of the X Window System protocol, the most widely deployed version used on Unix-like operating systems. X11 provides network-transparent windowing and is being gradually replaced by Wayland.

A feature of SSH that allows graphical applications running on a remote server to display their windows on the local machine. X11 forwarding tunnels X Window System protocol over an encrypted SSH connection.

A family of instruction set architectures based on the Intel 8086 processor, dominating desktop and server computing for decades. x86-64 (AMD64) extends the architecture to 64-bit, though ARM processors are increasingly challenging its dominance in both mobile and server markets.
The 64-bit extension of the x86 instruction set architecture, also known as AMD64 or x64. It supports larger memory addressing, more registers, and wider data paths than 32-bit x86 processors.

Everything as a Service — an umbrella term for the growing number of services delivered over the internet on a subscription basis. XaaS encompasses SaaS, PaaS, IaaS, and emerging models like AIaaS, DaaS, and FaaS.

eXtensible Access Control Markup Language is an XML-based standard for defining access control policies. XACML provides a fine-grained, attribute-based approach to authorization decisions in enterprise systems.

Explainable Artificial Intelligence — methods and techniques that make AI model decisions interpretable and understandable to humans. XAI addresses the black-box nature of complex models and is essential for regulated industries.

Extensible Application Markup Language is a declarative XML-based language developed by Microsoft for defining user interfaces in WPF, UWP, and .NET MAUI applications. It separates UI definition from business logic.

A Python library for working with labeled multi-dimensional arrays, extending NumPy with dimension names, coordinates, and metadata. Xarray is widely used in climate science, oceanography, and geospatial data analysis.

eXtensible Business Reporting Language is an XML-based standard for exchanging business and financial information. XBRL enables automated processing of financial statements and is required by many regulatory agencies.

An Apple bundle format that packages compiled libraries for multiple platforms and architectures in a single distributable artifact. XCFrameworks support iOS, macOS, tvOS, and watchOS with both simulator and device variants.

Apple's integrated development environment for building applications across all Apple platforms — iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS. Xcode includes a code editor, Interface Builder, simulator, debugging tools, and performance instruments.

A performance analysis and testing toolset bundled with Xcode for profiling iOS and macOS applications. Instruments provides tools for measuring CPU usage, memory allocation, energy impact, and network activity.
Apple's testing framework for writing unit tests, integration tests, and UI tests for Swift and Objective-C applications. XCTest integrates with Xcode and supports test-driven development workflows.

Apple's UI testing framework that allows automated interaction with iOS and macOS application interfaces. XCUITest uses accessibility identifiers to locate elements and supports recording user interactions as test code.

A PHP extension that provides debugging and profiling capabilities, including stack traces, breakpoints, and code coverage analysis. Xdebug integrates with IDEs like PhpStorm and VS Code for interactive debugging.

A freedesktop.org specification that defines standard locations for user-specific configuration, data, and cache files on Unix systems. Following XDG conventions prevents cluttering the home directory with dotfiles.

A freedesktop.org interface that allows sandboxed applications to request access to host system resources like files, printing, and screenshots through user-mediated dialogs. Portals are essential for Flatpak and Snap applications.

eXpress Data Path is a high-performance programmable network data path in the Linux kernel that enables packet processing at the earliest possible point. XDP programs use eBPF and can process millions of packets per second.

External Data Representation — a platform-independent serialization standard defined by RFC 4506 for encoding data structures in a portable binary format, originally used in Sun RPC and NFS protocols.
An open-source type-1 hypervisor that allows multiple operating systems to run concurrently on a single physical machine. Xen was foundational to early cloud platforms including Amazon EC2.

An open-source type-1 hypervisor that enables multiple virtual machines to run on a single physical host. Xen is used by major cloud providers including AWS, which built its early infrastructure on Xen.

A lightweight open-source desktop environment for Unix-like operating systems that emphasizes speed and low resource usage. XFCE provides a complete desktop experience while remaining suitable for older hardware.
A high-performance 64-bit journaling file system originally developed by SGI and now commonly used in Linux. XFS excels at parallel I/O operations and is the default filesystem in RHEL and CentOS.

The metadata journaling subsystem of the XFS filesystem that records pending changes in a write-ahead log, enabling rapid crash recovery by replaying only uncommitted transactions instead of scanning the entire filesystem.

Extreme Gradient Boosting — an optimized, scalable machine learning library for gradient boosted decision trees. XGBoost is renowned for its speed and performance in structured data competitions and production ML systems for classification and regression tasks.

XMLHttpRequest is a browser API for making HTTP requests from JavaScript without reloading the page. While largely replaced by the Fetch API, XHR was the original technology enabling AJAX and dynamic web applications.

A technique where the browser repeatedly sends XMLHttpRequest calls at regular intervals to check for server updates. XHR polling is a simple but inefficient method for near-real-time communication, largely replaced by WebSockets.

Extensible HyperText Markup Language is a reformulation of HTML as valid XML, requiring stricter syntax like properly closed tags and lowercase element names. While HTML5 superseded it, XHTML influenced modern coding practices.

A semiconductor company (now part of AMD) that pioneered field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) and adaptive computing. Xilinx FPGAs and SoCs are widely used in data centers, communications, and embedded systems.

A W3C specification that provides a mechanism for merging XML documents by including content from other XML or text resources. XInclude simplifies the management of large XML documents by enabling modular composition.

A popular webcomic by Randall Munroe frequently referenced in tech culture for its humor about programming, mathematics, and science. Several tech concepts like XKCD-style password generation have been inspired by it.

Accelerated Linear Algebra is a compiler for linear algebra that optimizes computations on TPUs and GPUs. Developed by Google, XLA compiles TensorFlow and JAX operations into efficient machine code for hardware accelerators.

Accelerated Linear Algebra — a domain-specific compiler developed by Google that optimizes TensorFlow computations by fusing operations, eliminating redundancies, and generating efficient machine code for GPUs and TPUs.
XML Localization Interchange File Format — an XML-based format for exchanging localization data between tools during the translation process. XLIFF standardizes how translatable text is represented.

A generalized autoregressive pretraining method that captures bidirectional contexts by using permutation-based training. XLNet outperformed BERT on several NLP benchmarks by addressing its limitations with masked language modeling.
The XML-based file format used by Microsoft Excel for spreadsheet documents since Office 2007. XLSX files are actually ZIP archives containing XML files that define worksheets, styles, and formulas.
A lightweight cross-platform build utility based on Lua that simplifies project building and package management. XMake supports C, C++, Rust, and other languages with a simpler syntax than CMake.

Extensible Markup Language — a flexible, human-readable format for encoding structured data. While largely superseded by JSON for web APIs, XML remains important in enterprise systems, configuration files (like Android layouts), RSS feeds, and document formats.

The process of mapping XML document elements and attributes to programming language objects and vice versa. XML binding frameworks like JAXB automatically generate code to serialize and deserialize XML data.
A process of converting XML documents to a canonical form so that two logically equivalent documents produce identical byte sequences. This is essential for XML digital signatures to be verifiable.
A W3C and IETF standard for creating digital signatures in XML syntax, supporting enveloped, enveloping, and detached signatures to ensure document integrity and signer authentication.

A W3C specification that defines how to encrypt and decrypt XML content, providing element-level or document-level confidentiality. XML Encryption enables selective encryption of sensitive data within XML documents.
A denial-of-service attack exploiting XML parsers by defining nested entity references that expand exponentially, consuming excessive memory. The Billion Laughs attack is the most well-known example.

A mechanism for qualifying element and attribute names in XML documents to avoid naming conflicts when combining multiple XML vocabularies. Namespaces use URIs as unique identifiers prefixed to element names.

A software component that reads XML documents and provides access to their content and structure. Common parsing approaches include DOM (loads entire document), SAX (event-driven), and StAX (streaming pull-based).
A language for defining the structure, content, and data types of XML documents, also known as XSD. It provides more expressive constraints than DTDs, including data typing and namespace support.
A remote procedure call protocol that uses XML to encode calls and HTTP as a transport mechanism. XML-RPC was a precursor to SOAP and is still used in legacy systems like older WordPress APIs.

An early file transfer protocol for serial communication that sends data in 128-byte blocks with error checking. XMODEM was widely used in the bulletin board system era and influenced later protocols like YMODEM and ZMODEM.

Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol — an open communication protocol for real-time messaging, presence information, and contact list maintenance. XMPP powers instant messaging systems and IoT communication with its decentralized, extensible architecture.

An XMPP extension protocol implementing publish-subscribe messaging where publishers push items to topic nodes and subscribers receive real-time notifications, used for activity feeds, IoT telemetry, and event distribution.
A server implementing the XMPP protocol for handling real-time messaging, presence, and federation between domains. Popular XMPP servers include ejabberd, Prosody, and Openfire.
The basic unit of communication in the XMPP protocol, consisting of XML elements exchanged between clients and servers. The three core stanza types are message, presence, and iq (info/query).
The hybrid kernel used by Apple's operating systems including macOS, iOS, and iPadOS. XNU combines the Mach microkernel, components from BSD, and an object-oriented I/O framework called IOKit.

A software flow control method for serial communication where XON signals the sender to resume transmission and XOFF signals it to pause. It is one of the simplest forms of flow control for serial data links.
Exclusive OR is a logical operation that returns true when inputs differ and false when they are the same. XOR is fundamental in cryptography, error detection, and bitwise operations in programming.

A simple encryption algorithm that combines plaintext with a key using the XOR operation. While trivially breakable alone, XOR operations are fundamental building blocks in modern cryptographic algorithms.

An algorithm that swaps two values using XOR operations without requiring a temporary variable. While clever, modern compilers optimize regular swap operations better, making XOR swap largely a curiosity.

Extreme Programming — an agile software development methodology that emphasizes technical practices like pair programming, test-driven development, continuous integration, and frequent releases. XP values communication, simplicity, feedback, and courage.

A query language for selecting nodes from an XML or HTML document tree. XPath expressions navigate through elements and attributes, commonly used in web scraping, XSLT transformations, and automated testing.
A query and functional programming language designed to query and transform collections of structured and unstructured data, especially XML. XQuery is to XML what SQL is to relational databases.

Extended Reality is an umbrella term encompassing virtual reality (VR), augmented reality (AR), and mixed reality (MR). XR technologies merge physical and digital worlds to create immersive experiences.

The process of verifying that an XML document conforms to the structure, data types, and constraints defined in an XML Schema Definition. XSD validation ensures data integrity in XML-based data exchange.

Extensible Stylesheet Language Transformations — a language for transforming XML documents into other formats like HTML, text, or different XML structures. XSLT uses template-based pattern matching to process and reshape XML data.

A software component that applies XSLT stylesheets to XML documents to transform them into other formats like HTML, PDF, or different XML structures. Common processors include Saxon, Xalan, and built-in browser engines.

Cross-Site Request Forgery, also known as CSRF, is an attack that tricks an authenticated user's browser into sending unintended requests to a web application. Protection methods include anti-CSRF tokens and SameSite cookies.

Cross-Site Scripting — a web security vulnerability where attackers inject malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users. XSS attacks can steal cookies, session tokens, and sensitive data, prevented through input sanitization and Content Security Policies.

A security mechanism that detects and blocks cross-site scripting attacks by sanitizing user input and output encoding. Modern approaches use Content Security Policy headers and context-aware output encoding.

Techniques and practices for defending web applications against cross-site scripting attacks. Prevention methods include output encoding, Content Security Policy headers, input validation, and using safe DOM APIs.

A JavaScript and TypeScript library for creating, interpreting, and executing finite state machines and statecharts. XState provides a formal model for managing complex application state and transitions.

Extended Tiny Encryption Algorithm — a block cipher designed as an improvement over the TEA algorithm, addressing its key-schedule weakness. XTEA is simple to implement and used in resource-constrained environments.

A configurable processor architecture developed by Cadence Design Systems, widely used in the ESP32 microcontroller family. Xtensa cores are popular in IoT devices due to their low power consumption and flexibility.

The standard terminal emulator for the X Window System that has been in development since 1984. Xterm.js is a modern TypeScript library that provides a terminal frontend for web applications like VS Code's integrated terminal.
A protocol that provides an additional layer of encryption by multiplexing TLS connections within TLS tunnels. XTLS improves privacy by making traffic analysis more difficult.

A family of unit testing frameworks sharing a common architecture, including JUnit for Java, NUnit for .NET, and pytest for Python. The xUnit pattern defines test fixtures, assertions, and test runners.

A simplified Unix-like teaching operating system developed at MIT as a re-implementation of Unix V6 in ANSI C for x86 processors. Xv6 is widely used in university courses to teach operating system concepts.
