Y.definition: A higher-order function in lambda calculus that enables recursion in languages that do not natively support it by allowing anonymous functions to call themselves. The Y combinator is a fundamental concept in theoretical computer science.

The vertical axis in a two-dimensional chart or graph, typically representing the dependent variable or measurement values. Proper Y-axis scaling and labeling are critical for honest and clear data visualization.

A scalability technique that splits an application into multiple services based on functionality, with each service responsible for a different set of features. It is one of the three scaling axes in the Scale Cube model.

A scaling strategy that splits an application into multiple different services, each responsible for a specific function. It is one of the three scaling dimensions in the Scale Cube model alongside X-axis and Z-axis scaling.

A higher-order function in lambda calculus that enables recursion in languages that don't natively support it. The Y combinator finds the fixed point of a function, allowing anonymous recursive functions.

A free online program by Y Combinator that provides startup education, mentorship, and community to founders building companies. It covers essential topics like product-market fit, fundraising, and growth strategies.

The Year 2000 problem was a computer bug where two-digit year representations could cause errors when the date rolled from 1999 to 2000. It prompted massive remediation efforts and highlighted the importance of forward-thinking data design.

You Aren't Gonna Need It — a software development principle that advises against implementing functionality until it is actually needed. YAGNI prevents over-engineering, reduces complexity, and keeps codebases lean and maintainable.

You Aren't Gonna Need It — an Extreme Programming principle stating that developers should not implement functionality until it is actually needed, avoiding speculative features that increase complexity and maintenance burden.

An interface for accessing financial market data including stock prices, historical data, and company information. It is widely used by developers and data scientists for building financial analysis tools and trading algorithms.

YAML Ain't Markup Language — a human-readable data serialization format commonly used for configuration files and data exchange. YAML's clean, indentation-based syntax makes it popular for Kubernetes manifests, CI/CD pipelines, and Docker Compose files.

A YAML feature that allows defining a block of configuration once and reusing it throughout the document using anchors (&) and aliases (*). Anchors reduce duplication in CI/CD configurations and Kubernetes manifests.

The practice of using YAML files to define application settings, infrastructure, and deployment parameters. YAML's readable syntax makes it the preferred format for tools like Kubernetes, Docker Compose, and CI/CD pipelines.

Metadata embedded at the top of a file between triple-dashed lines, written in YAML format. Front matter is commonly used in static site generators like Jekyll and Hugo to define page titles, dates, and custom properties.

The ability to reference and substitute variable values within YAML files using special syntax. YAML interpolation is supported by tools like Docker Compose and Ansible to create dynamic, parameterized configurations.

A tool that checks YAML files for syntax errors, formatting issues, and best practice violations. YAML linters like yamllint help prevent deployment failures caused by indentation errors and invalid syntax.
The process of analyzing YAML files for syntax errors, formatting issues, and best practice violations. YAML linters catch common mistakes like incorrect indentation and invalid data types.

A YAML feature using the << key that allows merging mappings from anchored nodes into the current mapping. It enables DRY configuration by sharing common settings across multiple configuration blocks.

YAML syntax for representing multiline strings using literal block scalar (|) that preserves newlines or folded block scalar (>) that joins lines. Understanding multiline syntax is essential for clean configuration files.

A CI/CD pipeline defined as code in a YAML file, used by platforms like Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions, and GitLab CI. YAML pipelines enable version-controlled, reviewable, and reproducible build and deployment processes.

A restricted YAML parsing mode that prevents deserialization of arbitrary objects, mitigating remote code execution vulnerabilities caused by unsafe YAML loaders constructing language-specific objects from untrusted input.

A specification that defines the expected structure, types, and constraints for a YAML document. YAML schemas enable validation, autocompletion, and documentation for configuration files in editors and CI pipelines.

YAML directives that explicitly specify the data type of a value using the ! or !! prefix notation. Tags enable custom type handling and disambiguation when the default type inference is insufficient.

A preprocessor that adds variable substitution, conditionals, and loops to YAML files, enabling dynamic generation of configuration for Kubernetes manifests, CI/CD pipelines, and infrastructure definitions.

A tool that checks YAML files for correctness against a schema or specification. YAML validators ensure configuration files are properly formatted before deployment, catching errors that could cause runtime failures.

A Russian multinational technology company providing internet-related products and services including a search engine, cloud platform, maps, and AI research. It operates the most popular search engine in Russian-speaking countries.

Search ranking algorithms developed by Yandex that incorporate machine learning models like MatrixNet for web search relevance. These algorithms handle multilingual content and morphologically rich languages.

A public cloud platform operated by Yandex that provides compute, storage, database, and machine learning services. Yandex Cloud serves customers primarily in Russia, CIS countries, and parts of Europe and Asia.
A command in Vim and other text editors that copies selected text into a register without deleting it. The yanked text can then be pasted elsewhere, forming a core part of the Vim editing workflow.

A tool for identifying and classifying malware by creating rules that describe patterns found in malicious files. Security researchers use YARA rules to detect malware families, hunt for threats, and automate incident response across large file sets.

A specific pattern definition written in the YARA rule language that describes the characteristics of malware families or suspicious files. YARA rules match binary patterns, strings, and conditions to identify threats.

A tool for identifying and classifying malware samples by creating descriptions of malware families based on textual or binary patterns. YARA rules are widely used in threat hunting and incident response.

A detection language developed by Google for Chronicle SIEM that extends YARA's pattern-matching capabilities to correlate events across multiple log sources over time windows for advanced threat detection.

A fast, reliable JavaScript package manager developed by Meta as an alternative to npm. Yarn introduced features like deterministic dependency resolution, offline caching, and workspaces for managing monorepos.

Yet Another Resource Negotiator — the resource management layer of Apache Hadoop that manages and schedules computing resources across a cluster. YARN separates resource management from job scheduling, enabling diverse workloads beyond MapReduce.

A per-application process in Hadoop YARN responsible for negotiating resources from the ResourceManager and coordinating task execution with NodeManagers. Each YARN application has its own Application Master.

The modern version of the Yarn package manager (v2+) that introduces Plug'n'Play installation strategy, eliminating node_modules by mapping dependencies directly. It provides stricter dependency resolution and zero-installs capability.

A local storage mechanism where Yarn saves downloaded packages to avoid re-downloading them on subsequent installs. The yarn cache speeds up dependency installation and enables offline package installation.
The unit of resource allocation in Hadoop YARN, representing a fixed amount of CPU and memory on a cluster node. Applications request containers from the ResourceManager to execute their tasks.

A feature of Hadoop YARN that allows multiple YARN clusters to be combined into a single federated cluster. It enables scaling beyond the limits of a single cluster while maintaining a unified resource management interface.
A file (yarn.lock) that records the exact versions of all installed dependencies in a Yarn project. It ensures deterministic installs across different machines and environments.

A per-node agent in Hadoop YARN that manages containers, monitors resource usage, and reports node health to the ResourceManager. NodeManagers are responsible for launching and managing application containers.
The per-node agent in Apache Hadoop YARN responsible for launching and monitoring containers, tracking resource usage, and reporting health and availability back to the ResourceManager.

Yarn Plug'n'Play is an installation strategy that eliminates the node_modules directory by using a single .pnp.cjs file to map package imports to their locations. PnP improves installation speed and disk usage.

A resource allocation mechanism in Hadoop YARN that partitions cluster resources among different teams, applications, or workloads. Queues support hierarchical organization and configurable scheduling policies.

The central authority in Hadoop YARN that manages cluster resources and schedules applications. The ResourceManager allocates resources to applications, monitors node health, and enforces resource policies.
The master daemon in Apache Hadoop YARN that arbitrates resources among all running applications, managing cluster-wide scheduling and tracking available capacity across NodeManagers.
The component of the YARN ResourceManager responsible for allocating resources to applications based on configured policies. Common schedulers include the Fair Scheduler and Capacity Scheduler.

A Yarn feature for managing multiple packages within a single repository, sharing dependencies and enabling cross-package references. Yarn workspaces are commonly used in monorepo setups to reduce duplication and simplify development.

A color encoding system used in digital video where Y represents luminance and Cb/Cr represent blue-difference and red-difference chrominance components, enabling efficient compression by subsampling color channels.

A computing limitation where 32-bit systems storing Unix time as a signed integer will overflow on January 19, 2038. Similar to Y2K, this affects embedded systems and legacy software that haven't been updated to use 64-bit timestamps.

A method of evaluating metrics by comparing results from one period to the same period in the previous year. Year-over-year analysis eliminates seasonal variations and reveals true growth trends in business data.

A monitoring pattern where synthetic transactions are continuously run against production systems to detect failures before real users encounter them, named after canaries used to detect gas in coal mines.

An older name for the Network Information Service (NIS), a directory service protocol for distributing system configuration data across Unix networks. NIS has been largely replaced by LDAP and Active Directory.

The default error page displayed by ASP.NET applications when an unhandled exception occurs, showing a yellow background with error details. It should be disabled in production to prevent information leakage.

A security team focused on building secure systems by combining development expertise with security awareness. Yellow teams bridge the gap between builders and security testers, embedding security into the development process.

A deployment strategy similar to blue-green where traffic is gradually shifted from the current (yellow) environment to the new (green) environment. It provides zero-downtime deployments with easy rollback capability.

An open-source Python library that provides visual analysis and diagnostic tools for machine learning. Yellowbrick extends scikit-learn with visualizations for feature analysis, model selection, and evaluation, making ML workflows more interpretable.

A large-scale dataset released by Yelp containing millions of reviews, business attributes, and user data. It is commonly used in academic research and machine learning projects for sentiment analysis and recommendation systems.
A RESTful API that provides access to Yelp's business listing data including reviews, ratings, and location information. Developers use it to integrate local business search into applications.

A scaffolding tool that generates project boilerplate code using community-created generators. While less popular now, Yeoman established patterns for project initialization that influenced modern CLI tools like Create React App.

A pluggable scaffold template for the Yeoman workflow tool that generates project boilerplates with opinionated directory structures, build configurations, and dependency setups for various frameworks and languages.

YACC is a Unix utility that generates a parser from a formal grammar specification. It produces C code that can parse input according to the defined grammar rules, commonly used with the Lex lexical analyzer.

The original backronym for YAML before it was recursively renamed to YAML Ain't Markup Language to emphasize its data-oriented rather than document-oriented nature. YAML focuses on data serialization rather than text markup.

A Rust framework for building web applications with WebAssembly, providing a component-based architecture similar to React. Yew enables writing frontend code in Rust with type safety and performance benefits.

A keyword used in generator functions that pauses execution and returns a value to the caller, maintaining state between calls. Yield enables lazy evaluation, infinite sequences, and cooperative multitasking in languages like Python, JavaScript, and C#.

The statistical modeling and visualization of the relationship between interest rates and bond maturities. In fintech, yield curve analysis uses data science techniques to forecast economic conditions.

A decentralized finance strategy where cryptocurrency holders lend or stake their assets in DeFi protocols to earn returns in the form of additional tokens or interest. Yield farming drives liquidity in DeFi ecosystems.

A keyword in languages like Python, JavaScript, and C# that pauses a function's execution and returns a value to the caller, enabling generator functions. Yield allows lazy evaluation and efficient handling of large data sequences.
A variable pricing strategy that adjusts prices based on predicted demand to maximize revenue. Used extensively in cloud computing for spot instances and dynamic resource pricing.

A high-performance PHP framework for developing web applications following the MVC pattern. Yii provides code generation, caching, authentication, and database access tools for rapid application development.

A high-performance CRDT implementation for building collaborative applications that supports real-time synchronization. Yjs enables conflict-free concurrent editing and works with various editors and frameworks.

The underlying conflict-free replicated data type algorithm in the Yjs library that enables real-time collaborative editing by merging concurrent document changes without a central server or operational transforms.
You Need A Budget is a personal budgeting application that follows a zero-based budgeting methodology. Its API allows developers to build integrations and tools around personal financial data.

A popular WordPress plugin that helps optimize websites for search engines by analyzing content readability, keyword usage, and technical SEO factors. It provides real-time suggestions and manages meta tags and sitemaps.

A collection of related metadata in the Yocto Project build system that provides recipes, configurations, and classes for building embedded Linux distributions. Layers enable modular and reusable build configurations.

An open-source collaboration project that provides tools and processes for creating custom Linux distributions for embedded systems. Yocto uses a layer-based architecture and BitBake build system to generate optimized images.
A distributed full-text search platform built on top of Apache Solr integrated into Riak KV, enabling Solr-powered queries across a distributed key-value store without a separate search cluster.

You Only Look Once — a real-time object detection algorithm that processes entire images in a single pass through a neural network. YOLO achieves impressive speed-accuracy tradeoffs and is widely used in autonomous vehicles, surveillance, and robotics.

A one-stage object detection architecture that frames detection as a single regression problem, predicting bounding boxes and class probabilities directly from full images in one evaluation. This approach enables real-time detection speeds.

The third version of the You Only Look Once real-time object detection system that introduced multi-scale detection using three detection heads. YOLOv3 improved small object detection while maintaining high inference speed.

A popular PyTorch-based implementation of the YOLO object detection family, known for its ease of use and strong community support. YOLOv5 introduced features like auto-learning anchors and model export to multiple formats.

A version of the YOLO object detection family that introduced trainable bag-of-freebies techniques and architectural improvements. YOLOv7 achieved state-of-the-art speed and accuracy trade-offs for real-time detection.

The eighth iteration of the You Only Look Once real-time object detection model, developed by Ultralytics. YOLOv8 supports detection, segmentation, classification, and pose estimation with improved accuracy and speed.

A recent iteration of the YOLO object detection series that introduced Programmable Gradient Information and Generalized Efficient Layer Aggregation Network. YOLOv9 improved accuracy while reducing parameter count.
An open-source framework for Verilog RTL synthesis that converts hardware description language code into gate-level netlists. Yosys is a key tool in the open-source FPGA and ASIC design toolchain.

A unit of digital information equal to one septillion (10²⁴) bytes or 1,000 zettabytes. To put it in perspective, all data ever created by humanity is estimated at around 120 zettabytes — less than one yottabyte.

Computing at the yottabyte scale, representing the frontier of data processing and storage capabilities. The term describes the massive infrastructure needed to handle the world's ever-growing data generation.

A set of APIs provided by Google that allows developers to integrate YouTube functionality into applications, including video search, upload, playback, and analytics. It supports both data access and embedded player customization.

Google's REST API that provides programmatic access to YouTube data including videos, playlists, channels, and comments. Developers use it to integrate YouTube search, upload, and analytics into applications.

A command-line YAML, JSON, and XML processor similar to jq but for YAML data. yq enables querying, filtering, and transforming YAML files from the command line, useful for scripting and CI/CD pipelines.

A cloud-based YubiKey OTP validation service operated by Yubico that verifies one-time passwords generated by YubiKeys. It provides free authentication infrastructure for applications integrating YubiKey support.

A hardware security module by Yubico designed for server-side cryptographic key protection. YubiHSM provides secure key storage, signing, and encryption operations in a compact USB form factor at a lower cost than traditional HSMs.

A hardware security key manufactured by Yubico that provides strong two-factor authentication. YubiKeys support multiple protocols including FIDO2, U2F, and OTP, offering phishing-resistant authentication that is more secure than SMS or app-based codes.
Yellowdog Updater Modified is a package manager for RPM-based Linux distributions like CentOS and RHEL. It has been largely replaced by DNF in newer Fedora and RHEL versions but remains widely used.

A JavaScript schema validation library commonly used with React form libraries like Formik. Yup provides a declarative API for defining validation rules with chainable methods and async validation support.

A color encoding system that separates image luminance from chrominance information. YUV is used in video compression and broadcasting because it allows chroma subsampling to reduce bandwidth with minimal perceptual quality loss.

A chroma subsampling scheme in digital video where color information is sampled at half the horizontal and half the vertical resolution of luminance, reducing bandwidth by 50% with minimal perceptual quality loss.

Z.definition: A linear-time string matching algorithm that computes an array where each element indicates the length of the longest substring starting from that position matching a prefix of the string. It is used in pattern matching.

A scalable low-latency garbage collector in the JVM designed for large heaps, performing concurrent compaction with pause times under one millisecond regardless of heap size by using colored pointers and load barriers.

A formal specification language based on set theory and predicate logic used to describe and model computing systems. Z notation helps verify system correctness before implementation in safety-critical applications.

A scaling strategy that splits data across multiple identical server instances based on an attribute like customer ID or region. Each instance handles a subset of the data, enabling horizontal data partitioning.

A depth-buffering technique in 3D computer graphics that stores the depth of each pixel to determine which objects are visible and which are hidden behind others. Z-buffering is fundamental to real-time 3D rendering.

A CSS property that controls the stacking order of overlapping positioned elements on a web page. Elements with higher z-index values appear in front of elements with lower values, essential for managing layers in complex UI layouts like modals and dropdowns.

A space-filling curve that maps multidimensional data to one dimension while preserving locality, used in database indexing and Delta Lake's Z-ordering to co-locate related data and accelerate range queries.

A multi-dimensional index structure that interleaves the bits of multiple column values into a single sort key, enabling efficient multi-column range scans in columnar storage systems like Delta Lake and Apache Iceberg.

A statistical measure that describes how many standard deviations a data point is from the mean of a distribution. Z-scores are used for anomaly detection, standardization, and comparing values across different scales.

A wireless communication protocol designed for home automation, operating on sub-GHz frequencies for better range and wall penetration than Wi-Fi. Z-Wave devices form a mesh network for smart home control.

An efficient SMT (Satisfiability Modulo Theories) solver developed by Microsoft Research used for program verification, constraint solving, and symbolic execution. Z3 checks the satisfiability of logical formulas over various theories.

An 8-bit microprocessor designed by Zilog that was widely used in early personal computers, game consoles, and embedded systems. The Z80 powered machines like the ZX Spectrum and TRS-80 and remains in embedded use.

An open-source enterprise monitoring solution for networks, servers, and cloud services. Zabbix provides real-time monitoring, alerting, visualization, and supports various data collection methods including agents, SNMP, and APIs.

OWASP ZAP (Zed Attack Proxy) is a free, open-source web application security scanner used for finding vulnerabilities during development and testing. It provides automated scanners and a set of tools for manual security testing.

A high-performance, structured logging library for Go applications developed by Uber. Zap provides blazing-fast, leveled logging with both a sugared (convenient) and a structured (zero-allocation) API.

A no-code automation platform that connects different web applications through automated workflows called Zaps. Zapier enables non-technical users to create integrations between thousands of apps without writing code.

A Python framework for deploying serverless web applications on AWS Lambda and API Gateway. Zappa allows deploying Django and Flask applications as serverless functions without modifying application code.

A storage format for chunked, compressed N-dimensional arrays designed for use in cloud and distributed computing environments. Zarr enables efficient access to large scientific datasets stored in cloud object stores.

A collection of 19 guiding principles for writing Python code, authored by Tim Peters and accessible via import this. The Zen of Python emphasizes readability, simplicity, and explicitness in software design.

A cloud-based customer service platform that provides tools for ticketing, live chat, knowledge bases, and customer relationship management. Zendesk helps organizations manage customer support interactions across multiple channels.
The programming interface for Zendesk customer service platform, enabling automation of ticket management, customer interactions, and support workflows through RESTful endpoints.
A command-line tool that creates graphical dialog boxes from shell scripts on Linux desktops. Zenity provides file selectors, message boxes, and input forms without requiring a full GUI toolkit.

The Bluetooth Low Energy subsystem in the Zephyr RTOS providing a complete host and controller implementation for resource-constrained IoT devices, supporting GATT profiles, mesh networking, and direction finding.

A scalable real-time operating system for resource-constrained devices, supported by the Linux Foundation. Zephyr supports multiple hardware architectures and provides a comprehensive set of IoT-focused features.
A test management solution for Jira that enables teams to create, organize, and execute test cases alongside development work. It supports BDD testing, test automation integration, and reporting.

A programming technique that avoids dynamic memory allocation during critical code paths to eliminate garbage collection pauses and allocation overhead. Zero-allocation code is common in high-performance Go and Rust programs.

A deployment strategy that ensures continuous service availability during application updates. Techniques like blue-green deployments, rolling updates, and canary releases enable zero downtime by maintaining active instances at all times.

A security framework that requires strict verification for every user and device attempting to access resources, regardless of their location inside or outside the network. Zero Trust follows the principle of 'never trust, always verify.'

A security framework that requires strict identity verification for every person and device trying to access resources, regardless of their network location. It follows the principle of never trust, always verify.

A security approach that applies zero trust principles specifically to data access, requiring continuous verification of identity and authorization for every data request regardless of network location.

A security solution that provides secure remote access to applications based on identity verification rather than network location. ZTNA replaces traditional VPNs with more granular, application-level access controls.

A budgeting method where every expense must be justified for each new period, starting from a zero base rather than adjusting the previous budget. In tech, it applies to cloud cost optimization and project funding decisions.

A cyberattack that requires no interaction from the victim to execute, exploiting vulnerabilities in software that automatically processes incoming data. Zero-click attacks target messaging apps, email clients, and media parsers.

Software or tools that work out of the box without requiring configuration files or setup. Zero-config tools like Parcel and Jest reduce onboarding friction and follow convention over configuration principles.

A technique where data is transferred between system components without CPU-mediated copying between memory buffers. Zero-copy operations use techniques like memory mapping and DMA to improve I/O performance significantly.

A software vulnerability that is unknown to the vendor and for which no patch exists, giving developers 'zero days' to fix it. Zero-day exploits are highly valued by attackers and cybersecurity researchers alike for their potential impact.

An attack that targets a previously unknown vulnerability in software before the vendor has issued a patch. Zero-day exploits are particularly dangerous because no defense exists at the time of discovery.

A database schema migration strategy that maintains full application availability by decomposing breaking changes into backward-compatible steps — such as expand-contract patterns — applied across multiple deployments.

An approach that eliminates the need for traditional extract, transform, and load pipelines by enabling direct querying or replication between data sources and analytics systems. AWS and other providers offer zero-ETL integrations.

The practice of initializing memory or data structures with zeros before use. Zero-filling prevents information leakage from previously used memory and ensures predictable initial state in buffers and allocated blocks.

A cryptographic method that allows one party to prove they know a value without revealing the value itself. Zero-knowledge proofs enable privacy-preserving authentication and transactions, playing a key role in blockchain and identity verification.

A technique in convolutional neural networks where zeros are added around the input data borders before applying convolution operations. Zero-padding preserves spatial dimensions and prevents information loss at the edges.

A machine learning paradigm where a model can recognize or classify objects or concepts it has never seen during training. It leverages semantic relationships and transfer learning to generalize to new categories.

A network security approach that creates fine-grained security zones around individual workloads within a data center, enforcing per-flow authentication and authorization based on zero-trust principles.

Zero-configuration networking — a set of technologies that automatically create usable IP networks without manual configuration or special servers. ZeroConf enables device discovery and address assignment on local networks.

A high-performance asynchronous messaging library that provides socket-like abstractions for building distributed and concurrent applications. ZeroMQ supports patterns like pub-sub, request-reply, and push-pull.

A data architecture pattern that unifies batch and real-time processing in a single framework, evolved from the Lambda architecture. Zeta architecture emphasizes a single technology stack to reduce complexity.
A unit of digital information storage equal to one sextillion bytes or 10^21 bytes. Global data creation surpassed several zettabytes per year in the early 2020s.

A personal knowledge management method using interconnected atomic notes, each containing a single idea with links to related notes. Digital tools like Obsidian and Roam Research have popularized this approach for developers and researchers.

A knowledge management system that organizes information into small interconnected notes with unique identifiers. Digital implementations like Obsidian and Roam Research use this method for personal knowledge bases.

A combined file system and volume manager originally developed by Sun Microsystems, known for data integrity features like checksumming, snapshots, and built-in RAID. ZFS provides enterprise-grade storage management.

A virtual storage volume in ZFS created by combining one or more physical disks or partitions. ZFS pools provide features like automatic data integrity verification, built-in RAID, and dynamic storage expansion.

A read-only point-in-time copy of a ZFS file system that captures the state of all files and directories at a specific moment. ZFS snapshots are created almost instantly and consume space only as data changes.

A systems programming language designed as a better alternative to C, featuring manual memory management without hidden control flow, comptime evaluation, and seamless C interop. Zig aims for robustness and optimal performance.

The declarative build system included with the Zig programming language that can also compile C and C++ code. It provides cross-compilation capabilities and serves as a drop-in replacement for complex build toolchains.

A feature of the Zig programming language that allows arbitrary code execution at compile time, enabling powerful metaprogramming without macros or separate code generation steps. Comptime replaces generics and compile-time evaluation.

A composable allocator interface in Zig that makes memory allocation strategies explicit and configurable. Unlike C's malloc, Zig allocators are passed as parameters, enabling custom allocation strategies per use case.

A low-power, low-data-rate wireless networking protocol designed for IoT and home automation devices. Zigbee creates mesh networks where devices can relay signals to extend range, connecting smart lights, sensors, locks, and other home devices.

Now known as the Connectivity Standards Alliance, this organization develops and maintains the Zigbee protocol and the Matter smart home standard. It promotes interoperability among IoT and smart home devices.
The central node in a Zigbee mesh network responsible for forming the network, assigning addresses, storing security keys, and bridging traffic between Zigbee devices and external networks.

An open-source bridge that allows Zigbee devices to communicate via MQTT without vendor-specific bridges. It enables integration of hundreds of Zigbee devices into home automation platforms like Home Assistant.

A technique that maps signed integers to unsigned integers so that numbers with small absolute values have small encoded values. Zigzag encoding is used in Protocol Buffers and other serialization formats for efficient variable-length encoding.

A lightweight full-text search engine written in Go, designed as a simpler alternative to Elasticsearch. Zinc provides search functionality with significantly lower resource requirements and easier deployment.

A file format and algorithm for lossless data compression that reduces file sizes for storage and transfer. Zip archives can contain multiple files and directories, and the format is universally supported across operating systems and programming languages.

A compressed file format that supports lossless data compression and can contain multiple files and directories. ZIP is universally supported across operating systems and is the basis for formats like JAR, DOCX, and APK.

A malicious archive file designed to crash or render useless the program or system that tries to decompress it by expanding to an enormous size. A small zip bomb can expand from kilobytes to petabytes of data.
The process of compressing one or more files into a smaller archive using the ZIP file format. Compression reduces storage requirements and transfer times while preserving the original data.

A widely used open-source data compression library that implements the DEFLATE algorithm. Zlib is the foundation for gzip compression and is embedded in countless applications, protocols, and file formats.

A file transfer protocol for serial connections that improved upon XMODEM and YMODEM with features like crash recovery, auto-start, and streaming transmission. ZMODEM was widely used in the dial-up bulletin board era.

A TypeScript-first schema declaration and validation library that provides runtime type checking with excellent developer experience. Zod schemas can infer TypeScript types, eliminating the need for duplicate type definitions.

A runtime type definition created using the Zod library that validates data structures at runtime while inferring TypeScript types at compile time. Zod schemas are commonly used for API input validation and form handling.
In testing terminology, a zombie test is one that runs but provides no useful assertions. In networking, a zombie refers to a compromised computer controlled remotely as part of a botnet.

A process that has completed execution but still has an entry in the process table because its parent has not yet read its exit status. Zombie processes consume minimal resources but can exhaust process table entries.

A high-availability strategy that distributes resources across multiple availability zones within a cloud region. Zonal redundancy protects applications from single-zone failures and improves reliability.

An isolated deployment area within a cloud region that provides redundancy and fault tolerance. Availability zones have independent power and networking, and distributing resources across zones improves application resilience.

A text file that contains the DNS records for a particular domain zone, mapping domain names to IP addresses and other resources. Zone files are the primary configuration mechanism for authoritative DNS servers.
The DNSSEC process of cryptographically signing all resource records in a DNS zone using zone-signing keys, enabling resolvers to verify the authenticity and integrity of DNS responses.

A DNS mechanism where a secondary server copies zone data from a primary DNS server. Unrestricted zone transfers are a security risk as they expose the complete DNS records of a domain to potential attackers.
A database or storage replication strategy that distributes replicas across different availability zones to ensure data durability and high availability even if an entire zone becomes unavailable.

A firewall model that groups network interfaces into security zones and applies policies based on traffic flowing between zones. It provides more intuitive and manageable security than traditional interface-based rules.

The practice of segmenting storage area networks into isolated groups to control which hosts can access which storage devices. SAN zoning improves security and prevents unintended data access between systems.

An open-source coordination service for distributed applications that maintains configuration information, naming, synchronization, and group services. Apache Zookeeper is used by Kafka, Hadoop, and other distributed systems for leader election and consensus.

A cluster of Apache ZooKeeper nodes that work together to provide reliable distributed coordination services. An ensemble requires a quorum (majority) of nodes to be available for the service to function.

An algorithm implemented using Apache ZooKeeper where distributed nodes coordinate to select a single leader through ephemeral sequential znodes. Leader election ensures exactly one process performs coordinating tasks in a cluster.

A compression algorithm developed by Google that produces output compatible with deflate, gzip, and zlib but achieves higher compression ratios at the cost of slower compression speed. Zopfli is ideal for static content.

A Linux kernel module that creates a compressed block device in RAM, commonly used as swap space. ZRAM improves performance on memory-constrained systems by compressing data rather than writing to slower disk swap.

Z Shell — a Unix shell that extends Bash with features like better autocompletion, spelling correction, plugin support, and theming. Zsh (with Oh My Zsh framework) is the default shell on macOS and a popular choice for developer productivity.

An extension for the Z shell that adds functionality like autocompletions, syntax highlighting, or custom commands. Plugin managers like Oh My Zsh and zinit simplify the installation and management of Zsh plugins.

A fast lossless compression algorithm developed by Facebook that provides high compression ratios comparable to zlib with speeds approaching LZ4. Zstandard is widely adopted in databases, file systems, and network protocols.

The container format for Zstd-compressed data defining magic numbers, frame headers, data blocks, and optional checksums, supporting both single-frame and multi-frame streaming compression with dictionary references.

Zstandard is a fast lossless compression algorithm developed by Facebook that provides high compression ratios with excellent speed. Zstd is used in databases, file systems, and network protocols as a modern alternative to gzip.

A pre-trained compression model used with Zstandard to improve compression ratios for small data samples that share common patterns. Dictionary compression is especially effective for compressing many similar small records.

A small, fast, and scalable state management library for React applications. Zustand provides a simple API based on hooks, avoiding the boilerplate of Redux while offering excellent performance and TypeScript support.

An API gateway service developed by Netflix that provides dynamic routing, monitoring, resiliency, and security for microservice architectures. Zuul handles request routing, load balancing, and authentication at the edge.

A special process in the Android operating system that is started at boot time and serves as the parent for all application processes. Zygote pre-loads common libraries and frameworks so new apps start faster via forking.

#.definition: A free, open-source developer platform created by Microsoft for building many types of applications including web, mobile, desktop, gaming, and IoT. It supports multiple languages including C#, F#, and Visual Basic.

A display device that presents visual information in three dimensions, creating the illusion of depth for the viewer. Technologies include stereoscopic displays, autostereoscopic screens, and holographic displays.
Computer graphics that use a three-dimensional representation of geometric data to perform calculations and render two-dimensional images. Used extensively in gaming, simulation, and visualization.

The fifth generation of mobile network technology offering significantly faster speeds, lower latency, and greater capacity than 4G. It enables new use cases like autonomous vehicles, remote surgery, and massive IoT deployments.
